Connor O'Brien: also need an audit trail for release gating. eng-releases needs to know when config changes vs runtime rollout, otherwise gates become meaningless.

Vanessa Ortiz: fleet side: if you allow targeting by hw_sku, we should use canonical SKUs from fleet service (A100-80G, H100-80G, L40S, etc). no freeform strings.

Quentin Harris: model naming: pls do not use display names. use registry canonical ids (`model_id`, `model_version_id`). we’ve had “llama-3-70b” mean 3 diff things.

Marissa Cole: product question: do we expect people to change thresholds frequently? if yes, we’ll need UI + approvals. if no, can we start GitOps and revisit.

Paula Mendes: +1. would love “safe defaults” + friction for paging thresholds changes. esp once it gates rollouts.

Logan Wright: ok, concrete design:
1) Control plane object: `PerfCanaryTargetSet`
- `name` (string)
- `enabled` (bool)
- `scope`: {regions[], tiers[], hw_skus[]}
- `targets[]`: each target is {model_id, model_version_id (optional), risk_tier (top|high-risk|other), params_ref}
2) Control plane object: `PerfCanaryThresholdProfile`
- `name`
- `baseline_ref` (points to baseline artifact + version)
- thresholds for metrics: p95_e2e_ms, p95_runtime_ms, tokens_per_sec, queue_ms, prefill_ms, decode_ms
- min_samples, evaluation_window, dampening

Noah Patel: + optional `pin_runtime_build`? or at least “valid_for_runtime_major”. otherwise baseline mismatch across runtime versions.

Nadia Rahman: i’d prefer baseline_ref include runtime build + kernel family baked into the artifact name. baseline generator can spit out: `baseline:model=<id>,runtime=<build>,sku=<sku>,region=<region>,ts=<...>`

Allison Grant: pls avoid “region required” everywhere. we said optionally per-region baselines. make region a dimension but not mandatory.

Caleb Johnson: whatever we do, canary needs to emit enough tags to join to baseline. but not too many.

Logan Wright: ok. schema should allow baseline_ref either:
- global (model+runtime+sku)
- regional override list (if set)

Connor O'Brien: re audit: we need write API to go through control plane w structured audit log. fields: actor, diff, ticket/ref, reason.

Marissa Cole: “reason” should be required if it affects paging or rollout gates.

Paula Mendes: and ideally gate: require approvals for “top model + paging thresholds” edits.

Logan Wright: we can do v1 as:
- all config changes require `change_request_id` (Linear/Jira)
- audit log records before/after JSON + requester
- optional `requires_approval` enforced by role if `risk_tier=top` and `severity=page` thresholds touched

Vanessa Ortiz: who has edit permissions? fleet doesn’t want random folks adding hw_sku scope that fans out and burns capacity.

Allison Grant: +1. need guardrails: max targets per set, max regions, max qps budget.

Noah Patel: can CP validate “capacity budget” too? e.g. config says 0.5% GPU cap, and canary enforces.

Logan Wright: CP can store desired budget + enforce fanout limits statically:
- `max_regions_per_set` (e.g., 3)
- `max_models_per_set` (e.g., 10)
- require explicit override flag for more
Canary service still enforces runtime QPS cap.

Caleb Johnson: also: do NOT store raw prompts anywhere in CP config. only “shape profile id” like `prompt_profile=v0.2`.

Nadia Rahman: on validation: pls validate that threshold profile references an existing baseline_ref, and that baseline_ref matches dims in target scope (sku/region).

Quentin Harris: also validate model_version_id exists in registry and is ACTIVE.

Connor O'Brien: and if model_version_id omitted, what’s the behavior? “latest”? that’s dangerous.

Noah Patel: agree. for top/high-risk we should require pin. for “other” models we can allow “follow default serving version” maybe.

Marissa Cole: from product side, i’d rather be strict: require pin always, at least for v1.

Logan Wright: ok, v1: require `model_version_id` always. we can add “follow channel” later.

Allison Grant: alerting policy ties into this. control plane should also store which threshold profile maps to warn vs page.

Logan Wright: yep. i’ll add: `alert_policy_ref` to target set, with severity mapping.

Paula Mendes: can we keep alert policy in SLO toolkit instead? i worry CP becomes a dumping ground.

Allison Grant: +1. but CP needs to know enough to annotate “this change impacts paging”. maybe store just the pointer.

Logan Wright: compromise:
- CP stores refs: `warning_rule_ref`, `paging_rule_ref`
- actual rule logic lives in observability-pack/SLO toolkit

Caleb Johnson: operationally: canary service will poll CP? or receive push?

Logan Wright: poll w etag every 60s to start. push later.

redwood-deploy-bot: (auto) FYI PR opened: redwood/pr-19203 "control plane config for canary target models" (reviewers: @Logan Wright @Nadia Rahman @Noah Patel)

Noah Patel: nice. can you ensure PR includes: default deny for unknown fields + server-side schema versioning.

Nadia Rahman: and migration story. we’ll have existing yaml configs in perf-canary repo.

Logan Wright: migration plan:
1) write one-time importer script: yaml -> CP objects (target set + threshold profile)
2) canary reads CP first, falls back to local yaml for 1 week w warning log
3) delete yaml path

Vanessa Ortiz: re audit log storage: can we pipe to our standard audit sink? (security asked for that for config affecting prod traffic)

Logan Wright: yup, CP already emits audit events -> audit-stream. will add event type `perf_canary_config_changed`.

Connor O'Brien: can you include `effective_time`? releases sometimes stage config ahead of enabling.

Logan Wright: good call. add `not_before` timestamp to target set + profile. canary ignores until then.

Allison Grant: also add `expires_at` for temporary dampening / incident mode.

Noah Patel: + “kill switch” per region.

Logan Wright: region kill switch is basically `enabled=false` scoped set. but we can add explicit `emergency_disable=true` that overrides.

Paula Mendes: ok, last question: who owns approvals / permissions?

Connor O'Brien: propose:
- runtime team can edit targets/thresholds
- eng-platform owns schema/validation
- eng-releases can edit enablement windows + freeze
- SRE can flip emergency disable

Allison Grant: + SRE can also change paging routing? (or is that separate)

Logan Wright: routing stays in alert rules (obs-pack). CP just references. SRE can change alert rules in obs-pack as usual.

Quentin Harris: pls add “config diff in audit event includes old/new model_version_id”. helps when folks forget they changed versions.

Logan Wright: will do.

Noah Patel: sounds good. i’ll review PR-19203 today. if we can land schema + audit this week, we can stop worrying about “mystery threshold edits”.

Marissa Cole: thanks all. once this is stable, we can talk about Console UI. for now, GitOps + audit is fine.

Logan Wright: cool. action items:
- Logan: update schema w pinned version req + not_before/exp + allowlisted dims
- Nadia: baseline_ref naming + validator checks
- Connor: permission matrix + change_request_id requirement wording
- Vanessa: provide canonical hw_sku list + validator source

Allison Grant: pls post once we have a sample audit log event + how to query it.

Logan Wright: will drop in #eng-platform when merged.

title: #eng-platform: 1768684400
preview:
Jordan Lee: heads up folks, PR for the `failover/simulate` endpoint is up (PR-19828). would love quick feedback from anyone planning to use this for rehearsal + incident analysis.

Miles Thornton: :eyes: can you drop an example request/response? trying to see if this is “game day friendly” or more of a dev-only thing.

Jordan Lee: yep. current shape:
```
POST /internal/failover/simulate
{
  "tenant_id": "t_..." | null,
  "route": "chat.completions",
  "primary_region": "us-east",
  "candidate_regions": ["eu-west","us-west"],
  "signals": {
    "reachability": {"us-east": 0.72, "eu-west": 0.99},
    "error_burn": {"us-east": 18.4, "eu-west": 0.8},
    "latency_burn": {"us-east": 6.2, "eu-west": 1.1},
    "capacity_headroom": {"us-east": 0.03, "eu-west": 0.21}
  },
  "policy_version": "2026-01-14.3",
  "mode": "dry_run" // or "explain"
}

200:
{
  "decision": "FAILOVER",
  "selected_region": "eu-west",
  "reason": "reachability<0.9 corroborated by 5xx_burn>14",
  "evidence": ["reachability", "error_burn"],
  "hold_down": "300s",
  "anti_flap": {"allowed": true, "remaining_flips": 2},
  "explain": "..."
}
```
`signals` is basically the normalized values the controller would see after aggregation.

Mina Haddad: first thought: in rehearsal we don’t want to hand craft signals json. can this take a time range and pull from region-health snapshots?

Allison Grant: +1. ideally `simulate?from=...&to=...` and we can replay what the aggregator saw.

Jordan Lee: totally fair. current PR is “bring your own signals” so we can test determinism. next step could be `simulate_from_audit_event` or `simulate_from_snapshot_id`.

Sergio Costa: if it’s BYO signals, we need a helper in the runbook / CLI. on-call won’t paste this during an incident.

Tomáš Novák: +1. on-call muscle memory: “give me the command that prints recommended action + why.” not “construct json by reading 4 dashboards.”

Ravi Menon: for incident analysis, BYO signals is fine if we can export signals from metrics (promql) in a standard format. maybe endpoint returns a “promql queries used” block?

Allison Grant: careful: we don’t want prometheus coupling in the policy service response. but we *do* want “evidence” to link to dashboards.

Jordan Lee: what about `links` in response? like `links: [dashboard_url, logs_url]` based on route+region.

Miles Thornton: yes please. also the response should show the *full evaluation path* (ordered signals considered, thresholds, dampening). “FAILOVER bc reachability” is not enough for tuning.

Jordan Lee: in `mode=explain` we dump the evaluation steps. example:
```
explain:
1) reachability(us-east)=0.72 < 0.90 => trigger
   corroboration: error_burn(us-east)=18.4 > 14.0 => OK
2) capacity_headroom(us-east)=0.03 < 0.05 => would trigger, but lower priority
3) anti_flap: flips_last_hour=0 < 3 => OK
4) action: failover to eu-west (best reachability w/ headroom)
```
right now it’s a string blob. could also emit as structured events but we were trying to keep schema small.

Benji Okafor: for safety review, I’d rather it be structured (even if flattened). string explain is hard to diff in tests.

Ethan Park: +1. also, if this becomes part of the controller CLI later, structured lets us format nicely + redact tenant.

Victor Alvarez: question from network side: can we simulate reachability based on the perf canary probes? i.e. take probe id / snapshot rather than a single scalar?

Jordan Lee: not yet. currently reachability is just the “region_health reachability score” (0..1). if you want probe-level breakdown, that probably lives in region-health service and we pass the rollup here.

Victor Alvarez: ok. but for incident analysis, I want `reachability_breakdown: dns, tcp, tls, http` or at least “source=perf-canary vs source=client telemetry”.

Allison Grant: +1, source matters. we’ve had cases where probe region differs from affected client geo.

Jordan Lee: makes sense. maybe add `signal_sources` summary to explain:
- reachability: perf-canary
- error_burn: api-gw
- latency_burn: serving-runtime
- capacity: fleet

Miles Thornton: another usability point: a “confidence” score would help when the signals are borderline. on-call needs to know if controller would actually flip or just recommend.

Jordan Lee: today it’s deterministic (same inputs => same decision), so “confidence” is basically “how far past threshold.” could output `margin` per signal.

Sergio Costa: yes. show `reachability=0.89 (threshold=0.90, margin=-0.01)` etc.

Tomáš Novák: can we also include what would block execution in prod? e.g. “needs approval hook”, “per-tenant flip limit hit”, “hold-down active”.

Jordan Lee: good call. we have `anti_flap` but not “approval required.” I can add:
- `execution_blocked_reason` (string)
- `would_page` (true/false)

Devon Price: from incident-bot side: if we can call simulate with an incident id, we can auto-post “policy would have failed over at T+X” during retro. even if we don’t do it now, pls keep response stable.

Ethan Park: +1. stable response keys or versioned schema. also `policy_version` should be optional but if omitted, it should default to “current deployed config in prod” vs “whatever the caller thinks.”

Jordan Lee: agreed. right now it defaults to “latest active in environment” if omitted.

Ravi Menon: edge case: partial impairment. does simulate support multi-route? e.g. embeddings ok but chat 5xx burn.

Jordan Lee: request is per route today. we can do a batch version:
`POST /internal/failover/simulate:batch` with list of routes. but that’s not in PR.

Miles Thornton: even without batch, can you make `route` optional and default to “all critical routes”? for game day we’d sanity check “chat+embeddings+rerank” together.

Jordan Lee: I’d rather not hide complexity. but we can put a wrapper in the controller CLI to run N routes and print a table.

Allison Grant: what about data residency constraints? for enterprise, simulation should show “candidate region rejected due to residency policy.”

Benji Okafor: +1. explain needs to include “policy constraint eliminated eu-west” etc. otherwise we’ll keep rediscovering this.

Jordan Lee: good catch. current response doesn’t show eliminated candidates. I’ll add:
- `rejected_regions` with reason strings
- `selected_region_rationale`

Victor Alvarez: logging: please ensure we don’t log full request body when tenant_id is present (or we need redaction). endpoint is internal but still.

Jordan Lee: yep, request logging is currently `route, primary_region, decision, selected_region, policy_version` only. no raw signals.

Sergio Costa: last ask: return a “replay payload” we can paste into a runbook comment, like a minimal canonical json (ordered keys) so two people don’t format differently.

Jordan Lee: I can add `canonical_request` field as a stringified minified json (with stable key ordering). would that help?

Tomáš Novák: yes, for incident notes. also makes it easier to attach to postmortem.

Miles Thornton: overall this is great. action items i’m hearing:
1) explain should be structured OR at least margins + rejected candidates
2) add links + sources
3) CLI wrapper to avoid hand crafting signals
4) longer-term: simulate from snapshots/audit events

Jordan Lee: :thumbsup: I’ll update the PR with: margins, rejected_regions, signal_sources summary, and an optional `links` array. CLI/snapshot-based replay I’ll open follow-up issues.

Devon Price: once you have `links`, loop me in. we can wire incident-bot to include the same dashboard URLs in the summary.

Allison Grant: pls also confirm label cardinality if you add anything that looks like region/route/tenant. we got burned last week.

Jordan Lee: yep. no per-tenant labels; this is an endpoint response only. metrics stay aggregated.

Miles Thornton: cool. tagging this thread in the game day checklist doc so we don’t forget. :bookmark:

title: #eng-platform: 1768660200
preview:
Benji Okafor: hey all — trying to lock feature-flag strategy for failover controller rollout (per tier/tenant). what’s the cleanest plan that gives us (1) staged enablement and (2) hard guardrails tied to metrics?

Mina Haddad: +1, we need something routing can reason about deterministically. “enabled for prod” isn’t enough; we want to pin by tenant/tier and be able to yank quickly.

Ravi Menon: agree. also reminder: the controller touches policy flips, so any rollout needs anti-flap + rate limiting (ADR-043). if we do per-tenant, we have to avoid config drift / “mystery allowlist”.

Jordan Lee: from policy-engine side, I’d prefer a small set of flags with explicit precedence:
1) global kill switch
2) environment gate (staging/prod)
3) execution mode (off | dry_run | execute)
4) scope (tier allowlist + tenant allowlist)
we can implement as config in control plane + cached.

Miles Thornton: +1 to “off/dry_run/execute”. dry-run should be default in prod for 1-2 weeks so we can validate recommendation quality vs actual incidents.

Ethan Park: question: where do we want the flags to live? helm values for controller + control-plane config for per-tenant scopes? i don’t want to redeploy controller to change tenant allowlist during an event.

Benji Okafor: yeah ideally no redeploy. can we do:
- controller deployment flag: `controller_enabled=true` (allows it to run)
- control plane config flags: `failover_mode` + `scope`
so we can change scope without bumping the deployment.

Allison Grant: guardrails tied to metrics: i want a simple “stop conditions” list we can encode and also monitor:
- multi_region_availability burn rate > X
- policy_flips_total rate > Y (indicates oscillation)
- failover_action_errors_total > 0 (controller failing)
- region_health_missing_signal > threshold (we’ve seen missing stream -> NPE before)
when any trip, auto-set mode to dry_run or off.

Sergio Costa: + dashboards can annotate when the kill switch trips. last rehearsal was super confusing because we couldn’t see when routing changed vs when alerts fired.

Aisha Bello: +1, but careful: don’t bake tenant_id as a label into every alert. we already did the cardinality review and it was borderline.

Vanessa Ortiz: for Dedicated: per-tenant isn’t sufficient; we also need per-pool gating. a single “tenant enable” could still drain a pool if they have multiple clusters. scope should include `dedicated_pool_id` allowlist.

Mina Haddad: maybe scope is hierarchical:
- Hosted: tier + tenant allowlist
- Dedicated: tier + tenant allowlist + pool allowlist
and routing evaluates: if controller says failover but pool not in scope, ignore.

Jordan Lee: that’s doable but we need to keep semantics clean. suggest:
`scope_selector` supports:
- `tier in {internal, sandbox, paid, enterprise}`
- `tenant_id in {...}`
- `dedicated_pool_id in {...}`
all optional; empty means “no scope” unless tier is set.

Benji Okafor: i’m worried empty selector accidentally means “all tenants” depending on parsing.

Jordan Lee: fair. we can make it explicit: selector must be present + must include at least one constraint. otherwise controller refuses to execute and logs `scope_invalid`.

Keiko Yamamoto: also add a concurrency/blast-radius limiter independent of scope:
- max N tenants affected per hour
- max N% traffic shifted per region per 5 min
- hold-down timer before new action in same route/region
if exceeded, auto-downgrade to dry_run.

Miles Thornton: yes. “blast radius constraints” should be orthogonal. scope says *who is eligible*, limiter says *how much we’re allowed to touch*.

Ethan Park: implementation thought:
- controller reads config from control plane every 30s (with jitter)
- decision engine enforces: `mode == execute` AND `scope_valid` AND `approval_state` (if required)
- writes audit event on every evaluate even if no-op

Devon Price: pls emit a single structured audit event when mode/scope changes too (who changed it, reason). incident-bot can post a summary in #incidents when prod mode flips to execute.

Sergio Costa: +1. also if we auto-kill switch, make sure audit says `trigger=auto_guardrail` and includes which metric threshold.

Allison Grant: re: metrics thresholds, we can keep it simple for v1:
- If `policy_flips_total{env=prod}` > 3 in 15m -> force dry_run
- If `failover_controller_errors_total` > 0 in 5m -> force off
- If `multi_region_availability_burn_rate_5m` > 14x AND controller recently executed -> force dry_run (prevents feedback loop)
we can tune later.

Aisha Bello: + can we key the burn rate logic to “regional event” signals only? otherwise a single model rollout causing 5xx could trip the kill switch and block real failover.

Miles Thornton: the whole point is to avoid failing over on local regressions. but for kill switch, yeah, we should avoid yanking it because of unrelated partial. maybe require corroboration: reachability/probe failures OR region_health says “impaired”.

Ravi Menon: exactly. also guardrails should look at controller-specific outcomes:
- did executing actually reduce 5xx burn / improve reachability?
if not, rollback and freeze.

Benji Okafor: ok so proposal:
Flags/config:
1) `failover_controller.global_enabled` (deployment)
2) `failover_controller.mode` = off|dry_run|execute
3) `failover_controller.scope_selector` (tier/tenant/pool allowlists, must be non-empty)
4) `failover_controller.require_approval` (prod default true initially)
5) `failover_controller.guardrails` (flip rate limits, blast radius, corroboration requirements)

Rollout plan:
- staging: execute for all test tenants
- prod week 1: dry_run for paid+enterprise (no free tier), log + audit only
- prod week 2: execute for internal tenants + 1-2 friendly paid tenants (explicit allowlist)
- expand by tier after game day success criteria (SLO burn, flip stability)

Metrics gates to expand:
- false positive rate < X (recommendations when region healthy)
- avg time-to-detect < Y
- no oscillation (flips/hour below threshold)

does this line up?

Mina Haddad: looks good. one more: we should include “route/model scoping” eventually. we don’t want to enable for embeddings if we only tested chat.

Jordan Lee: we can add later as selector predicate `route_group in {chat, embeddings, rerank}`. but let’s not block v1.

Vanessa Ortiz: for Dedicated, please ensure we can start with a single pool in us-east only. eu-west headroom is tight after failover.

Keiko Yamamoto: +1. also can we require `capacity_headroom > threshold` in destination region as a precondition for execute?

Ravi Menon: already in the signal hierarchy work, but yes: controller should refuse execute if capacity predicate fails and instead recommend “shed + partial failover” (or just no-op with reason).

Allison Grant: pls make sure we emit `failover_recommendation_total{reason=capacity_blocked}` type counters so we can see when capacity is the blocker.

Aisha Bello: and aggregate those reasons (limited enum). do not include raw signal names or tenant ids in labels.

Devon Price: for incident-bot: if mode flips to execute in prod, i want a message like:
“Failover controller set to EXECUTE for scope: tier=paid, tenants=[…], pools=[…]. Approval required=true. Dashboard: <link>”
(and on auto-kill: “AUTO: forced DRY_RUN due to guardrail trip: policy_flips_rate”).

Talia Benson: can someone drop this into the rollout plan doc + tag owners? i’ll track as action item in ENG-2411.

Benji Okafor: yup. action items:
- Jordan: confirm selector semantics + validation errors
- Ethan: where config lives + refresh behavior
- Allison/Sergio: draft guardrail thresholds + alert/annotation plan
- Vanessa/Keiko: Dedicated pool scoping requirements
I’ll write up the proposal and link in ENG-2411 + ADR-043 notes.

Miles Thornton: :thumbsup: also let’s be explicit: prod default stays `dry_run` until after game day + postmortem review. no surprise flips.

Mina Haddad: +1, and please add “single click rollback”: set mode=off globally. no approvals required for kill switch.

Benji Okafor: agreed. kill switch bypasses approval; we’ll log who did it and why.

Sergio Costa: cool. once we have the config keys, i’ll wire dashboard annotations off the audit stream.

Aisha Bello: and i’ll update SLO dashboard to show “controller mode” as a top banner, so oncall isn’t guessing.

Ethan Park: i’ll sanity-check caching/TTL so we don’t stampede control plane during an incident. 30s + jitter seems fine.

Benji Okafor: thanks all. i’ll post summary + proposed keys in-thread later today.
text:
Benji Okafor: hey all — trying to lock feature-flag strategy for failover controller rollout (per tier/tenant). what’s the cleanest plan that gives us (1) staged enablement and (2) hard guardrails tied to metrics?

Mina Haddad: +1, we need something routing can reason about deterministically. “enabled for prod” isn’t enough; we want to pin by tenant/tier and be able to yank quickly.

Ravi Menon: agree. also reminder: the controller touches policy flips, so any rollout needs anti-flap + rate limiting (ADR-043). if we do per-tenant, we have to avoid config drift / “mystery allowlist”.

Jordan Lee: from policy-engine side, I’d prefer a small set of flags with explicit precedence:
1) global kill switch
2) environment gate (staging/prod)
3) execution mode (off | dry_run | execute)
4) scope (tier allowlist + tenant allowlist)
we can implement as config in control plane + cached.

Miles Thornton: +1 to “off/dry_run/execute”. dry-run should be default in prod for 1-2 weeks so we can validate recommendation quality vs actual incidents.

Ethan Park: question: where do we want the flags to live? helm values for controller + control-plane config for per-tenant scopes? i don’t want to redeploy controller to change tenant allowlist during an event.

Benji Okafor: yeah ideally no redeploy. can we do:
- controller deployment flag: `controller_enabled=true` (allows it to run)
- control plane config flags: `failover_mode` + `scope`
so we can change scope without bumping the deployment.

Allison Grant: guardrails tied to metrics: i want a simple “stop conditions” list we can encode and also monitor:
- multi_region_availability burn rate > X
- policy_flips_total rate > Y (indicates oscillation)
- failover_action_errors_total > 0 (controller failing)
- region_health_missing_signal > threshold (we’ve seen missing stream -> NPE before)
when any trip, auto-set mode to dry_run or off.

Sergio Costa: + dashboards can annotate when the kill switch trips. last rehearsal was super confusing because we couldn’t see when routing changed vs when alerts fired.

Aisha Bello: +1, but careful: don’t bake tenant_id as a label into every alert. we already did the cardinality review and it was borderline.

Vanessa Ortiz: for Dedicated: per-tenant isn’t sufficient; we also need per-pool gating. a single “tenant enable” could still drain a pool if they have multiple clusters. scope should include `dedicated_pool_id` allowlist.

Mina Haddad: maybe scope is hierarchical:
- Hosted: tier + tenant allowlist
- Dedicated: tier + tenant allowlist + pool allowlist
and routing evaluates: if controller says failover but pool not in scope, ignore.

Jordan Lee: that’s doable but we need to keep semantics clean. suggest:
`scope_selector` supports:
- `tier in {internal, sandbox, paid, enterprise}`
- `tenant_id in {...}`
- `dedicated_pool_id in {...}`
all optional; empty means “no scope” unless tier is set.

Benji Okafor: i’m worried empty selector accidentally means “all tenants” depending on parsing.

Jordan Lee: fair. we can make it explicit: selector must be present + must include at least one constraint. otherwise controller refuses to execute and logs `scope_invalid`.

Keiko Yamamoto: also add a concurrency/blast-radius limiter independent of scope:
- max N tenants affected per hour
- max N% traffic shifted per region per 5 min
- hold-down timer before new action in same route/region
if exceeded, auto-downgrade to dry_run.

Miles Thornton: yes. “blast radius constraints” should be orthogonal. scope says *who is eligible*, limiter says *how much we’re allowed to touch*.

Ethan Park: implementation thought:
- controller reads config from control plane every 30s (with jitter)
- decision engine enforces: `mode == execute` AND `scope_valid` AND `approval_state` (if required)
- writes audit event on every evaluate even if no-op

Devon Price: pls emit a single structured audit event when mode/scope changes too (who changed it, reason). incident-bot can post a summary in #incidents when prod mode flips to execute.

Sergio Costa: +1. also if we auto-kill switch, make sure audit says `trigger=auto_guardrail` and includes which metric threshold.

Allison Grant: re: metrics thresholds, we can keep it simple for v1:
- If `policy_flips_total{env=prod}` > 3 in 15m -> force dry_run
- If `failover_controller_errors_total` > 0 in 5m -> force off
- If `multi_region_availability_burn_rate_5m` > 14x AND controller recently executed -> force dry_run (prevents feedback loop)
we can tune later.

Aisha Bello: + can we key the burn rate logic to “regional event” signals only? otherwise a single model rollout causing 5xx could trip the kill switch and block real failover.

Miles Thornton: the whole point is to avoid failing over on local regressions. but for kill switch, yeah, we should avoid yanking it because of unrelated partial. maybe require corroboration: reachability/probe failures OR region_health says “impaired”.

Ravi Menon: exactly. also guardrails should look at controller-specific outcomes:
- did executing actually reduce 5xx burn / improve reachability?
if not, rollback and freeze.

Benji Okafor: ok so proposal:
Flags/config:
1) `failover_controller.global_enabled` (deployment)
2) `failover_controller.mode` = off|dry_run|execute
3) `failover_controller.scope_selector` (tier/tenant/pool allowlists, must be non-empty)
4) `failover_controller.require_approval` (prod default true initially)
5) `failover_controller.guardrails` (flip rate limits, blast radius, corroboration requirements)

Rollout plan:
- staging: execute for all test tenants
- prod week 1: dry_run for paid+enterprise (no free tier), log + audit only
- prod week 2: execute for internal tenants + 1-2 friendly paid tenants (explicit allowlist)
- expand by tier after game day success criteria (SLO burn, flip stability)

Metrics gates to expand:
- false positive rate < X (recommendations when region healthy)
- avg time-to-detect < Y
- no oscillation (flips/hour below threshold)

does this line up?

Mina Haddad: looks good. one more: we should include “route/model scoping” eventually. we don’t want to enable for embeddings if we only tested chat.

Jordan Lee: we can add later as selector predicate `route_group in {chat, embeddings, rerank}`. but let’s not block v1.

Vanessa Ortiz: for Dedicated, please ensure we can start with a single pool in us-east only. eu-west headroom is tight after failover.

Keiko Yamamoto: +1. also can we require `capacity_headroom > threshold` in destination region as a precondition for execute?

Ravi Menon: already in the signal hierarchy work, but yes: controller should refuse execute if capacity predicate fails and instead recommend “shed + partial failover” (or just no-op with reason).

Allison Grant: pls make sure we emit `failover_recommendation_total{reason=capacity_blocked}` type counters so we can see when capacity is the blocker.

Aisha Bello: and aggregate those reasons (limited enum). do not include raw signal names or tenant ids in labels.

Devon Price: for incident-bot: if mode flips to execute in prod, i want a message like:
“Failover controller set to EXECUTE for scope: tier=paid, tenants=[…], pools=[…]. Approval required=true. Dashboard: <link>”
(and on auto-kill: “AUTO: forced DRY_RUN due to guardrail trip: policy_flips_rate”).

Talia Benson: can someone drop this into the rollout plan doc + tag owners? i’ll track as action item in ENG-2411.

Benji Okafor: yup. action items:
- Jordan: confirm selector semantics + validation errors
- Ethan: where config lives + refresh behavior
- Allison/Sergio: draft guardrail thresholds + alert/annotation plan
- Vanessa/Keiko: Dedicated pool scoping requirements
I’ll write up the proposal and link in ENG-2411 + ADR-043 notes.

Miles Thornton: :thumbsup: also let’s be explicit: prod default stays `dry_run` until after game day + postmortem review. no surprise flips.

Mina Haddad: +1, and please add “single click rollback”: set mode=off globally. no approvals required for kill switch.

Benji Okafor: agreed. kill switch bypasses approval; we’ll log who did it and why.

Sergio Costa: cool. once we have the config keys, i’ll wire dashboard annotations off the audit stream.

Aisha Bello: and i’ll update SLO dashboard to show “controller mode” as a top banner, so oncall isn’t guessing.

Ethan Park: i’ll sanity-check caching/TTL so we don’t stampede control plane during an incident. 30s + jitter seems fine.

Benji Okafor: thanks all. i’ll post summary + proposed keys in-thread later today.

title: #eng-platform: 1768616900
preview:
Miles Thornton: heads up: controller just auto-rolled back the us-east→eu-west flip. looks like it tripped the hold-down timer condition, not an operator action. anyone else seeing this?

incident-bot: FailoverController event
`action=ROLLBACK`
`scope=hosted-api/chat`
`from=eu-west`
`to=us-east`
`reason=hold_down_expired_without_sustained_healthy`
`evidence=reachability_ok,error_burn_ok,tail_latency_burn=warning`
`policy_flip_id=fo-2026-01-15-019`
Dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.example/d/failover/overview?var=policy_flip_id=fo-2026-01-15-019

Ravi Menon: yep saw the audit event. the “sustained healthy” predicate never went true because p99 stayed elevated in eu-west for ~7m, even though 5xx stopped.

Allison Grant: confirming from dashboards: error burn dropped back under 2% almost immediately post-flip, but tail latency burn for `chat.completions` stayed > threshold. it oscillated around the line (like 880ms-1.1s) so the controller never got its green window.

Mina Haddad: question: did we intend tail latency to block rollback *out of* the failover state? i thought rollback should require primary is healthy again + destination not on fire, but not strictly “destination is perfect”.

Benji Okafor: current logic is kinda strict:
- after failover, we enter HOLD_DOWN for N minutes
- at expiry, if `health_state != HEALTHY` => rollback eligible
- `health_state` is computed as worst-of signals w/ no hysteresis
so a single “warning” keeps it not healthy.

Miles Thornton: right, which is probably why it rolled back even though eu-west was fine-ish and we *wanted* to stay there until us-east is actually stable. this feels too twitchy.

Jordan Lee: for context: that’s straight from the ADR-043 draft semantics (“avoid long-lived failovers when destination is degraded”), but we didn’t implement the “warning != unhealthy” nuance. we basically mapped warning->not healthy.

Rafael Mendes: +1 that this looks like too aggressive rollback. in a real incident, bouncing back and forth is the worst case. we need hysteresis + a longer “stable” requirement before rollback.

Ravi Menon: also note: the hold-down timer is only 10m in this config pack (staging preset got copied?) i expected 20-30m for prod tests.

Benji Okafor: yeah config for the game day route set:
```
hold_down: 600s
healthy_window: 180s
rollback_on: ["hold_down_expired", "destination_unhealthy"]
```
so it basically asked for 3m of perfect health right after expiry.

Allison Grant: and “healthy_window=180s” is evaluated on 30s buckets, so it needs 6 consecutive buckets clean. if p99 crosses threshold once, counter resets.

Mina Haddad: suggestion: treat tail latency as corroboration signal, not a hard gate. e.g.
- rollback only if reachability+5xx indicate destination is actually impaired
- tail latency should maybe raise confidence or add dampening, but not force rollback alone

Peter Holtz: adding: gateway retries/backoff could be contributing to the p99 spikes after flip (clients reconnecting / retry storms). so using tail latency as “destination unhealthy” might just reflect post-failover churn.

Miles Thornton: makes sense. so action items:
1) bump hold_down for prod tests (at least for hosted routes)
2) add hysteresis for latency (two thresholds: warn vs critical)
3) change “warning” state to not block “stay in failover” (or not trigger rollback)

Jordan Lee: we can implement state machine changes quickly:
- introduce `DEGRADED_OK` as a terminal state for destination after failover
- only rollback if `UNHEALTHY_CRITICAL` OR `capacity_exhaustion` OR `reachability_fail`
- tail latency stays as `DEGRADED` and only blocks *new* failovers, not rollback

Ravi Menon: careful: we still need “don’t get stuck on a degraded destination forever.” maybe add max_failover_duration (like 2h) and require human ack if still degraded.

Rafael Mendes: +1. on-call should get paged/alerted if we’re in failover > X mins with DEGRADED status, not auto-rollback.

Allison Grant: metrics-wise: we should separate “rollback_reason=hold_down” vs “rollback_reason=destination_unhealthy” because right now both look the same in the panel. it’s confusing.

Devon Price: also: incident-bot summary line currently says “rolled back due to hold-down expired without sustained healthy” which reads like the system is confident it should rollback. can tweak to include severity + which signal blocked (tail latency).

Benji Okafor: tuning proposal for next run:
- hold_down: 1800s
- latency thresholds: warn at 1.2s p99, critical at 2.0s
- healthy_window: 5m for rollback back to primary, but only evaluated against *primary* health, not destination
- destination guard: only rollback if destination hits critical OR reachability fails

Mina Haddad: wait, shouldn’t rollback require *primary* is healthy too? otherwise we bounce back into broken us-east.

Ravi Menon: yep. rollback should require corroborated “primary recovered” + “we can safely move traffic back.”

Miles Thornton: agree. what happened here was rollback fired basically just because eu-west wasn’t pristine, no strong signal us-east recovered.

Jordan Lee: i’ll open a patch on the controller config + logic:
- rollback gate: `primary_health == HEALTHY_STABLE(5m)` AND (`dest_health != UNHEALTHY_CRITICAL`)
- auto-rollback disabled if primary unknown / signal missing

Allison Grant: please also add dashboard annotation when controller enters HOLD_DOWN and when the healthy_window counter resets. it’ll help tune this.

Peter Holtz: and we should watch for client retry-after behavior on the rollback. might be causing another wave of reconnect p99 spikes.

Rafael Mendes: for today: can we pin the policy to stay on eu-west for the remainder of the test? i don’t want another flip.

Benji Okafor: we can set `automation_mode=approval_required` + increase rate-limit immediately. i’ll do that now.

Miles Thornton: do it. and let’s capture this as a concrete finding for the retro: “hold-down + healthy_window too strict => premature rollback.”

Devon Price: logged in the game day notes + linked this thread. also adding a follow-up ticket for bot wording + signal detail.

Allison Grant: dropping a screenshot in #eng-sre too. the p99 spikes line up exactly w/ the hold_down expiry. good evidence this is timer-driven not real impairment.

Ravi Menon: cool. after the game day, we should run the simulation endpoint with recorded metrics to replay this and test new hysteresis without prod risk.

Miles Thornton: +1. ok, thanks all. keep an eye out if it tries to rollback again; we’ll treat it as a bug, not a valid decision.

title: #eng-platform: 1768603310
preview:
Jada Williams: quick gut check on feature flag cohort assignment for lifecycle growth experiments (email + console). we need deterministic cohorts + a clean holdout group. what’s the approach we’re going with?

Ethan Park: proposal: assign at **workspace/org** level (not user) using a stable hash on `org_id` + `experiment_key` + a salt.
- output bucket 0-99
- bucket ranges map to variants (e.g., 0-44 = treatment_a, 45-89 = treatment_b, 90-99 = holdout)
- stickiness guaranteed bc hash is deterministic

Benji Okafor: +1 org-level. if we do user-level we’ll end up with mixed experiences inside the same team (esp console nudges). also makes support “why did my teammate see X” harder.

Logan Wright: from analytics side: please make sure we emit a single canonical property for every relevant event (signup, api_key_created, first_request, 100k_tokens) like:
- `exp_lifecycle_growth_v1` = `holdout|email_a|email_b|nudge_a|nudge_b` (whatever we decide)
- AND `exp_assignment_unit` = `org`
otherwise we’ll have a fun time joining flags later :grimacing:

Paula Mendes: also need to confirm: holdout == no lifecycle touches at all? (no new email drip + no new nudge surfaces) or just “no treatment variant but still baseline onboarding”? if baseline changes, holdout isn’t a holdout.

Jada Williams: yeah, this is the key question. we said “holdout mechanism” in the plan but need to be explicit. my assumption: holdout = current status quo onboarding (existing emails + no new console nudges).

Ethan Park: agree. implementation detail: treat holdout as `variant=control` that bypasses *new* sends + new nudge flag checks. (status quo paths still run as-is)

Dylan Brooks: what about users/orgs created before we turn this on? do we backfill assignment or only for new signups?

Ethan Park: for this sprint, I’d scope to: assignment created at first qualifying event we control (signup event in app) and persisted. for pre-existing orgs, we can assign on first seen post-launch (same hash, so it’ll be stable either way). no need to run a backfill job unless analytics wants it.

Logan Wright: stable hash is fine. as long as when an org shows up, we persist the assigned variant somewhere so we don’t get weirdness if IDs change formats. (but if hash is only org_id it should be stable; persistence is more for auditing/debug)

Benji Okafor: where do we persist? `experiments_assignments` table in console db? or in the flag service?

Ethan Park: simplest: flag service does deterministic calculation but ALSO writes `experiment_key`, `org_id`, `variant`, `assigned_at` to a small table on first evaluation. that gives us:
- consistent behavior across email triggers + console
- easy debugging (“why is org in holdout?”)
- analytics can join if needed

Paula Mendes: re: “email triggers + console” — please ensure **the same assignment function** is called by both pipelines. we can’t have email side using user_id while console uses org_id.

Jada Williams: yep. also need a way to force an org into a variant for QA / dogfooding.

Ethan Park: override path:
- allowlist table keyed by `org_id` (or domain) with explicit variant
- used in staging by default, production only for Redwood internal orgs + QA orgs
- precedence: override > persisted assignment > computed hash

Dylan Brooks: edge case: anonymous site visitors for retargeting? (no org_id yet)

Ethan Park: for this thread: cohort assignment is for product/email experiments post-signup. retargeting audiences are separate (Tyler/Ben owning). we can ignore anonymous assignment here.

Logan Wright: one more ask: include `exp_holdout=true/false` as a derived boolean too. makes dashboards easier.

Benji Okafor: % split: do we want 10% holdout? 5%? and do we need separate holdout per experiment or one global holdout for the whole sprint?

Jada Williams: I’d prefer one global holdout for the sprint so we can measure net impact without “control contamination”. like `lifecycle_growth_sprint_q1_2026` assignment, then sub-variants for email/nudge.

Ethan Park: we can do hierarchical:
- global experiment key determines {holdout, eligible}
- if eligible, then separate keys for email + nudge variants (or we gate them on the global cohort)
that keeps holdout clean while still letting marketing iterate inside eligible.

Paula Mendes: +1 hierarchical. requirement: events must include both keys (global + specific) or at least the global.

Dylan Brooks: sounds good. can we document this decision in the ENG ticket so we don’t re-litigate in review?

Ethan Park: yep, I’ll update ENG-10501 + PR notes in pr-28477. decision summary:
1) assignment unit = org/workspace
2) deterministic hash + persisted assignment
3) global holdout = status quo onboarding (no new drip + no new nudges)
4) overrides allowed for internal/QA

Jada Williams: perfect. :thumbsup: once the PR is up I’ll review specifically for “same assignment path for email + console” + property names.
text:
Jada Williams: quick gut check on feature flag cohort assignment for lifecycle growth experiments (email + console). we need deterministic cohorts + a clean holdout group. what’s the approach we’re going with?

Ethan Park: proposal: assign at **workspace/org** level (not user) using a stable hash on `org_id` + `experiment_key` + a salt.
- output bucket 0-99
- bucket ranges map to variants (e.g., 0-44 = treatment_a, 45-89 = treatment_b, 90-99 = holdout)
- stickiness guaranteed bc hash is deterministic

Benji Okafor: +1 org-level. if we do user-level we’ll end up with mixed experiences inside the same team (esp console nudges). also makes support “why did my teammate see X” harder.

Logan Wright: from analytics side: please make sure we emit a single canonical property for every relevant event (signup, api_key_created, first_request, 100k_tokens) like:
- `exp_lifecycle_growth_v1` = `holdout|email_a|email_b|nudge_a|nudge_b` (whatever we decide)
- AND `exp_assignment_unit` = `org`
otherwise we’ll have a fun time joining flags later :grimacing:

Paula Mendes: also need to confirm: holdout == no lifecycle touches at all? (no new email drip + no new nudge surfaces) or just “no treatment variant but still baseline onboarding”? if baseline changes, holdout isn’t a holdout.

Jada Williams: yeah, this is the key question. we said “holdout mechanism” in the plan but need to be explicit. my assumption: holdout = current status quo onboarding (existing emails + no new console nudges).

Ethan Park: agree. implementation detail: treat holdout as `variant=control` that bypasses *new* sends + new nudge flag checks. (status quo paths still run as-is)

Dylan Brooks: what about users/orgs created before we turn this on? do we backfill assignment or only for new signups?

Ethan Park: for this sprint, I’d scope to: assignment created at first qualifying event we control (signup event in app) and persisted. for pre-existing orgs, we can assign on first seen post-launch (same hash, so it’ll be stable either way). no need to run a backfill job unless analytics wants it.

Logan Wright: stable hash is fine. as long as when an org shows up, we persist the assigned variant somewhere so we don’t get weirdness if IDs change formats. (but if hash is only org_id it should be stable; persistence is more for auditing/debug)

Benji Okafor: where do we persist? `experiments_assignments` table in console db? or in the flag service?

Ethan Park: simplest: flag service does deterministic calculation but ALSO writes `experiment_key`, `org_id`, `variant`, `assigned_at` to a small table on first evaluation. that gives us:
- consistent behavior across email triggers + console
- easy debugging (“why is org in holdout?”)
- analytics can join if needed

Paula Mendes: re: “email triggers + console” — please ensure **the same assignment function** is called by both pipelines. we can’t have email side using user_id while console uses org_id.

Jada Williams: yep. also need a way to force an org into a variant for QA / dogfooding.

Ethan Park: override path:
- allowlist table keyed by `org_id` (or domain) with explicit variant
- used in staging by default, production only for Redwood internal orgs + QA orgs
- precedence: override > persisted assignment > computed hash

Dylan Brooks: edge case: anonymous site visitors for retargeting? (no org_id yet)

Ethan Park: for this thread: cohort assignment is for product/email experiments post-signup. retargeting audiences are separate (Tyler/Ben owning). we can ignore anonymous assignment here.

Logan Wright: one more ask: include `exp_holdout=true/false` as a derived boolean too. makes dashboards easier.

Benji Okafor: % split: do we want 10% holdout? 5%? and do we need separate holdout per experiment or one global holdout for the whole sprint?

Jada Williams: I’d prefer one global holdout for the sprint so we can measure net impact without “control contamination”. like `lifecycle_growth_sprint_q1_2026` assignment, then sub-variants for email/nudge.

Ethan Park: we can do hierarchical:
- global experiment key determines {holdout, eligible}
- if eligible, then separate keys for email + nudge variants (or we gate them on the global cohort)
that keeps holdout clean while still letting marketing iterate inside eligible.

Paula Mendes: +1 hierarchical. requirement: events must include both keys (global + specific) or at least the global.

Dylan Brooks: sounds good. can we document this decision in the ENG ticket so we don’t re-litigate in review?

Ethan Park: yep, I’ll update ENG-10501 + PR notes in pr-28477. decision summary:
1) assignment unit = org/workspace
2) deterministic hash + persisted assignment
3) global holdout = status quo onboarding (no new drip + no new nudges)
4) overrides allowed for internal/QA

Jada Williams: perfect. :thumbsup: once the PR is up I’ll review specifically for “same assignment path for email + console” + property names.

title: #eng-platform: 1768584321
preview:
Ava Chen: Hey — noticed an odd billing spike for acct acct-onboard-42 after a model fallback during a streaming request. Console shows negative provisional credits then a compensating positive adjustment. Anyone seen this pattern?
Ravi Patel: Hmm. Can you paste the trace id and a short log excerpt? Likely related to retry/dedupe in routing.
Ava Chen: trace=5f9d2c3e, request id X-Req-Id: 01F7... Attached logs: ```INFO stream_start req=01F7 client=web model=redwood-quantized-v2 route=fallback_to-fp
WARN toolcall_truncate req=01F7 tool=search trunc_after=1024 reason=timeout
INFO stream_resume req=01F7 route=fp model=redwood-fp-v1 retry=1
INFO billing provisional_charge tokens=1532
INFO billing credit_adjustment tokens=-1532 reason=partial_tool_trunc``` 
Maya Singh: key bit: toolcall_truncate + retry caused two accounting events: provisional charge at stream start then a later credit when we detected truncation. Why did provisional charge get negative adjustment before finalization?
Chris O'Neill: Might be our provisional window closing on stream reconnects. When route switches from quant -> fp we currently re-emit a billing provisional event with a new request token delta but missing dedupe metadata.
Ravi Patel: Are the billing events tied to X-Req-Id or to a per-path session id? We had bug where reconnect creates new session id but same request header, leading downstream aggregator to treat them as separate attempts.
Ava Chen: Agree — logs show two provisional events with different session ids but same X-Req-Id. Console aggregation groups by X-Req-Id though, so we see negative credit applied first then a later positive charge, which looks like a spike.
Maya Singh: Quick ask: can we add the routing-session id into the provisional billing metadata and use it for dedupe? Also ensure toolcall truncation emits finalization before crediting.
Chris O'Neill: Suggested patch: 1) include session_id in BillingEvent 2) emit final billing marker on toolcall truncate path before sending credit 3) change console UI to show provisional vs finalized split.
deploy-bot: deploy request queued to eng-platform/billing-fixes branch.
Ravi Patel: Also add a small reconciliation job that looks for provisional negative adjustments without a following finalized event within 30s and flags them.
Ava Chen: For the dashboard: create an alert when net provisional adjustments exceed 1k tokens in 1m for a customer -> send to #incidents. We had a support ticket earlier that was confusing.
Maya Singh: I'll prototype a trace-to-billing join in telemetry that correlates session_id + toolcall_id + X-Req-Id. Should help debug customer reports.
Chris O'Neill: Can someone own the rollout plan? We should canary the billing metadata change with a dedicated account and manually verify console numbers before wide rollout.
Ava Chen: I'll own canary. Plan: rollback flag, canary to acct-onboard-42 for 1 hour, monitor net token drift, then gradual roll. Adding cherry-pick to changelog.
Ravi Patel: Thanks — PR review incoming. If we get the dedupe metadata in BillingEvent that'll probably fix the negative spike.
Maya Singh: FYI I'll add a unit test for the billing dedupe logic and an integration test for streaming+toolcall truncation.
Ava Chen: Great. marking this resolved for now — will post followups after canary.

title: #eng-platform: 1768557720
preview:
Dylan Brooks: trying to nail down Console surface area for batching SLO tiers. question: where do we configure tier (fast/standard/cost-efficient) so it matches policy precedence and doesn’t confuse customers?

Nikhil Sharma: +1. Hosted API wants: if you do nothing, you get a sane default. but also want customers to see what they’re actually getting.

Aditya Rao: Optimize POV: Console should show “Recommended tier” + “Effective tier”. recommendation can be a hint, effective is what’s enforced.

Chloe Martin: we currently have Route Policy editor (per endpoint) where you can pick model + params. adding a “Latency/Cost tier” dropdown there seems straightforward. but what about per-project default / org default?

Priya Natarajan: careful: control plane schema is route policy > project default > platform default. Dedicated also adds tenant override. Console needs to represent that stack w/o implying request-level guarantees.

Lauren Bishop: Dedicated requirement: override at tenant/cluster level should be visible and explain precedence. support tickets will happen otherwise.

Jordan Lee: current precedence from config proposal:
1) Dedicated tenant override (cluster-level)
2) Route policy explicit tier
3) Route policy “inherit” -> Project default tier
4) Project default “inherit” -> Platform default tier
5) Safety fallback (model/hw constraints)
Console should reflect “effective tier” after evaluation + reason code.

Marissa Cole: for rollout we also have feature flag / staged enable. should show that too? like “tiered defaults not enabled for this route yet”

Ethan Park: tier eval happens in policy engine at request admission. we can expose decision trace fields: effectiveTier, selectionReason, overrideSource. there’s already a reason code enum coming from runtime (see ENG-18426) but we can mirror at policy level.

Alicia Perez: UX thought: separate “Configuration” vs “Runtime behavior”.
- Config: dropdown w/ “inherit” option.
- Runtime behavior card: Effective tier = X, Why = Y, Overrides = Z.
This avoids customers thinking the dropdown is the only thing affecting it.

Kenji Nakamura: agree. in Route Policy UI: Tier field with states:
- Inherit (default)
- Fast
- Standard
- Cost-efficient
If Inherit, show inline text: “Inherited from Project settings” with link.

Felix Schneider: question on “effective profile vs overrides”: do we show actual knob values (max_wait_ms etc) or just tier?

Aditya Rao: for most users, tier + SLO target. for advanced, show “profile details” accordion. but we need to be cautious w/ hardware-specific values.

Nikhil Sharma: also need “what happens if model can’t support cost-efficient” (e.g., quantization constraint) -> then effective tier may be standard. show that.

Jordan Lee: that maps to reason code like `MODEL_CONSTRAINT_FALLBACK` or `HW_CLASS_CAP`.

Chloe Martin: if we add an accordion, it should show “profile family” rather than all knobs? like:
- Max batch tokens
- Max wait budget
- Streaming prefill cap
But we can keep it read-only.

Priya Natarajan: + ensure we don’t expose internal fleet details that change per region. maybe show “range” or “current profile version id”.

Ethan Park: we can display:
- Effective tier
- Profile ID (e.g., `rt-1.22:llama3.1-70b:h100:fast:v3`)
- Selection reason code
- Override source
That’s stable and lets support/debug link to internal mapping.

Lauren Bishop: Dedicated operators will ask “I set fast on route but it’s still standard” — this would answer if tenant override forces standard.

Marissa Cole: rollout angle: if feature flag disables tiered defaults, effective tier might say “legacy/manual”. need a state.

Jordan Lee: we’ll have reason code `TIER_DEFAULTS_DISABLED` or similar. In that case, UI should show “Tiered defaults disabled for this route (rollout)” and point to “Enable tiered defaults” toggle (if we expose it).

Dylan Brooks: do we actually want to expose that toggle in Console? release plan says staged enablement internal -> subset of hosted -> etc. I’d rather keep flags internal at first.

Nikhil Sharma: +1 keep flag internal. but Console still needs to not lie. maybe just show “Status: Not yet available” and tell them to talk to support.

Alicia Perez: ok: show “Availability” pill.
- Available
- Not enabled (contact support)
- Disabled due to incident/rollback
But only visible to admins? or everyone?

Priya Natarajan: show to project admins/operators; hide from basic users. still debating roles.

Kenji Nakamura: on the Config side we should add at least Project-level default tier in Settings > Inference Defaults. that’s the “don’t repeat for each route” path.

Chloe Martin: implementation: Project Settings page exists; adding “Default batching tier” there + route policy can inherit. minimal.

Felix Schneider: where does “recommended tier” show? maybe on model page / optimize page.

Aditya Rao: Optimize page can show rec based on observed traffic (prompt length distribution, streaming). but initial release might just show a static “standard recommended” + link to docs.

Dylan Brooks: focus this thread on Console surface area for tier selection + effective behavior. propose:
1) Route Policy editor: new field “Batching tier” (inherit/fast/standard/cost-efficient).
2) Project Settings: “Default batching tier” (inherit/fast/standard/cost-efficient).
3) Route details page: “Effective batching tier” card w/ profile id + reason + override source.
4) Dedicated only: Tenant override surfaced on tenant page + banner on route if overridden.

Lauren Bishop: banner is important. “Overridden by Dedicated tenant policy” with link to Tenant overrides page.

Ethan Park: also need to show per-route “effective config” with diff highlighting. customers love “what’s in effect” vs “what I set”.

Chloe Martin: we can add a small “Effective configuration” section that lists:
- Tier
- Model
- Region policy
- Any overrides
But we should avoid showing runtime internal scheduler knobs unless “advanced”.

Alicia Perez: I’ll mock a card:
Effective tier: Fast
Source: Route policy override
Why: Explicit route configuration
Profile: rt-1.22 / fast / v3
Notes: Streaming enabled, wait budget tightened

Jordan Lee: note: “why” should be strictly from reason codes. don’t freeform generate. keep consistent with telemetry.

Felix Schneider: + link to “View decision trace” (JSON) for internal/support users. helpful for escalation.

Nikhil Sharma: for Hosted API customers, request-level tier param exists in SDK work (header/param). Console should acknowledge that: “Requests may override tier if client sets X”.

Priya Natarajan: only if policy allows it. for now I think request-level tier is opt-in. Console should show “Client overrides: disabled/enabled”.

Ethan Park: yes, policy has `allowClientTierOverride` boolean. default false. if true, effective tier becomes “variable”. UI should show “Effective tier: varies by request” and show distribution if we have metrics.

Chloe Martin: ok that’s another field in route policy: “Allow client tier override”. behind advanced toggle.

Marissa Cole: careful: that complicates support. but at least show it read-only if set.

Dylan Brooks: action items:
- Chloe/Alicia: propose UI spec for route editor + effective tier card + banners for Dedicated override.
- Jordan/Ethan: confirm fields we can get from policy eval (`effectiveTier`, `overrideSource`, `reasonCode`, `profileId`, `clientOverrideAllowed`).
- Nikhil/Lauren: sanity check copy so we don’t overpromise SLOs.

Alicia Perez: :thumbsup: will post mocks in Figma by EOW.

Lauren Bishop: :+1: and please include the Dedicated precedence examples (tenant override > route > project > default) in UI help text.

Jordan Lee: I’ll paste draft reason codes here for UI mapping:
- `EXPLICIT_ROUTE_TIER`
- `PROJECT_DEFAULT_TIER`
- `PLATFORM_DEFAULT_TIER`
- `DEDICATED_TENANT_OVERRIDE`
- `MODEL_CONSTRAINT_FALLBACK`
- `OVERLOAD_MODE_ADJUSTMENT`
- `TIER_DEFAULTS_DISABLED`
We should decide which ones are user-facing vs internal.

Nikhil Sharma: user-facing: explicit/inherited/fallback. overload mode maybe too scary? but customers will notice behavior shifts.

Priya Natarajan: overload mode should be phrased gently: “Capacity protection active” and link to docs.

Chloe Martin: noted. we can gate that detail behind “operator details” section.

Dylan Brooks: cool. will follow up in DES-771 and link this thread.

title: #eng-platform: 1768519988
preview:
Dylan Brooks: pulling folks in here re: activation funnel instrumentation. we need to lock event taxonomy + required props + dedupe logic today so console + api teams aren’t implementing diff versions. current funnel is signup -> api key -> first request -> first 100k tokens within 7d.

Nadia Rahman: yep. biggest issue rn is 1) inconsistent event names between console + api, 2) duplicates (especially signup), 3) missing join keys (org_id / workspace_id) so Mei can’t build clean funnels.

Logan Wright: +1. also: we should decide what’s authoritative for “first_successful_request”. if SDK emits it, we’ll get false positives / retries. best is server-side from API gateway/service.

Dylan Brooks: agree. console can emit ui events (api key created button click etc) but anything “request succeeded” should be server.

Paula Mendes: from telemetry side: we also need cohort/flag properties on *every* event in the funnel so we can do holdouts + attribution later. otherwise it’s impossible to separate email vs nudge effects.

Ethan Park: for cohort, we’re rolling out `lifecycle_growth_experiments` flag w/ assignment at signup. so we can attach `experiment_cohort` (control/email_v2/nudges_v1/etc) once and propagate.

Mei Lin: pls ensure we have a stable identifier for user + org, and a request identifier for API events. right now I see `distinct_id` sometimes, `userId` other times, and no org in some console events.

Nadia Rahman: proposal (naming + required props). keep event names snake_case + namespaced:

```text
Event names (MVP)
- ri_signup_created
- ri_console_api_key_created
- ri_api_request_succeeded_first (first success per user)
- ri_api_tokens_milestone_reached (100k)

Required properties (all events)
- event_id (uuid v4)
- emitted_at (server ts if available)
- user_id
- org_id
- region
- environment (prod/staging)
- source (console/api/sdk)
- experiment_cohort
- feature_flags (string list or csv)

API-only properties
- request_id
- model
- route (e.g. /v1/chat/completions)
- deployment_mode (hosted/dedicated/private)
- sdk_language (if known)

Console-only properties
- page (route)
- ui_surface (onboarding_checklist/nudge/banner)
```

Dylan Brooks: looks good. question: do we want separate event for “api key created” vs “api key first viewed/copied”? PMs keep asking what step is actually unblocking.

Allison Grant: from observability standpoint, “created” is the milestone. “copied/viewed” is nice but noisy + hard to interpret. if you add it, pls keep it non-blocking for the funnel.

Jada Williams: +1. also if we emit a bunch of console micro-events, they’ll skew “activation” if someone refreshes / toggles. we can still track clicks but don’t count them as funnel stage.

Chloe Martin: for API key we can do:
- `ri_console_api_key_created` (stage)
- `ri_console_api_key_copied` (optional)

but keep only `created` in the funnel query.

Logan Wright: re dedupe: we should not rely on “event_id” alone because retries can create new ids. we need a deterministic idempotency key for each milestone.

Nadia Rahman: yep. suggested dedupe keys:
- signup_created: dedupe on (user_id, org_id) within 24h, keep earliest emitted_at
- api_key_created: dedupe on (org_id, api_key_id) forever
- api_request_succeeded_first: compute server-side: first success per (user_id, org_id) where http_status 2xx and auth ok; emit once. dedupe on (user_id, org_id)
- tokens_milestone_100k: compute server-side in metering; emit once per (user_id, org_id, milestone='100k', window='7d_from_signup')

Benji Okafor: on implementation: for server-side events, we can generate `event_id = sha256(event_name + user_id + org_id + milestone)` so retries don’t create dupes. still store raw event_id as property.

Mei Lin: yes pls. also important: include `signup_ts` as a property on later milestone events? or at least make sure we can join to signup table easily.

Logan Wright: joining is ok if user_id/org_id consistent, but adding `signup_ts` helps debug. optional property ok.

Paula Mendes: +1 optional `signup_cohort_date` (YYYY-MM-DD) can make dashboards faster.

Dylan Brooks: can we standardize the casing? i’d prefer `org_id` everywhere (not `workspaceId`). console uses `workspace_id` today.

Nadia Rahman: let’s set canonical:
- `org_id`
- `user_id`
- `request_id`
- `event_id`
any legacy aliases get mapped in the pipeline (Logan) but engineering should emit canonical.

Logan Wright: confirmed we can map old keys in the ingestion layer for a bit, but pls stop the bleeding going forward.

Ethan Park: cohort property naming: do we want `experiment_cohort` (string) AND `holdout` (bool)? feature flags list is already there.

Jada Williams: we need an explicit holdout bool for analysis sanity. people will forget how flags map. so:
- `experiment_cohort`: control | email_v2_a | email_v2_b | nudge_v1 | etc
- `experiment_holdout`: true/false

Allison Grant: +1. also add `schema_version` so we can evolve properties without breaking dashboards.

Nadia Rahman: ok adding `schema_version: 1` required.

Dylan Brooks: any concerns about PII? emails? domains?

Paula Mendes: no raw email in events. user_id is internal UUID. domain can be derived in CRM, but don’t emit domain here unless legal ok. keep it out for now.

Mei Lin: thank you. also: please ensure `region` is always present (we had missing in eu-west last week).

Chloe Martin: we can enforce region on server events. console region is trickier if user is remote.

Logan Wright: for console, set region = user’s selected console region / default account region. doesn’t need to be perfect, but can’t be null.

Dylan Brooks: cool. action items:
1) Nadia to post finalized taxonomy + props in the event spec doc
2) Ethan/Jada align cohort + holdout props with flag assignment
3) Chloe/Benji implement server-side dedupe strategy for first_request + 100k milestone
4) Logan/Mei validate mapping + funnel query once events land

Nadia Rahman: will update spec + link the PR when it’s up. also calling out: duplicate signup emission is still happening from console + auth service, so until fixed, ingestion dedupe is mandatory.

Benji Okafor: ack. I’ll add deterministic `event_id` for `ri_signup_created` in auth service + add “emit once” guard. will post PR link here.

Allison Grant: once this is in, I can wire the activation funnel panels without a bunch of exception logic. pls don’t rename events after we ship dashboards :)

Dylan Brooks: deal. locking names today. thanks all.

Nadia Rahman: yep. biggest issue rn is 1) inconsistent event names between console + api, 2) duplicates (especially signup), 3) missing join keys (org_id / workspace_id) so Mei can’t build clean funnels.

Logan Wright: +1. also: we should decide what’s authoritative for “first_successful_request”. if SDK emits it, we’ll get false positives / retries. best is server-side from API gateway/service.

Dylan Brooks: agree. console can emit ui events (api key created button click etc) but anything “request succeeded” should be server.

title: #eng-platform: 1768466901
preview:
Jordan Lee: got a few Qs on deterministic eval semantics for ADR-042 before we lock the policy engine config schema.

Jordan Lee: 1) When multiple signals are "bad" at the same time, are we strictly priority-ordered (first match wins), or do we compute some combined score/confidence?

Jordan Lee: 2) For corroboration: is that expressed in the config (e.g., reachability requires >=N probes failing), or is it hardcoded per signal type?

Jordan Lee: 3) Missing signals: if capacity feed is delayed / nil, do we treat as unknown or good? trying to avoid NPEs + avoid accidentally suppressing failover.

Jordan Lee: Draft schema idea (rough):
```yaml
failover:
  evaluation_mode: deterministic
  hierarchy:
    - reason: REACHABILITY
      when:
        probe_fail_rate: ">=0.6"
        window: 60s
      action: FAILOVER
    - reason: ERROR_BURN
      when:
        five_xx_burn_rate: ">=14x"
        window: 5m
      corroborate:
        - tail_latency_burn_rate: ">=6x"
          window: 5m
      action: FAILOVER
    - reason: TAIL_LATENCY_BURN
      when:
        p99_latency_burn_rate: ">=10x"
        window: 10m
      action: DEPRIORITIZE
```
curious if this matches what you all had in mind.

Ravi Menon: yep, we want strict priority with deterministic tie-breaking. no weighted scoring for v1.

Ravi Menon: semantics we discussed in routing sync: evaluate hierarchy top->bottom, first rule that is satisfied yields a single "decision" output.

Ravi Menon: important: decision includes BOTH `recommended_action` and `evidence` (which signals, values, windows) for audit + controller.

Ethan Park: +1 on first-match-wins. control plane needs predictable behavior for approvals + rollback. if a rule flips between two reasons, audits get messy.

Mina Haddad: the only nuance: some rules are "authoritative" (reachability) and should not require corroboration.

Mina Haddad: for others (5xx burn, tail latency), we said they require either (a) corroboration signal OR (b) sustained breach beyond hold-down/dampening. otherwise noisy staging flips.

Miles Thornton: from SRE POV: reachability is the only one that can trigger fast without corroboration. 5xx burn can be fast too but we *must* include regional scoping + avoid local service incident causing cross-region blast.

Miles Thornton: also pls ensure we can get a "DEGRADE_ONLY" outcome (circuit breakers/load shedding) that does NOT force region flip when downstream is degraded but region is healthy.

Jordan Lee: ok, so deterministic.

Jordan Lee: for corroboration, do we put that in config or in code? (i’d rather config so we can tune without redeploy)

Ravi Menon: config.

Ravi Menon: idea: rule has `primary` predicate + optional `requires_any` or `requires_all` list.

Ravi Menon: e.g.
```yaml
- reason: ERROR_BURN
  if:
    - metric: regional_5xx_burn
      op: ">="
      value: 14
      window: 5m
  requires_any:
    - metric: cross_region_reachability_fail_rate
      op: ">="
      value: 0.4
      window: 2m
    - metric: regional_p99_burn
      op: ">="
      value: 6
      window: 5m
  action: FAILOVER
```

Ravi Menon: reachability rule would omit `requires_any`.

Victor Alvarez: +1. reachability probe itself already aggregates multiple vantage points. the corroboration there is ">=2 of 3 vantage points failing" which we can represent as a probe_fail_rate threshold.

Allison Grant: if we represent that in config, pls keep label cardinality sane. don’t want `vantage_point` exploding downstream.

Allison Grant: better to have perf-canary publish already-aggregated per-region metrics: `reachability_fail_rate{src_region=..., dst_region=...}` and policy engine just consumes it.

Mina Haddad: agreed; policy engine shouldn’t be reasoning about individual probes.

Jordan Lee: makes sense.

Jordan Lee: Q on missing signals: ex: capacity signal missing due to fleet exporter delay. do we treat as unknown and skip rules depending on it? or treat unknown as 0?

Ethan Park: treat unknown as UNKNOWN; rule evaluation should be tri-state.

Ethan Park: if required metric is UNKNOWN, rule does not match. BUT output should record `missing_inputs=[...]` so controller can page/annotate.

Miles Thornton: +1. if we treat unknown as good, we might keep traffic on a bad region. if treat as bad, we’ll flap. so: unknown => "cannot conclude" and move to next rule.

Ravi Menon: yep. explicit `UNKNOWN` with deterministic fallback.

Ravi Menon: ordering matters: reachability / 5xx burn should be above capacity exhaustion. capacity is a gating signal (don’t fail *into* a full region), not always a trigger.

Jordan Lee: oh interesting. so capacity mostly used to *block* a failover decision?

Mina Haddad: exactly. we want something like:
- triggers: reachability, 5xx burn, tail latency burn
- constraints: destination capacity >= threshold, dns/traffic steering allowed, residency constraints

Benji Okafor: this is the piece i care about: the evaluation output should separate `trigger_reason` vs `blocked_by`.

Benji Okafor: so the controller can say "would failover due to ERROR_BURN but blocked by DEST_CAPACITY" and choose load-shed instead.

Benji Okafor: also anti-flap: we should output `hold_down_remaining_sec` if we’re inside the timer window.

Jordan Lee: ok, so schema maybe:
```yaml
- reason: ERROR_BURN
  trigger:
    ...
  constraints:
    - metric: dest_headroom
      op: ">="
      value: 0.15
      window: 2m
  action:
    on_pass: FAILOVER
    on_blocked: DEGRADE_ONLY
```
?

Ravi Menon: close. we were thinking action is still FAILOVER, but evaluation result includes `blocked=true` and `fallback_action=DEGRADE_ONLY`.

Ravi Menon: keep config simpler; controller owns final behavior based on blocked status + run mode (dry-run vs execute).

Ethan Park: +1. controller is the actor. policy engine is pure function (signals + config => result).

Allison Grant: pure function is great for replay/simulate endpoint too.

Miles Thornton: +1 replay. also helps postmortems.

Jordan Lee: tie-breaking: if two rules both match (e.g., reachability + 5xx burn), we pick first. but do we want to record both for observability?

Ravi Menon: record only winning rule as `decision_reason`, but include `secondary_matches=[...]` in debug logs (not metrics) to avoid cardinality issues.

Allison Grant: please yes on "debug logs only". metrics labels should be limited to a small enum.

Victor Alvarez: for reachability -> pls ensure we’re using BOTH control plane API reachability and data plane request success. we’ve seen cases where internal probes are ok but client path is broken (dns/anycast).

Mina Haddad: ADR-042 had reachability as "authoritative" but defined as a composite. we can model that as single aggregated metric from region-health service.

Jordan Lee: ok, so policy engine consumes *region-health verdicts* rather than raw metrics?

Ethan Park: yes ideally. region-health service should provide `region_health{region=...}=HEALTHY|DEGRADED|IMPAIRED` with components and timestamps.

Miles Thornton: and TTL/jitter so we don’t stampede during partial outages.

Jordan Lee: got it.

Jordan Lee: last Q: config naming / stability. do we call them `rules` vs `hierarchy`? and do we version schema?

Ravi Menon: `hierarchy` is fine, but call each item `rule`.

Ravi Menon: version it: `schema_version: 1` top-level.

Benji Okafor: and include `policy_id` / `policy_hash` in every evaluation output so audits can link decision to exact config.

Ethan Park: +1; control plane audit events need that.

Allison Grant: can we also standardize output fields now?

Allison Grant: something like:
```json
{
  "decision": "FAILOVER|DEPRIORITIZE|NOOP",
  "reason": "REACHABILITY|ERROR_BURN|TAIL_LATENCY_BURN|...",
  "blocked_by": ["DEST_CAPACITY","RESIDENCY","HOLDDOWN"],
  "fallback_decision": "DEGRADE_ONLY|NOOP",
  "evidence": "...stringified summary...",
  "missing_inputs": ["cap_headroom"],
  "policy_hash": "...",
  "evaluated_at": "..."
}
```
(we can keep evidence as a string blob to avoid nested objects in some sinks)

Jordan Lee: ok, will align.

Mina Haddad: :thumbsup: can you drop a follow-up in the ADR thread once you update schema draft? i want to make sure routing config tooling can validate it.

Ravi Menon: +1. please also add unit tests showing deterministic eval ordering + unknown handling.

Jordan Lee: will do. action items i’m taking:
- update config draft: primary trigger + optional corroboration, tri-state unknown, separate constraints/blocked_by
- add schema_version + policy_hash
- clarify that policy engine is pure function; controller decides execute vs fallback

Miles Thornton: thanks. once you have the updated draft, SRE can sanity check against game day scenarios.

Allison Grant: and i’ll review metrics/logging so we don’t blow up cardinality.

Victor Alvarez: i’ll send details on which reachability aggregates we can reliably publish (vantage point count etc) so it’s one metric per region pair.

Jordan Lee: perfect, thx all.
