Allison Grant: something like:
```json
{
  "decision": "FAILOVER|DEPRIORITIZE|NOOP",
  "reason": "REACHABILITY|ERROR_BURN|TAIL_LATENCY_BURN|...",
  "blocked_by": ["DEST_CAPACITY","RESIDENCY","HOLDDOWN"],
  "fallback_decision": "DEGRADE_ONLY|NOOP",
  "evidence": "...stringified summary...",
  "missing_inputs": ["cap_headroom"],
  "policy_hash": "...",
  "evaluated_at": "..."
}
```
(we can keep evidence as a string blob to avoid nested objects in some sinks)

Jordan Lee: ok, will align.

Mina Haddad: :thumbsup: can you drop a follow-up in the ADR thread once you update schema draft? i want to make sure routing config tooling can validate it.

Ravi Menon: +1. please also add unit tests showing deterministic eval ordering + unknown handling.

Jordan Lee: will do. action items i’m taking:
- update config draft: primary trigger + optional corroboration, tri-state unknown, separate constraints/blocked_by
- add schema_version + policy_hash
- clarify that policy engine is pure function; controller decides execute vs fallback

Miles Thornton: thanks. once you have the updated draft, SRE can sanity check against game day scenarios.

Allison Grant: and i’ll review metrics/logging so we don’t blow up cardinality.

Victor Alvarez: i’ll send details on which reachability aggregates we can reliably publish (vantage point count etc) so it’s one metric per region pair.

Jordan Lee: perfect, thx all.

title: #eng-platform: 1768427740
preview:
Ethan Park: trying to unblock the Cost Model API shape question for v1. do we want Console to *pull* (sync request -> cost breakdown), or do we want a *push* model (emit events + precomputed aggregates in a store) and Console reads from that?

Ethan Park: constraints: Console hot paths need p95 < ~150ms end-to-end for the cost panel. also we cannot let this endpoint become a cardinality bomb.

Thomas Baird: push is the only way you’re gonna hit p95 reliably for “dashboards” use cases. if Console is rendering a table for 7d + group by route/model/customer it’s basically an analytics query.

Chloe Martin: +1 on perf concerns, but Console also has “request details” view (per request / trace) where we want to show breakdown for *that* request. that’s not really a push/agg thing.

Benji Okafor: we might be mixing 2 things:
1) estimate/breakdown for a single request or small batch -> synchronous
2) aggregates for dashboards -> should be precomputed, or at least bounded w/ pagination + caching

Jada Williams: for Console hot path we can also cache at Console backend. cost breakdown for a request-id is immutable after the request finishes (minus telemetry backfill weirdness). so we can do a read-through cache keyed by (request_id, cost_model_version).

Thomas Baird: request_id lookups are fine. the danger is “give me breakdown for 10k requests” or “group by 12 dimensions”. if we expose that as pull, someone will do it.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: from auth/tenancy POV: push model (events) needs super clear ownership on what’s allowed to be written + data isolation. pull is simpler because the service can enforce tenant in one place w/ existing service-to-service auth.

Rafael Mendes: SRE angle: synchronous on Console path means we need strict SLO + graceful degradation. if this API has an outage, Console shouldn’t blank out cost tabs and page us all day.

Allison Grant: API standards reminder: anything “aggregate list” must have explicit bounding semantics (limit, cursor/pagination, max group-by keys, max time range) and deterministic ordering. no “unbounded” queries.

Ethan Park: ok, so: maybe v1 = pull for (a) single request, (b) small bounded set, and explicitly *not* a general analytics surface. and for dashboards we use telemetry pipeline + a materialized view later.

Chloe Martin: what does “small bounded set” mean? Console might need e.g. last 50 requests in a trace view or last 100 failed requests in a route view.

Thomas Baird: propose hard limits:
- max 200 request_ids per call
- max time range 24h on any “search” endpoint (if we even have it)
- max group-by dims = 2
- cap number of returned buckets = 500
anything else -> async job / offline pipeline

Benji Okafor: + add rate limiting by caller + tenant. also return 400 w/ a specific error code if bounds exceeded (so clients can degrade nicely).

Jada Williams: also prefer endpoint that takes `request_ids` explicitly vs “filter” semantics. filters tend to explode (regex on route etc).

Ethan Park: i like that. explicit IDs = self-bounding.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: +1. and require `tenant_id` in auth context, not in request body. don’t want clients “typing” tenant ids.

Allison Grant: caching strategy: if response is deterministic given inputs, we can support ETag / `If-None-Match` so Console can avoid re-downloading payload. also stable `schema_version` field and clear forward-compat rules.

Rafael Mendes: also need to define what happens when telemetry is lagging. if we do request_id lookup but telemetry store hasn’t ingested KV-cache stats yet, do we return partial breakdown or 404 or “pending”?

Ethan Park: good call. probably `status=partial` w/ `missing_inputs=[...]` and an `advice=retry_after_ms`.

Thomas Baird: careful: “retry after” could cause thundering herd. Console will refresh like crazy.

Chloe Martin: we can handle in Console: only retry on user action or after a backoff, and show “cost breakdown still processing”.

Benji Okafor: service-side caching/memoization can help too. if the same request_id is asked 20x in 30s, just serve from cache.

Ethan Park: yes. plan:
- L1 in-process cache in cost-model svc keyed by (tenant, request_id, schema_version)
- TTL maybe 5m for partial, 1h for complete
- optional L2 (redis) later if needed

Allison Grant: note: TTL rules need to match determinism expectations. if breakdown can change due to backfills, then we should expose `data_freshness_ts` and be explicit: v1 returns “best available at query time” and backfills may adjust for N hours.

Jada Williams: finance will want auditability. but for Console UX it’s ok if we show “estimated” and then reconciles later. we just need consistency between Console and Optimize, not necessarily perfect instant truth.

Thomas Baird: still think push for aggregates is mandatory. otherwise every dashboard load is going to compute sums on the fly.

Ethan Park: agree. but that’s phase 2. for v1 we only commit to request-level + bounded lists, and Console’s dashboard panels continue to read from existing aggregates until we migrate.

Rafael Mendes: +1, reduces outage blast radius.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: what about “what-if” endpoints? those can be expensive. also a pull model there could be abused.

Benji Okafor: for what-if, require a “workload summary” input (counts + token histograms) not raw request lists. and enforce max bins / max scenarios.

Thomas Baird: yes. if Optimize wants to run 50 scenarios x 20 models, that’s not a request/response endpoint.

Ethan Park: setting guardrails:
- `max_scenarios_per_request=5`
- `max_histogram_bins=50`
- `max_models_compared=3`
- compute budget per request, fail fast with `RESOURCE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED`

Allison Grant: make sure error codes are standardized (e.g. `RW_COSTMODEL_BOUNDS_EXCEEDED`, `RW_COSTMODEL_RESOURCE_LIMIT`, `RW_COSTMODEL_PARTIAL_INPUTS`).

Connor O'Brien: (jumping in) for release train, would be great if the v1 decision is “pull w/ hard bounds + caching + explicit IDs” so we can scope work for next sprint. :thumbsup:

Chloe Martin: caching question: should Console cache or should we tell them to rely on server caching? i’d rather cache in Console backend to keep UI stable even if service p95 jitters.

Rafael Mendes: do both. server caching protects us from thundering herd + retries. client caching protects end user p95.

Ethan Park: ok summary:
- v1 is PULL for request-level estimate/breakdown and small bounded lists (explicit request_ids)
- NO general “aggregate/group-by over time range” in v1 for Console dashboards
- strict request bounding + rate limits + clear error codes
- caching: server memoization + recommend Console backend read-through cache; add ETag if feasible
- partial results + `missing_inputs` when telemetry lag/backfill

Ethan Park: i’ll write this into the design spec + API guidelines addendum. if anyone strongly disagrees, pls say now.

Thomas Baird: no disagreement as long as “aggregate dashboards” are explicitly out-of-scope for v1 and we don’t sneak in filter endpoints.

Jada Williams: +1. i’ll coordinate with Chloe on Console caching behavior + fallback (if API degraded, show legacy values / hide breakdown).

Chloe Martin: sounds good. pls include recommended limits in doc so we don’t guess.

Benji Okafor: i’ll add quota/rate limit notes + suggested defaults.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: and i’ll review the auth section (no tenant_id in body, service-to-service only).

Rafael Mendes: loop me on SLO/failure modes once you have draft.

Allison Grant: same, I’ll sanity check bounding/pagination language.

Ethan Park: thanks all. action item: i’ll post doc link in-thread once updated.

title: #eng-platform: 1768123456
preview:
Ethan Park: quick follow-up from the recent postmortem — noticed recurring theme: ramps tuned for throughput, not failure isolation. want a small set of defensible controls we can ship quickly.
Zoe Alvarez: yep. suggestion: signature-based rollback (identify regression fingerprints) + probabilistic canary sampling so we don't blast the entire cohort. keep it simple to start.
Kai Thompson: signature rollback sounds interesting. basically we capture a small vector of metrics (error codes, top stack traces, product KPI delta) and if the vector matches a known regression pattern we automatically abort?
Priya Nair: exactly. and add a cooldown so we don't flip flags repeatedly. also add a human-in-loop tier for major-product-impact flags.
Ethan Park: proposed minimal defaults: initial_pct=2%, ramp minutes=20, probabilistic_sampling=true (sample 2% of users globally, then deterministic routing for that subset), signature_rollback window=3m, cooldown=30m.
Zoe Alvarez: I'll prototype the sampling mechanism in the flag client. plan: client-side sampling token + server-side deterministic routing to avoid double-counting. ETA: Mon 2026-03-30.
Kai Thompson: I'll take signature detection + abort logic. plan: small fingerprint payload (error_type, top_trace_hash, kpi_delta_pct). rollout: emit abort_event with fingerprint, reason. ETA: Tue 2026-03-31.
Priya Nair: I'll update runbooks: add instructions for reviewing signature aborts and an onboarding checklist for product PMs to opt flags into human-review mode. ETA: Wed 2026-04-01.
Ethan Park: good. also need telemetry: dashboard that groups aborts by fingerprint and flag. Lina from SRE recommended a quarterly digest for flags with >1 abort fingerprint in 90d. can we create a ticket?
ops-bot: ticket created INF-5012 -> quarterly-flag-fingerprint-audit, assigned to product-ops.
Zoe Alvarez: implementation notes: client sampling uses HMAC(user_id, flag_id, seed) mod 10000 < sample_threshold. will add admin toggle to force-sample for test users.
Kai Thompson: for forensic replay, I'll add a lightweight harness that replays sanitized request traces through the sampler to validate detection sensitivity. will include a small dataset in the PR.
Priya Nair: we should also add cost-aware ramping: if cost per token or infra CPU climbs > X during ramp, pause and evaluate. small protection for expensive models.
Ethan Park: add that to schema as optional metric checks: cost_delta_pct and infra_cpu_pct. default disabled for now.
Zoe Alvarez: pairing offer: I can pair with Kai Thu morning to wire up abort_event telemetry.
Kai Thompson: thanks, will take that.
Ethan Park: final action items for tracker: 1) Zoe - client sampling + admin toggles + PR (due 2026-03-30) 2) Kai - signature-detection + abort automation + replay harness (due 2026-03-31) 3) Priya - runbook + human-review workflow + digest ticket follow-up (due 2026-04-01) 4) Ethan - add schema + tracker entry + coordinate canary for two low-risk flags (due 2026-03-29).
ops-bot: reminder: short-sync scheduled Fri 10:00 -> calendar invite sent.
Priya Nair: ack. I'll draft the runbook and handoff notes and post link here when ready.
Zoe Alvarez: starting branch feat/prob-sample-flag, will post PR link when ready.
Kai Thompson: branch feat/signature-rollback up next.
Ethan Park: thanks team — keeping this thread for quick updates. if anyone hits a blocker ping me directly. :thumbsup:

title: #eng-platform: 1768080411
preview:
Julian Moore: hey platform folks — quick q on config propagation for the KV compaction kill-switch. We need to know worst/typical latency from flipping the flag to every runtime worker actually observing it. Any gotchas w/ safe-disable semantics?

Julian Moore: context: runtime-1.22 canary. kill-switch should stop compaction/eviction quickly if we see p99 blow up. we’re wiring it into scheduler loop, but control-plane propagation is the unknown.

Benji Okafor: yep. which path are you using? the standard runtime dynamic config (control-plane -> configd -> sidecar -> runtime), or LaunchDarkly-only?

Julian Moore: standard runtime dynamic config. we’re also gating behind FF for rollout, but the “oh crap” switch we want as close to immediate as possible.

Logan Wright: for dynamic config: control-plane publishes update within ~1–3s, configd long-polls w/ 10s max wait. typical node pickup is ~2–6s. worst-case if a node misses a poll (network blip) you’re looking at next poll cycle; we’ve seen ~20–30s tails in noisy regions.

Julian Moore: ok so not sub-second, but mostly single-digit seconds. do we have any hard SLO we can quote in the runbook?

Benji Okafor: we don’t have a formal SLO in writing beyond “<30s p99 propagation” for dynamic config changes. LD is similar (streaming is fast but clients can reconnect and you get a ~10–60s tail depending on SDK/backoff). for oncall docs, i’d phrase it as: typical <10s, worst-case ~30s.

Rafael Mendes: +1. for incident response we can’t assume instant. if runtime can do local backoff too (e.g., stop compaction when latency is already bad), that’s better than relying solely on config.

Sofia Ivanova: from scheduler side we can add local guardrail: if decode loop sees compaction step exceed budget or we trip the “tail-latency protect” threshold, we can auto-disable compaction locally (per-process) for a cooldown window. but we still want the global kill-switch.

Samir Qureshi: can you confirm safe-disable semantics expected by platform? we want “disable = don’t start new moves; allow in-flight relocation to finish; stop background compactor; do not evict.” i’m worried about half-moved blocks if we just hard-stop.

Benji Okafor: platform doesn’t enforce semantics — it’s just config distribution. but we *do* recommend idempotent + monotonic behaviors because of eventual consistency (some nodes see it earlier). so your plan (don’t start new work, let in-flight finish) is the right direction.

Logan Wright: +1. assume mixed state for ~30s: some nodes compacting, some not. avoid anything that requires cluster-wide coordination to be consistent. also make the “disabled” path cheap — don’t log spam on every loop.

Julian Moore: how do people usually handle “acknowledgement” that kill-switch took effect? SRE asked for something they can verify.

Caleb Johnson: easiest is emit a gauge + annotate traces/logs when the config version changes. e.g., `runtime.kv_compaction.enabled=0/1` + `runtime.config.version` label. then dashboard can show rollout of the state across pods.

Rafael Mendes: for oncall: we’ll want a runbook step like “flip flag, wait 60s, confirm enabled=0 across >95% pods in region”. is that observable today?

Caleb Johnson: yes if you export it as a metric per instance + tag by pool/region/model. if you need something fast, add a log line on config change and an exemplar span “config_update_applied” but metrics are better.

Julian Moore: ok. and for propagation: any risk of config ordering issues? e.g., we flip kill-switch then revert canary FF, etc.

Benji Okafor: ordering is “last write wins” in control-plane; nodes apply newest version they see. but because of propagation, you can get a short window where a node sees B then later sees A if your writes are racing from different sources. avoid having two different systems fight (LD + control-plane) for the *same* knob. keep kill-switch single-source.

Julian Moore: makes sense. we’ll keep kill-switch only in dynamic config, and FF only for initial enable in canary.

Samir Qureshi: one more: do you have guidance on default caching TTL? we don’t want runtime to cache config locally for minutes.

Logan Wright: configd pushes updates immediately when it sees new version; runtime should not add extra TTL beyond whatever it needs for thread-safety. most services do atomic swap on update event.

Sofia Ivanova: we’ll implement as atomic bool + read each scheduler tick. no caching.

Rafael Mendes: can someone (platform) drop a one-liner we can paste into the runtime runbook about expected propagation + mixed-state assumption?

Benji Okafor: sure:

Benji Okafor: "Dynamic runtime config propagates via control-plane -> configd. Typical pickup is <10s; plan for up to ~30s tail latency and temporary mixed state across pods. Kill-switch behaviors must be safe under partial rollout (some nodes enabled while others disabled)." 

Julian Moore: perfect, thank you. we’ll incorporate + add metrics for `kv_compaction_enabled` so oncall can verify.

Caleb Johnson: ping me when you have the metric name; i’ll make sure it’s ingested + shows up in the standard dashboard template.

Julian Moore: will do. thanks all.

Julian Moore: ok so not sub-second, but mostly single-digit seconds. do we have any hard SLO we can quote in the runbook?

Benji Okafor: we don’t have a formal SLO in writing beyond “<30s p99 propagation” for dynamic config changes. LD is similar (streaming is fast but clients can reconnect and you get a ~10–60s tail depending on SDK/backoff). for oncall docs, i’d phrase it as: typical <10s, worst-case ~30s.

Rafael Mendes: +1. for incident response we can’t assume instant. if runtime can do local backoff too (e.g., stop compaction when latency is already bad), that’s better than relying solely on config.

Sofia Ivanova: from scheduler side we can add local guardrail: if decode loop sees compaction step exceed budget or we trip the “tail-latency protect” threshold, we can auto-disable compaction locally (per-process) for a cooldown window. but we still want the global kill-switch.

Samir Qureshi: can you confirm safe-disable semantics expected by platform? we want “disable = don’t start new moves; allow in-flight relocation to finish; stop background compactor; do not evict.” i’m worried about half-moved blocks if we just hard-stop.

Benji Okafor: platform doesn’t enforce semantics — it’s just config distribution. but we *do* recommend idempotent + monotonic behaviors because of eventual consistency (some nodes see it earlier). so your plan (don’t start new work, let in-flight finish) is the right direction.

Logan Wright: +1. assume mixed state for ~30s: some nodes compacting, some not. avoid anything that requires cluster-wide coordination to be consistent. also make the “disabled” path cheap — don’t log spam on every loop.

Julian Moore: how do people usually handle “acknowledgement” that kill-switch took effect? SRE asked for something they can verify.

Caleb Johnson: easiest is emit a gauge + annotate traces/logs when the config version changes. e.g., `runtime.kv_compaction.enabled=0/1` + `runtime.config.version` label. then dashboard can show rollout of the state across pods.

Rafael Mendes: for oncall: we’ll want a runbook step like “flip flag, wait 60s, confirm enabled=0 across >95% pods in region”. is that observable today?

Caleb Johnson: yes if you export it as a metric per instance + tag by pool/region/model. if you need something fast, add a log line on config change and an exemplar span “config_update_applied” but metrics are better.

Julian Moore: ok. and for propagation: any risk of config ordering issues? e.g., we flip kill-switch then revert canary FF, etc.

Benji Okafor: ordering is “last write wins” in control-plane; nodes apply newest version they see. but because of propagation, you can get a short window where a node sees B then later sees A if your writes are racing from different sources. avoid having two different systems fight (LD + control-plane) for the *same* knob. keep kill-switch single-source.

Julian Moore: makes sense. we’ll keep kill-switch only in dynamic config, and FF only for initial enable in canary.

Samir Qureshi: one more: do you have guidance on default caching TTL? we don’t want runtime to cache config locally for minutes.

Logan Wright: configd pushes updates immediately when it sees new version; runtime should not add extra TTL beyond whatever it needs for thread-safety. most services do atomic swap on update event.

Sofia Ivanova: we’ll implement as atomic bool + read each scheduler tick. no caching.

Rafael Mendes: can someone (platform) drop a one-liner we can paste into the runtime runbook about expected propagation + mixed-state assumption?

Benji Okafor: sure:

Benji Okafor: "Dynamic runtime config propagates via control-plane -> configd. Typical pickup is <10s; plan for up to ~30s tail latency and temporary mixed state across pods. Kill-switch behaviors must be safe under partial rollout (some nodes enabled while others disabled)." 

Julian Moore: perfect, thank you. we’ll incorporate + add metrics for `kv_compaction_enabled` so oncall can verify.

Caleb Johnson: ping me when you have the metric name; i’ll make sure it’s ingested + shows up in the standard dashboard template.

Julian Moore: will do. thanks all.

title: #eng-platform: 1767938855
preview:
Ethan Park: Heads up: SDK parity work is blocked on a couple gateway consistency things. Specifically:
- 429s need consistent `Retry-After` (present on *all* 429s, incl OpenAI-compat paths)
- error body needs stable fields so SDKs can classify (code, request_id, status)
- request_id needs to always be present on errors (streaming + non-streaming)

We have PRs queued on SDK side that assume these. Can we align on what the gateway will emit + timeline?

Ibrahim Khan: +1. Conformance tests are failing on a simulated 429 where Python/TS parse Retry-After but gateway doesn’t always send it today. We’re standardizing SDK behavior to:
- if 429 and Retry-After present -> sleep that long (bounded)
- if missing -> exponential backoff + jitter

So gateway consistency is the best path.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: For error fields: please confirm we’re not including any auth context or tokens anywhere (headers/body). Some of our upstream services include a debug “auth_type” field in error responses in dev; we should strip that at gateway.

Allison Grant: From SRE side: +1 on Retry-After, but we should be careful about thundering herd. If Retry-After is always “1s” we’ll just synchronize clients. Ideal is either:
- a value derived from rate-limit window reset (seconds)
- or propagate upstream Retry-After if service sets it

SDKs will add jitter around *their* backoff when Retry-After is absent. But if Retry-After is present, most clients will follow it exactly.

Ethan Park: Current state (as far as I can tell):
- rate limiter path in gateway sets 429 but Retry-After is inconsistent (sometimes absent)
- upstream 429/503 sometimes pass through without Retry-After
- OpenAI compat layer wraps errors and sometimes loses request_id

We need to land:
1) Always attach request_id header + include request_id in error JSON
2) For 429, always include Retry-After header
3) Ensure error “code” is one of the enums the SDKs are using

Dana Lo: On TS side we’re parsing Retry-After in both `seconds` and `HTTP-date` formats. But if gateway can commit to `seconds` only, that’s simpler and matches our docs.

Mateusz Zielinski: Go SDK has similar parsing but we’d strongly prefer seconds. Date parsing is annoying and timezones cause fun.

Sabrina James: Python SDK PR assumes a `request_id` field in the JSON error (in addition to header) because sometimes proxies strip headers in customer logs. Having it in body is helpful.

Henry Cho: Product preference: keep error shape minimal but stable. We should avoid introducing new fields that imply guarantees we don’t actually support. But request_id + code are already part of our support/debug story.

Sean Gallagher: Agree with Allison: Retry-After should be “seconds until next allowed request” (integer). If we’re emitting a computed value, add a small randomization server-side? Or at least ensure it’s not always the exact same boundary for all requests in the window.

Ethan Park: Re jitter in Retry-After: standard says clients must respect Retry-After. If we add randomness server-side, it might be surprising but arguably good for load. However, we should be careful: Retry-After is semantically “when to retry”, not “a suggested delay with jitter”.

Allison Grant: I’d avoid jittering Retry-After server-side for now. Emit accurate “seconds to reset” and let SDKs add jitter only when Retry-After is absent. Otherwise we’ll have a hard time explaining behavior.

Nadia Rahman: What exact error shape do SDKs want? We can standardize JSON structure in gateway, but I want to avoid a breaking change for existing clients who parse `error.message`.

Ibrahim Khan: What we’re standardizing across SDKs (typed errors) is basically these fields, if present:
- `request_id` (string)
- `status_code` (int) or `status` (int)
- `code` (string enum)
- `message` (string)
- optional `retryable` (bool) if server wants to assert; otherwise SDK infers from status/code

We can adapt per existing shape, but need it consistent.

Ethan Park: Proposal (gateway output) for error JSON (non-streaming):

```
{
  "error": {
    "message": "Rate limit exceeded",
    "code": "rate_limited",
    "request_id": "req_..."
  }
}
```

Headers:
- `x-request-id: req_...` (or `x-redwood-request-id` if that’s our canonical)
- `retry-after: <seconds>` on 429 only

For streaming: when the stream is already established, we can’t “return JSON” midstream reliably, but on initial handshake failure we should return the same JSON.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: +1 but please confirm “request_id” format. Also we should ensure we never echo user-provided idempotency key values back (some folks put PII there).

Ethan Park: request_id is generated server-side; we won’t echo idempotency key.

Dana Lo: For OpenAI compat: today it returns `{"error": {"message": ..., "type": ..., "param": ..., "code": ...}}`. If gateway can map/augment that with request_id consistently without violating OpenAI shape, TS can still consume.

Ethan Park: We can do:
- For OpenAI compat paths, keep OpenAI fields but add `request_id` under `error` (OpenAI clients should ignore unknown fields)
- Ensure `code` is our enum string (or map from upstream) but if OpenAI expects `code` nullable, we might need `error.code` to remain compatible. We can also add `error.redwood_code` if needed, but would rather not.

Henry Cho: Please don’t add `redwood_code` unless we have to. Prefer a single `code` that’s stable across both.

Allison Grant: Also ensure 5xx errors always include request_id even if upstream times out. If the gateway synthesizes a 504, it must still stamp request_id.

Nadia Rahman: Implementation detail: in gateway, we currently stamp request_id header early, but some code paths (websocket/stream proxy) don’t include it in JSON body on error. We can unify via the same error formatter.

Ethan Park: Great. Timeline-wise, SDK releases are targeting end of week 4. We need gateway changes in staging by mid-week 3 to validate conformance.

Sean Gallagher: What’s the rollout plan? If we change error bodies, could break brittle clients.

Ethan Park: We’re not removing existing fields, only ensuring:
- request_id is always present
- code is standardized (and documented)
- Retry-After added on 429

Most clients shouldn’t break by extra fields + header. But we’ll do staged rollout (1% -> 10% -> 100%) and watch 4xx/5xx rates and support.

Redwood Deploy Bot: :information_source: PR opened: redwood/pr-28411-api-gateway-add-retry-after-on-429 (status: review requested)

Ethan Park: ^ this PR addresses Retry-After on 429. Next PR will tackle request_id propagation and error code enum mapping.

Ibrahim Khan: Can you also confirm Retry-After format? Please make it integer seconds. SDKs already support HTTP-date but we’d rather not rely on it.

Ethan Park: Yes: integer seconds. If upstream sends HTTP-date, gateway will convert to seconds (ceil) before emitting.

Mateusz Zielinski: One more nuance: some 429s come from upstream services (quota) vs gateway rate limiter. Are we standardizing `code` values? e.g. `rate_limited` vs `quota_exceeded`?

Ethan Park: Good call. We’ll map:
- gateway throttling -> `rate_limited`
- account quota -> `quota_exceeded`
- capacity -> `overloaded`

We’ll align with the error code enum doc (PR redwood/pr-28440-error-code-enum-for-sdk-consumption). If upstream doesn’t provide a code, gateway will set a best-effort one based on status + source.

Sabrina James: Works. Conformance suite will treat both `rate_limited` and `quota_exceeded` as retryable if 429 + Retry-After present.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: Security note: make sure error message doesn’t include account identifiers (we’ve seen “org_123 exceeded quota”). Should be generic; request_id is enough for support.

Ethan Park: Ack. We’ll scrub messages to be generic. Detailed reason goes to internal logs with request_id.

Allison Grant: +1. Also, if you can add `x-redwood-request-id` consistently (in addition to `x-request-id`) that’ll help support macros. Customers sometimes already capture x-request-id.

Ethan Park: We’ll standardize on `x-request-id` as the canonical externally. Internally we can keep whatever, but externally we’ll ensure a single header is always present. If we already emit `x-redwood-request-id` somewhere, we can alias it during transition.

Redwood Deploy Bot: :information_source: PR opened: redwood/pr-28426-standardize-request-id-propagation (status: review requested)

Ethan Park: Two PRs up now:
- 28411 Retry-After on 429
- 28426 request_id propagation across gateway + streaming handshake errors

Next:
- 28440 error code enum mapping + docs

If folks can review today/tomorrow, we can land to staging early next week.

Sean Gallagher: Reviewed 28411 quickly. Looks fine. Make sure Retry-After is bounded (e.g., max 60s) so a misconfigured upstream doesn’t tell clients to sleep for an hour.

Ethan Park: Added clamp to 60s on gateway-emitted Retry-After. If upstream sends >60s, we log and clamp.

Ibrahim Khan: Perfect. Once this is in staging, we’ll run conformance fixtures (429 + Retry-After, 503, network timeouts). I’ll post results here.

Dana Lo: Also please confirm 429 on streaming handshake returns JSON body, not a partial event stream. We need a consistent place to read error fields.

Ethan Park: Yes. If request is rejected before stream starts, it’ll be a normal HTTP error with JSON body. If stream drops mid-flight, the SDKs will rely on request_id from initial response headers (and/or initial event) — we can’t always send a structured error after bytes have been streamed.

Allison Grant: Sounds good. After rollout, we should watch: retry rate, 429 rate, and any spikes in concurrent connections (streaming). We’ll set up dashboards/alerts accordingly.

Ethan Park: Cool. I’ll keep this thread updated when staging deploy happens.

title: #eng-platform: 1767923401
preview:
Benji Okafor: Kicking off schema review for `shadow_traffic_policy` fields. Need agreement today-ish on required vs optional + validation, and how it composes w/ existing rollout config (route policy + canary/A-B). Dropping current proposal here.

Benji Okafor:
```yaml
shadow_traffic_policy:
  enabled: true
  sampling:
    type: "bernoulli" | "consistent_hash"
    rate: 0.01
    hash_key: "request_fingerprint_v1"   # only for consistent_hash
  shadow_targets:
    - target_ref: "model://llama3.1-70b-instruct@2026-01-05"  # or variant_id
      weight: 1.0
      timeout_ms: 1500
  behavior:
    fail_open: true
    max_inflight_per_route: 200
    include_request_headers: ["x-rw-trace-id"]
    drop_request_headers: ["authorization", "cookie"]
    metadata_passthrough: false
  output_capture:
    capture: "none" | "redacted" | "full"
    redact_profile: "default"  # required if capture != none
  scoring:
    enabled: true
    rubric_bundle: "quality-v1"
    rubric_version: "2026-01-1"
```

Ethan Park: +1 to deciding quickly. First reaction: `enabled` is redundant if policy exists; but we’ve historically kept it for feature-flag-like behavior. I’m ok either way.

Ethan Park: Required IMO: `sampling.rate`, `shadow_targets[].target_ref`. Everything else should be optional w/ safe defaults.

Paula Mendes: Product POV: we need minimal config for beta. “Pick route, pick candidate variant(s), pick sampling %, hit save.” Everything else advanced.

Ravi Menon: On composition: route has a base routing policy + rollout overlay (canary). Shadow policy should attach at the same level as rollout policy and apply *after* variant assignment for the control response.

Ravi Menon: i.e. request comes in -> select control variant (existing rollout bucketing) -> return that to user -> in parallel, mirror to shadow targets.

Jordan Lee: Agree with Ravi. Precedence should be: rollout variant selection is source of truth for control. Shadow policy shouldn’t change that.

Jordan Lee: Question: do we allow `shadow_targets` to reference “variant_id” inside the rollout object? That might be easiest for canary comparisons.

Benji Okafor: Yes, that’s what `variant_id` path was for, but it complicates validation across objects.

Mina Haddad: If shadow target is “variant inside the same rollout” we need to ensure we don’t double-apply traffic splitting. Shadow should be explicitly out-of-band.

Mina Haddad: Suggest `shadow_targets[].target_ref` accepts either:
- absolute model ref (model id + version + quant profile)
- route+variant reference (`rollout_variant_ref`)
But backend should resolve to a concrete “inference target” at publish-time, not per-request.

Cole Summers: From quotas/guardrails: we *must* know max incremental load. Strongly want `sampling.rate` required and also a route-level `max_shadow_qps` or `budget_tokens_per_min` (even if optional for now, but we should reserve field).

Paula Mendes: Please don’t force QPS budgeting for beta. Sampling rate + hard defaults is fine.

Ethan Park: Maybe compromise: keep `max_inflight_per_route` optional, default low (like 100?) and we enforce global circuit breaker anyway.

Ravi Menon: Validation rules I want:
- `sampling.rate` in (0, 1], allow 0 only if enabled=false? (otherwise weird)
- `shadow_targets` max N (2? 3?) to avoid accidental explosion
- cannot include the control target as a shadow target unless explicitly allowed (`allow_self_shadow: true`?)
- per-target `timeout_ms` must be <= some ceiling (2s?) and < user request timeout

Jordan Lee: +1 on rate validation. Also for consistent hashing, require `hash_key` fixed value (we shouldn’t allow arbitrary keys or folks will accidentally include high-cardinality/PII).

Benji Okafor: Re: hashing: proposal was `type=consistent_hash` implies we use `request_fingerprint_v1` internally; no custom input. Field becomes more of an enum.

Mina Haddad: Yes please. Allowing custom hash inputs will become a support nightmare.

Paula Mendes: On the UI: we will explain “consistent sampling” as the default, so user sees stable comparisons. That means schema should default to consistent_hash.

Ethan Park: Ok but then required fields become even smaller. Maybe:
- `sampling.rate` required
- `shadow_targets` required
Everything else defaulted.

Benji Okafor: What about `output_capture.capture`? Security is going to ask about defaults.

Cole Summers: Default should be `redacted` or `none`. Never `full` by default.

Jordan Lee: +100. For Hosted it should be `redacted` w/ strict allowlist; for Private maybe customer sets.

Ravi Menon: From routing perspective, capture mode shouldn’t affect mirroring, only what we emit to telemetry/scoring.

Paula Mendes: For beta, do we *require* capture config? I’d prefer default `redacted` + `redact_profile=default`.

Ethan Park: If `capture=redacted` by default, then `redact_profile` shouldn’t be required (assume default). But if `capture=full`, require explicit `redact_profile=none`? (or just disallow `full` unless a separate entitlement/flag)

Benji Okafor: I like: `output_capture.capture` optional; default `redacted`. `redact_profile` optional; default `default`. Validation:
- if capture==none -> ignore redact_profile
- if capture==full -> reject unless `allow_full_capture=true` (server-side capability)

Mina Haddad: Also: mirroring should not forward arbitrary headers by default. We need strict behavior.

Ravi Menon: Agree. In the data plane, simplest is: forward minimal set (trace id, request id) and never forward auth/cookies. If customer needs custom header passthrough, it’s explicit allowlist.

Jordan Lee: So fields like `include_request_headers` optional and allowlist-only. Validation that forbidden headers can’t be included (authorization/cookie/etc).

Benji Okafor: Noted. I’ll codify a denylist check.

Paula Mendes: Composition question: can a route have shadow policy *and* a rollout? Yes, that’s the main use case.

Paula Mendes: But can shadow policy live inside the rollout object too? i.e. enable shadowing only for the canary window.

Ethan Park: Implementation-wise we can support both by modeling it as:
- route has `shadow_policy` (baseline)
- rollout can optionally override/merge `shadow_policy` while active
Precedence: rollout override wins (or merges?)

Ravi Menon: Merging gets hairy. I’d prefer “rollout shadow policy overrides route shadow policy” (no merge) to avoid ambiguity.

Jordan Lee: +1 override. Merge semantics always lead to “why is it shadowing 2 targets?”

Benji Okafor: ok, so composition rule:
- If rollout has shadow policy set: use that
- Else use route’s shadow policy

Cole Summers: Any plan for “disable shadowing during incident”? Need a kill switch at org or region level. Not necessarily schema, but FYI.

Ethan Park: That’s platform flag / circuit breaker. But schema should be compatible with fail-open.

Mina Haddad: Another validation: shadow policy must be fail-open = true. If a customer tries fail_open=false we should reject in v1.

Paula Mendes: +1. PM requirement: shadowing can’t impact user response.

Benji Okafor: Great. I’ll make `fail_open` optional but only allowed value `true` for now (or just remove field). Leaving it as field w/ validation might be clearer.

Jordan Lee: Leave it but validate to true. That prevents future back-compat pain.

Ravi Menon: For streaming requests, do we need a policy field here? (like `streaming_capture: "first_n_tokens"`)

Benji Okafor: Not in this thread. But we can reserve in schema without exposing in UI. I’ll add TODO comment in PR; not blocking.

Talia Benson: Summarizing decisions for PR notes (tell me if wrong):
1) Required fields: `sampling.rate`, `shadow_targets[]` (each requires `target_ref`).
2) Defaults: sampling type defaults to consistent_hash (uses internal request_fingerprint_v1). No custom hash inputs.
3) Shadow targets max N (TBD, propose 3) and per-target timeout bounded (<= 2000ms and < request timeout).
4) Output capture defaults to `redacted` + `redact_profile=default`; `full` disallowed unless server capability flag.
5) Header passthrough default none; optional allowlist w/ forbidden header denylist.
6) Composition: rollout shadow policy overrides route shadow policy (no merge).
7) Fail-open is mandatory (only `true` allowed in v1).

Paula Mendes: That matches what I heard.

Ethan Park: Yep.

Ravi Menon: +1.

Benji Okafor: Perfect. I’ll update ENG-5401 schema + validation accordingly and link this thread in the PR description. :thumbsup:

Cole Summers: Thanks. Please include a default `max_inflight_per_route` (even if not user-set) so we can cap load.

Benji Okafor: Will do. Default 100 for now, configurable server-side.

Jordan Lee: Also make sure `shadow_targets[].weight` either defaults to 1.0 or is disallowed in v1. We probably don’t need weighting for beta.

Paula Mendes: Prefer no weighting in UI.

Benji Okafor: I’ll keep field in schema but validate: if provided, must be 1.0 for v1 (or we just ignore). Leaning validate.

Mina Haddad: Validate. Ignoring config is spooky.

Benji Okafor: ok, validate.

Talia Benson: I’ll paste this summary into the milestone doc + note open TBDs (max targets, timeout ceiling).
text:
Benji Okafor: Kicking off schema review for `shadow_traffic_policy` fields. Need agreement today-ish on required vs optional + validation, and how it composes w/ existing rollout config (route policy + canary/A-B). Dropping current proposal here.

Benji Okafor:
```yaml
shadow_traffic_policy:
  enabled: true
  sampling:
    type: "bernoulli" | "consistent_hash"
    rate: 0.01
    hash_key: "request_fingerprint_v1"   # only for consistent_hash
  shadow_targets:
    - target_ref: "model://llama3.1-70b-instruct@2026-01-05"  # or variant_id
      weight: 1.0
      timeout_ms: 1500
  behavior:
    fail_open: true
    max_inflight_per_route: 200
    include_request_headers: ["x-rw-trace-id"]
    drop_request_headers: ["authorization", "cookie"]
    metadata_passthrough: false
  output_capture:
    capture: "none" | "redacted" | "full"
    redact_profile: "default"  # required if capture != none
  scoring:
    enabled: true
    rubric_bundle: "quality-v1"
    rubric_version: "2026-01-1"
```

Ethan Park: +1 to deciding quickly. First reaction: `enabled` is redundant if policy exists; but we’ve historically kept it for feature-flag-like behavior. I’m ok either way.

Ethan Park: Required IMO: `sampling.rate`, `shadow_targets[].target_ref`. Everything else should be optional w/ safe defaults.

Paula Mendes: Product POV: we need minimal config for beta. “Pick route, pick candidate variant(s), pick sampling %, hit save.” Everything else advanced.

Ravi Menon: On composition: route has a base routing policy + rollout overlay (canary). Shadow policy should attach at the same level as rollout policy and apply *after* variant assignment for the control response.

Ravi Menon: i.e. request comes in -> select control variant (existing rollout bucketing) -> return that to user -> in parallel, mirror to shadow targets.

Jordan Lee: Agree with Ravi. Precedence should be: rollout variant selection is source of truth for control. Shadow policy shouldn’t change that.

Jordan Lee: Question: do we allow `shadow_targets` to reference “variant_id” inside the rollout object? That might be easiest for canary comparisons.

Benji Okafor: Yes, that’s what `variant_id` path was for, but it complicates validation across objects.

Mina Haddad: If shadow target is “variant inside the same rollout” we need to ensure we don’t double-apply traffic splitting. Shadow should be explicitly out-of-band.

Mina Haddad: Suggest `shadow_targets[].target_ref` accepts either:
- absolute model ref (model id + version + quant profile)
- route+variant reference (`rollout_variant_ref`)
But backend should resolve to a concrete “inference target” at publish-time, not per-request.

Cole Summers: From quotas/guardrails: we *must* know max incremental load. Strongly want `sampling.rate` required and also a route-level `max_shadow_qps` or `budget_tokens_per_min` (even if optional for now, but we should reserve field).

Paula Mendes: Please don’t force QPS budgeting for beta. Sampling rate + hard defaults is fine.

Ethan Park: Maybe compromise: keep `max_inflight_per_route` optional, default low (like 100?) and we enforce global circuit breaker anyway.

Ravi Menon: Validation rules I want:
- `sampling.rate` in (0, 1], allow 0 only if enabled=false? (otherwise weird)
- `shadow_targets` max N (2? 3?) to avoid accidental explosion
- cannot include the control target as a shadow target unless explicitly allowed (`allow_self_shadow: true`?)
- per-target `timeout_ms` must be <= some ceiling (2s?) and < user request timeout

Jordan Lee: +1 on rate validation. Also for consistent hashing, require `hash_key` fixed value (we shouldn’t allow arbitrary keys or folks will accidentally include high-cardinality/PII).

Benji Okafor: Re: hashing: proposal was `type=consistent_hash` implies we use `request_fingerprint_v1` internally; no custom input. Field becomes more of an enum.

Mina Haddad: Yes please. Allowing custom hash inputs will become a support nightmare.

Paula Mendes: On the UI: we will explain “consistent sampling” as the default, so user sees stable comparisons. That means schema should default to consistent_hash.

Ethan Park: Ok but then required fields become even smaller. Maybe:
- `sampling.rate` required
- `shadow_targets` required
Everything else defaulted.

Benji Okafor: What about `output_capture.capture`? Security is going to ask about defaults.

Cole Summers: Default should be `redacted` or `none`. Never `full` by default.

Jordan Lee: +100. For Hosted it should be `redacted` w/ strict allowlist; for Private maybe customer sets.

Ravi Menon: From routing perspective, capture mode shouldn’t affect mirroring, only what we emit to telemetry/scoring.

Paula Mendes: For beta, do we *require* capture config? I’d prefer default `redacted` + `redact_profile=default`.

Ethan Park: If `capture=redacted` by default, then `redact_profile` shouldn’t be required (assume default). But if `capture=full`, require explicit `redact_profile=none`? (or just disallow `full` unless a separate entitlement/flag)

Benji Okafor: I like: `output_capture.capture` optional; default `redacted`. `redact_profile` optional; default `default`. Validation:
- if capture==none -> ignore redact_profile
- if capture==full -> reject unless `allow_full_capture=true` (server-side capability)

Mina Haddad: Also: mirroring should not forward arbitrary headers by default. We need strict behavior.

Ravi Menon: Agree. In the data plane, simplest is: forward minimal set (trace id, request id) and never forward auth/cookies. If customer needs custom header passthrough, it’s explicit allowlist.

Jordan Lee: So fields like `include_request_headers` optional and allowlist-only. Validation that forbidden headers can’t be included (authorization/cookie/etc).

Benji Okafor: Noted. I’ll codify a denylist check.

Paula Mendes: Composition question: can a route have shadow policy *and* a rollout? Yes, that’s the main use case.

Paula Mendes: But can shadow policy live inside the rollout object too? i.e. enable shadowing only for the canary window.

Ethan Park: Implementation-wise we can support both by modeling it as:
- route has `shadow_policy` (baseline)
- rollout can optionally override/merge `shadow_policy` while active
Precedence: rollout override wins (or merges?)

Ravi Menon: Merging gets hairy. I’d prefer “rollout shadow policy overrides route shadow policy” (no merge) to avoid ambiguity.

Jordan Lee: +1 override. Merge semantics always lead to “why is it shadowing 2 targets?”

Benji Okafor: ok, so composition rule:
- If rollout has shadow policy set: use that
- Else use route’s shadow policy

Cole Summers: Any plan for “disable shadowing during incident”? Need a kill switch at org or region level. Not necessarily schema, but FYI.

Ethan Park: That’s platform flag / circuit breaker. But schema should be compatible with fail-open.

Mina Haddad: Another validation: shadow policy must be fail-open = true. If a customer tries fail_open=false we should reject in v1.

Paula Mendes: +1. PM requirement: shadowing can’t impact user response.

Benji Okafor: Great. I’ll make `fail_open` optional but only allowed value `true` for now (or just remove field). Leaving it as field w/ validation might be clearer.

Jordan Lee: Leave it but validate to true. That prevents future back-compat pain.

Ravi Menon: For streaming requests, do we need a policy field here? (like `streaming_capture: "first_n_tokens"`)

Benji Okafor: Not in this thread. But we can reserve in schema without exposing in UI. I’ll add TODO comment in PR; not blocking.

Talia Benson: Summarizing decisions for PR notes (tell me if wrong):
1) Required fields: `sampling.rate`, `shadow_targets[]` (each requires `target_ref`).
2) Defaults: sampling type defaults to consistent_hash (uses internal request_fingerprint_v1). No custom hash inputs.
3) Shadow targets max N (TBD, propose 3) and per-target timeout bounded (<= 2000ms and < request timeout).
4) Output capture defaults to `redacted` + `redact_profile=default`; `full` disallowed unless server capability flag.
5) Header passthrough default none; optional allowlist w/ forbidden header denylist.
6) Composition: rollout shadow policy overrides route shadow policy (no merge).
7) Fail-open is mandatory (only `true` allowed in v1).

Paula Mendes: That matches what I heard.

Ethan Park: Yep.

Ravi Menon: +1.

Benji Okafor: Perfect. I’ll update ENG-5401 schema + validation accordingly and link this thread in the PR description. :thumbsup:

Cole Summers: Thanks. Please include a default `max_inflight_per_route` (even if not user-set) so we can cap load.

Benji Okafor: Will do. Default 100 for now, configurable server-side.

Jordan Lee: Also make sure `shadow_targets[].weight` either defaults to 1.0 or is disallowed in v1. We probably don’t need weighting for beta.

Paula Mendes: Prefer no weighting in UI.

Benji Okafor: I’ll keep field in schema but validate: if provided, must be 1.0 for v1 (or we just ignore). Leaning validate.

Mina Haddad: Validate. Ignoring config is spooky.

title: #eng-platform: 1764021507
preview:
Ethan Park: heads up — planning to roll out the owner-mapping config update today (control plane config: incident_type/subtype -> owner + secondary + runbook links). this is the one that fixes the Routing vs Fleet split for capacity subtypes + adds a couple missing subtypes for Private connectivity.

Ethan Park: proposal is a prod canary first, then full rollout if metrics are clean. can someone sanity check the canary + monitoring checklist below?

Dylan Brooks: yep. what’s the blast radius here again — only incident-bot owner resolution + pager routing that consumes the mapping, right? no serving path.

Ethan Park: correct. no request path. consumers:
- incident-bot modal validation + auto-owner + runbook link suggestions
- pager routing helper that tries to page primary owner when `incident_type` is set
- console ops view (read-only) showing resolved owner

Allison Grant: + metrics tagging. we’re also emitting `owner_resolution_status` and `owner_team` (allowlist) in the incident events pipeline. want to avoid a cardinality bomb if someone adds random owner strings.

Ethan Park: yep. config validator enforces owner_team in allowlist (`SRE`, `Platform-ControlPlane`, `Platform-Routing`, `Infra-Fleet`, `Observability`, `Serving-Runtime`, `Security-Ops`, `Support`, `Private-Deployments`).

Mina Haddad: can you paste the specific disputed rows that changed? want to make sure we’re not accidentally owning “regional capacity hard limit” again.

Ethan Park: changes summary:
- `Capacity/Fleet Shortage -> Regional GPU exhaustion`: primary `Infra-Fleet` (secondary `Platform-Routing`)
- `Routing/Fallback Misbehavior -> Wrong fallback target`: primary `Platform-Routing` (secondary `Infra-Fleet`)
- `Overload/Throttling -> Quota enforcement bug`: primary `Platform-ControlPlane` (secondary `Platform-Routing`)
- `Private Connectivity -> DNS/TLS handshake failures`: primary `Private-Deployments` (secondary `Infra-Fleet`)

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup: for the first two. for the overload one — quota enforcement bugs live in control plane, agreed.

Devon Price: from incident-bot side, we should canary by workspace too. last time we pushed mapping changes, one workspace was on an older modal schema and it failed open (no owner field set).

Ethan Park: good call. plan is:
1) staging: apply mapping v12 + run `owner-mapping-smoke` (calls resolver with ~30 known types/subtypes)
2) prod: enable `owner_mapping_config_v12` flag for canary environments (us-east-1 control plane + incident-bot canary workspace)
3) observe for 60–90 min
4) roll to all regions + all workspaces

Sergio Costa: what are we watching during the 60–90 min window? if you’ve got a checklist, i’ll keep an eye too.

Ethan Park: checklist draft (please edit):
```
CANARY CHECKLIST (owner mapping config v12)

Pre:
- [ ] config validator passes (no unknown types/subtypes, no invalid owner_team)
- [ ] audit log entry created (who/what/when + diff)
- [ ] resolver cache warmup ok (p95 < 20ms)

During canary:
- [ ] owner_resolution_error_rate < 0.1% (control-plane metric: owner_mapping_lookup_errors / lookups)
- [ ] unknown_owner_rate not increasing (pipeline: owner_resolution_status=UNKNOWN)
- [ ] incident-bot modal submits not failing (Slack error logs / Sentry)
- [ ] pager-routing “auto-page owner” actions not spiking in reversals (manual overrides)
- [ ] mapping drift monitor clean (no config reload loops)
- [ ] metrics cardinality stable (owner_team label is allowlisted)

Spot checks:
- [ ] create a test incident in canary workspace for each:
    - Capacity/Fleet Shortage -> Regional GPU exhaustion (expect Infra-Fleet)
    - Routing/Fallback Misbehavior -> Wrong fallback target (expect Platform-Routing)
    - Auth/RBAC -> Token validation failure (expect Security-Ops as secondary, Platform-ControlPlane primary)

Rollback:
- [ ] flip flag back to v11
- [ ] verify resolver serving v11 + audit log entry for rollback
- [ ] post in #eng-sre + #incidents if any misroutes occurred
```

Allison Grant: looks good. one tweak: for cardinality, check the *event* side too (not just metrics). we should confirm `owner_team` is one of the allowlisted strings in the event payload, otherwise downstream dashboards break.

Aisha Bello: +1. i can watch the dashboard panel “Unknown/Unclassified bucket rate” + “Owner resolution failures by service”. if unknown jumps, we should pause.

Mina Haddad: for spot checks, can we add one more routing case?
- `Routing/Fallback Misbehavior -> Fallback disabled by policy` (expect Platform-Routing)
that one gets misclassified a lot.

Ethan Park: added.

Devon Price: also: test the "subtype not provided" path. incident-bot should force subtype selection now, but we still get `/incident declare` from CLI without subtype sometimes. resolver should return `UNKNOWN` with a helpful message, not null.

Ethan Park: yup. validator ensures mappings only exist for known subtype enums, but the runtime should handle missing.

deploy-bot: Deploy started: control-plane-config
- Change: enable owner_mapping_config_v12 (canary)
- Target: prod us-east-1
- Rollout: 5%
- Link: https://deploy.redwood.internal/runs/884213

Sergio Costa: i’m watching logs now. so far `owner_mapping_lookup_errors` is flat (0 in last 10m).

Aisha Bello: dashboards look normal. unknown bucket at 2.1% (baseline ~2%). no spike.

Allison Grant: i see 1 event with `owner_team="Platform-ControlPlane"` ok. no new strings. :thumbsup:

Vanessa Ortiz: canary test incident created (Capacity/Fleet Shortage -> Regional GPU exhaustion). incident-bot assigned owner `Infra-Fleet` and suggested #eng-fleet-oncall. correct.

Mina Haddad: did the routing spot check too. `Wrong fallback target` -> `Platform-Routing` came through. also got the right runbook link.

Devon Price: tested CLI declare w/ missing subtype. it returned “owner unresolved: subtype required” + pointed to the modal. no crash.

Ethan Park: nice. if we stay clean for another ~30m, i’ll expand to all regions and remove the workspace gate.

deploy-bot: Deploy complete: owner_mapping_config_v12 canary enabled (prod us-east-1)

Allison Grant: one more thing before full rollout: make sure we’re not paging the wrong team when a subtype changes mid-incident. the current pager routing caches owner at incident creation time, right?

Dylan Brooks: correct. pager helper reads the resolved owner at incident creation and writes it into the incident record. if classification is updated, it’ll recompute on update too (but only if the incident_type/subtype field changes).

Ethan Park: cool. proceeding with full rollout.

deploy-bot: Deploy started: control-plane-config
- Change: enable owner_mapping_config_v12 (all regions)
- Target: prod global
- Rollout: 25% -> 100%
- Link: https://deploy.redwood.internal/runs/884229

Sergio Costa: keeping an eye. if we see unknown bucket move > +1% or any lookup errors > 0.1% i’ll ping here.

Aisha Bello: +1. also watching “Manual owner override rate” as a proxy for misassignment.

deploy-bot: Deploy complete: owner_mapping_config_v12 enabled (prod global)

Ethan Park: rollout done. thanks all. i’ll post a short note in #eng-sre with “what changed + where to report misroutes”.

Devon Price: can you also drop in the rollback steps? people always ask during an incident.

Ethan Park: yep — will include: flip flag to v11 + link to deploy run + who to page (me / Dylan).

title: #eng-platform: 1764017782
preview:
Ethan Park: heads up: Console needs to show quality gate results + artifact links for Optimize recommendations. Can we align on what the Console API needs from Optimize service vs a separate artifacts endpoint?

Chloe Martin: yup, we’re building the Console backend slice now. Need an explicit contract from Optimize. Biggest questions: (1) what IDs we get back, (2) how to fetch artifacts securely, (3) what’s included in the “summary” vs “details”.

Dylan Brooks: +1. UX wants a compact banner in the recommendation drawer + a drilldown page with comparisons. We can’t render if we only have a single pass/fail boolean.

Yara Haddad: from Optimize side we already store gate runs (state machine). Can return `gate_run_id` + status and some rollup metrics. For artifacts we currently have S3 object keys + eval-harness run id.

Benji Okafor: request: please don’t leak raw S3 keys to Console clients. We should give Console an `artifact_id` list and Console calls a server-side endpoint to mint presigned URLs (RBAC + audit).

Jordan Lee: also note policy: some accounts will have override-only visibility (enterprise). So artifact access needs to check route/account permissions, not just “user logged in”.

Felix Schneider: what do we need for the dashboard view? We want pass/fail by config type and model family; but for the drilldown we’ll show: baseline config, proposed config, delta metrics, flake score, and links to prompt-level diffs.

Chloe Martin: proposing two endpoints:
1) `GET /v1/optimize/recommendations/{id}` returns recommendation + embedded `gate_summary`.
2) `GET /v1/quality-gates/{gate_run_id}` returns `gate_details` (metrics, thresholds, prompt set coverage, artifact refs).
Then Console backend calls an artifact url endpoint per artifact ref.

Ethan Park: +1. Keep rec endpoint small/fast. Question: do we need `gate_summary` in the list view too (recommendations table)?

Dylan Brooks: yes. List view should show “Gates: Pending/Passed/Failed/Override required” without extra round trips. If user clicks, then we fetch the gate details.

Kenji Nakamura: UX notes: for fail state we need “top 3 reasons” copy. Ex: “Rubric score -0.7 below threshold (-0.3)” + “Task success -1.2pp (min -0.5pp)”. Also show “Artifacts” with labels: “summary.json”, “prompt_diffs.html”, “raw_results.parquet”.

Alicia Perez: +1 and we need to show “Compare” CTA. So we need enough info to link baseline vs candidate outputs. If that’s a single artifact, fine, but it must be labeled.

Yara Haddad: on Optimize side we can populate:
- `status`: pending | running | passed | failed | overridden | canceled
- `gate_tier`: smoke | full
- `started_at`, `completed_at`
- `baseline_id` (or baseline config hash)
- `config_diff_id`
- `metrics_rollup`: list of key metric deltas + threshold
- `failure_reasons`: strings (already in PR-19531)
- `artifact_refs`: typed list (summary, prompt_diff, logs)

Benji Okafor: can we make `artifact_refs` stable? Like `{id, type, display_name, content_type, size_bytes}`. Display name is important for the UI.

Chloe Martin: we can do that, but reminder: our JSON responses need to be consistent across services. If Optimize owns artifact IDs, it should also own the minting endpoint.

Caleb Johnson: observability note: if Console is fetching artifacts through backend, include headers/tags so we can attribute download volume and errors. Also any presign should be short-lived (<= 15 min) and logged (audit).

Jordan Lee: +1 and we should include `requires_permission: true` on artifacts? Some artifacts might contain customer prompts (PII). We may want to hide prompt-level diffs unless the user has `prompt_viewer` role.

Ethan Park: good call. RBAC matrix:
- everyone with Optimize access can see gate summary and aggregate metrics.
- prompt-level artifacts require elevated role (or enterprise admin).
- raw logs should probably be internal only.

Logan Wright: if we’re doing that, don’t bake “role names” into payloads. Use `artifact_access_level`: `aggregate_only` | `prompt_level` | `internal` and let Console map to UI.

api-contract-bot: Reminder: API changes must be captured in the OpenAPI spec before merge. Please add to `optimize-service/openapi.yaml` and tag @api-review.

Felix Schneider: for drilldown page, do we need per-prompt metrics in the gate details payload, or is that always via artifact? If it’s in payload it can get huge.

Yara Haddad: we prefer artifact for per-prompt. Gate details payload will include:
- metric aggregates (mean/median), and maybe top-N prompt ids affected
- prompt set coverage summary (N prompts, categories)
- `flake_score` + rerun count
- `artifact_refs` for everything else

Dylan Brooks: sounds fine. Can we also include a `ui_links` object? Like direct link to eval-harness UI.

Chloe Martin: we can’t add nested objects in some of our Console templates? (for this file maybe irrelevant) — but as a service contract, sure. Alternatively, a string `external_run_url`.

Ethan Park: keep it simple: `external_run_url` as string in gate details.

Benji Okafor: re: artifact URL minting: suggest `POST /v1/quality-gates/{gate_run_id}/artifacts/{artifact_id}:mintUrl` with body `{"ttl_seconds":900}`. Response includes `url` + `expires_at`. Console backend only.

Chloe Martin: ok. Action items:
- @Yara Haddad to draft the `gate_summary` + `gate_details` schema fields + artifact ref fields.
- @Benji Okafor + @Jordan Lee to confirm access levels + enforcement points.
- @Dylan Brooks/@Kenji Nakamura to confirm which 3 failure reasons to surface + naming conventions.
We’ll start wiring Console backend with stubbed fields this week.

Ethan Park: perfect. please post the draft schema in #eng-platform once ready; we’ll do quick API review async.

title: #eng-platform: 1763764440
preview:
Sergio Costa: need 15 min of alignment on perf baseline dashboards. main goal: dashboard -> specific run manifest -> traces w/ zero guessing. what fields do we standardize + what’s the run naming convention?

Allison Grant: +1. if we don’t lock tags now we’ll have 10 near-duplicates in grafana. propose we treat run manifest as source of truth and dashboards only reflect manifest fields.

Rina Kobayashi: for the harness we can output whatever, but we need a convention that’s stable + parseable. also: engineers are already copy/pasting “run names” into slack, so it should be human-readable.

Logan Wright: from telemetry side, easiest if every metric series has a `run_id` (uuid) AND a `run_slug` (human). uuid for joins, slug for eyeballing.

Sean Gallagher: also for oncall, when we get an alert we need a one-click link to the exact manifest + trace. “which run was this?” should not be a question at 2am.

Sergio Costa: ok. draft dashboard drilldown layout:
- top: suite run selector (baseline vs PR vs nightly)
- panels: TTFT p50/p95/p99, TPOT p50/p95/p99, throughput tokens/s, plus variance panel
- each panel data point should link to: run page -> manifest.json -> trace(s)

Felix Schneider: do we want this in grafana only or also mirrored in Console? console view is useful for product folks but grafana should remain canonical.

Allison Grant: grafana canonical for eng. console can consume same data later. for Q1 baseline, priority is grafana + stable links.

Claire Dubois: methodology point: run naming should encode the measurement boundary (cold vs warm cache, streaming vs non-streaming) otherwise comparisons are invalid.

Rina Kobayashi: yup. we already have `cache_mode` and `streaming_mode` in the manifest draft. question is how much goes into the slug.

Sergio Costa: propose slug format:
`q1-2026/{suite_version}/{workload}/{model}/{region}/{gpu}/{streaming}/{cache}/{runtime_tag}/{sched_tag}/{seed}`
example:
`q1-2026/v1/chat_stream/llama-3.1-70b/us-east/h100/streaming/cold/runtime-7b3c9f/sched-q1baseline/seed42`
thoughts?

Logan Wright: looks good but slug might get super long for dashboards. maybe have both:
- `run_slug_short`: `q1-2026:v1 chat_stream llama-3.1-70b us-east h100 stream cold`
- `run_slug_full`: your full path style
and still `run_id` uuid for joining.

Allison Grant: agree. and we should avoid spaces in the canonical slug (grafana variables hate spaces). short one can have spaces if it’s just UI.

Sean Gallagher: pls include runtime build identifier that maps to code. `runtime_tag` should be either git SHA or release tag, not “mateo-test-2”. :slightly_smiling_face:

Rina Kobayashi: we can enforce via validator: `runtime.git_sha`, `runtime.image_digest` required. slug can take short SHA.

Connor O'Brien: for release gating we also need `branch` + `pr_number` sometimes. but baseline runs won’t have PR. can we do optional fields and still keep slug stable?

Allison Grant: optional fields ok, but don’t bake PR number into slug. that explodes cardinality / breaks diffs. PR info should live in manifest + run page, not the metrics series name.

Sergio Costa: +1. dashboards should group by workload/model/region/gpu/cache/streaming + show latest by `run_group`.

Aisha Bello: can we align with SLO dashboards? if we add `region` and `tier` we can compare baseline vs prod cohorts.

Logan Wright: `tier` is tricky for benchmarks (not really tiers). but we can add `cohort=baseline_suite` + reuse region naming same as prod (`us-east-1`, `eu-west-1`). don’t invent new region strings.

Claire Dubois: also please standardize model name. we’ve got `llama3.1-70b` vs `Llama-3.1-70B-Instruct`. pick one canonical and keep display_name separate.

Rina Kobayashi: in harness we can output:
- `model.id` (canonical): `llama-3.1-70b-instruct`
- `model.family`: `llama`
- `model.display_name`: `Llama 3.1 70B Instruct`
slug should use `model.id`.

Sergio Costa: ok locking: slug uses canonical IDs (model.id, workload.id, region.id, gpu_sku.id). no display strings.

Sean Gallagher: on traces: what’s the actual link target? jaeger? honeycomb? we need a stable URL scheme.

Logan Wright: for perf suite runs we can emit a trace id set in manifest (root span). run page can link to tracing UI via template:
`https://traces.redwood.ai/jaeger/search?service=runtime&tags=run_id={run_id}`
(or direct trace id if we have it)

Allison Grant: prefer `run_id` tag search rather than trace id. trace id changes if run retries.

Rina Kobayashi: we can ensure runtime spans include `run_id` and `manifest_uri`. already in PR-21503 we’re adding correlation ids for scheduler metrics.

Sergio Costa: dashboard links: each panel click should go to a lightweight “run details” page (can be a static html served by the artifacts bucket) showing:
- manifest summary
- links: manifest.json, results.json, traces search, flamegraph (if exists)
- commit diff (if PR run)

Connor O'Brien: +1. release gating wants that page in the CI output too.

Aisha Bello: can we include “noise flags” / “run health” (clock locked, warmup completed, nvidia-smi snapshot) so people stop arguing about bad runs?

Claire Dubois: yes please. minimal: `env.gpu_clock_lock`, `env.placement`, `warmup.iterations`, and a boolean `run_validated=true/false` from harness.

Rina Kobayashi: harness can write `validation` summary into results (schema). but careful: no nested json in metrics tags. keep tags flat.

Allison Grant: agreed. metrics tags (grafana labels) should be limited and bounded:
MUST: `run_id`, `run_slug`, `suite_version`, `workload`, `model`, `region`, `gpu_sku`, `streaming`, `cache_mode`, `runtime_sha`, `scheduler_profile`
NICE: `kernel_profile`
NOT IN TAGS: driver versions, container digest (too high cardinality)
those live in manifest only.

Sergio Costa: makes sense. for drilldown, dashboard pulls `manifest_uri` via join? or we derive uri from `run_id`?

Logan Wright: derive. store artifacts under path by run_id:
`s3://redwood-bench-artifacts/runs/{run_id}/manifest.json`
then manifest has the pretty slug + everything else.

Rina Kobayashi: +1. slug can change format later; run_id stays.

Sean Gallagher: love it. also add `latest_for_group` concept so dashboard variable “baseline anchor” is stable.

Allison Grant: yep: add `run_group` tag like:
- `run_group=q1-2026-baseline`
- `run_group=nightly-main`
- `run_group=pr-canary`
then dashboard default selects `run_group=q1-2026-baseline` and “most recent successful”.

Sergio Costa: action items:
1) Sergio: update dashboard spec w/ required tags + drilldown URL templates
2) Rina: confirm harness emits `run_id`, `run_slug`, `run_group`, and artifacts at `/runs/{run_id}/`
3) Logan: confirm tracing tags: runtime spans include `run_id`
4) Allison: post final list of allowed metric tags + naming rules (no spaces, canonical ids)

Rina Kobayashi: :thumbsup: I’ll add to ENG-4822 acceptance criteria.

Allison Grant: I’ll drop the tag list in-thread and in the metrics dictionary draft. and yes: no spaces. ever. :wink:

Sean Gallagher: pls also document in runbook so new folks don’t freestyle naming. thanks.

Sergio Costa: will do. closing the loop once updated spec is up.

title: #eng-platform: 1763408120
preview:
Dylan Brooks: seeing a weird owner-resolution bug in incident-bot. subtype `capacity_fleet.provisioning_lag` is resolving owner as `@eng-platform-routing` instead of `@eng-infra-fleet`.

Dylan Brooks: repro: `/incident create` -> select Incident Type: Capacity/Fleet -> Subtype: Provisioning lag -> modal shows Owner: Routing (Smart Routing). expected Fleet.

Devon Price: yep, can repro in #incidents too. also the runbook link suggestion is the routing runbook, not the fleet capacity one.

incident-bot: Owner resolution details
- incident_type: capacity_fleet
- incident_subtype: provisioning_lag
- resolved_owner: eng-platform-routing
- mapping_source: control-plane (owner_mapping v7)

Ethan Park: thanks. sounds like a bad entry in the owner-mapping config (or fallback logic picking wrong default). can someone paste the current mapping row for that subtype?

Devon Price: from `owner_mapping v7` (pulled via internal API):
```
capacity_fleet.provisioning_lag -> primary=eng-platform-routing secondary=eng-infra-fleet
```
so it’s explicitly wrong :(

Vanessa Ortiz: lol yeah provisioning lag is 100% Fleet primary. routing can be consulted for "degradation/fallback behavior" but not this.

Ravi Menon: +1. routing only owns when fallback policy or router health checks are misbehaving. provisioning lag is supply/provisioning/allocator.

Ethan Park: ack. likely came from the earlier dispute where we flipped a few "capacity_*" rows and one got fat-fingered.

Ethan Park: quick fix path:
1) patch control-plane config: set `capacity_fleet.provisioning_lag` primary to `eng-infra-fleet`
2) bump config version (v8)
3) invalidate owner-mapping cache in incident-bot (or wait TTL). what’s TTL currently?

Devon Price: TTL is 15m in incident-bot. but we also pin config in memory per workspace until refresh. I can force refresh via `/incident admin reload-owner-mapping` (new command from last week) or just deploy a small change to auto-refresh on version bump.

Allison Grant: quick note: if you’re changing config, please keep the label set stable. the incident_type/subtype values stay the same here so we’re good. just don’t rename the subtype in the process.

Dylan Brooks: can we do the config patch now + manual reload command for immediate relief? we’ve got oncall starting in ~30.

Ethan Park: yes. I’ll open a tiny PR to the control-plane mapping config and get it merged + rolled out (canary first). Devon can you do manual reload once v8 is live.

Vanessa Ortiz: also pls add `consulted=eng-platform-routing` on that row so it’s clear in the modal why routing is getting pulled in sometimes.

Ethan Park: good call. schema supports `consulted`. I’ll set:
- primary: eng-infra-fleet
- secondary: eng-sre (for comms / coordination)
- consulted: eng-platform-routing

Ravi Menon: secondary should probably stay none unless we want to notify SRE on every provisioning lag incident. consulted routing is enough IMO.

Dylan Brooks: agree. keep secondary empty for now. SRE gets pulled in by severity anyway.

Ethan Park: 👍 will do primary Fleet, consulted Routing.

Devon Price: once v8 is out, I’ll run `/incident admin reload-owner-mapping` in the affected workspaces. if anyone sees it still wrong after that, ping me.

Ethan Park: rollout plan: canary to staging + 5% prod for 10 mins, then 100%. I’ll post when it’s live.

Ethan Park: (also adding a validation test so we don’t regress: `capacity_fleet.*` subtypes cannot have routing as primary; allow routing only as consulted.)

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup:

Allison Grant: thanks for adding a guardrail test, that’s exactly the kind of thing we need.

Ethan Park: update: config PR merged, owner_mapping v8 deployed to prod.

Ethan Park: version check:
```
GET /control-plane/owner-mapping/version -> v8
```

Devon Price: running reload now.

re-tested modal:
- Type: Capacity/Fleet
- Subtype: Provisioning lag
Owner now shows `@eng-infra-fleet`
Runbook link now is fleet capacity runbook.

Dylan Brooks: confirmed on my side too. thanks all.

Ravi Menon: please drop a note in the taxonomy adoption thread that this was fixed + if anyone saw misroutes, to refresh.

Devon Price: will do. also adding a short note to the incident-bot changelog: "owner mapping reload available".

Ethan Park: follow-up for tomorrow: audit other `capacity_fleet.*` rows for similar issues. I’ll script a diff vs the spreadsheet.

Vanessa Ortiz: 👍 ping me if anything looks off, happy to review.
text:
Dylan Brooks: seeing a weird owner-resolution bug in incident-bot. subtype `capacity_fleet.provisioning_lag` is resolving owner as `@eng-platform-routing` instead of `@eng-infra-fleet`.

Dylan Brooks: repro: `/incident create` -> select Incident Type: Capacity/Fleet -> Subtype: Provisioning lag -> modal shows Owner: Routing (Smart Routing). expected Fleet.

Devon Price: yep, can repro in #incidents too. also the runbook link suggestion is the routing runbook, not the fleet capacity one.

incident-bot: Owner resolution details
- incident_type: capacity_fleet
- incident_subtype: provisioning_lag
- resolved_owner: eng-platform-routing
- mapping_source: control-plane (owner_mapping v7)

Ethan Park: thanks. sounds like a bad entry in the owner-mapping config (or fallback logic picking wrong default). can someone paste the current mapping row for that subtype?

Devon Price: from `owner_mapping v7` (pulled via internal API):
```
capacity_fleet.provisioning_lag -> primary=eng-platform-routing secondary=eng-infra-fleet
```
so it’s explicitly wrong :(

Vanessa Ortiz: lol yeah provisioning lag is 100% Fleet primary. routing can be consulted for "degradation/fallback behavior" but not this.

Ravi Menon: +1. routing only owns when fallback policy or router health checks are misbehaving. provisioning lag is supply/provisioning/allocator.

Ethan Park: ack. likely came from the earlier dispute where we flipped a few "capacity_*" rows and one got fat-fingered.

Ethan Park: quick fix path:
1) patch control-plane config: set `capacity_fleet.provisioning_lag` primary to `eng-infra-fleet`
2) bump config version (v8)
3) invalidate owner-mapping cache in incident-bot (or wait TTL). what’s TTL currently?

Devon Price: TTL is 15m in incident-bot. but we also pin config in memory per workspace until refresh. I can force refresh via `/incident admin reload-owner-mapping` (new command from last week) or just deploy a small change to auto-refresh on version bump.

Allison Grant: quick note: if you’re changing config, please keep the label set stable. the incident_type/subtype values stay the same here so we’re good. just don’t rename the subtype in the process.

Dylan Brooks: can we do the config patch now + manual reload command for immediate relief? we’ve got oncall starting in ~30.

Ethan Park: yes. I’ll open a tiny PR to the control-plane mapping config and get it merged + rolled out (canary first). Devon can you do manual reload once v8 is live.

Vanessa Ortiz: also pls add `consulted=eng-platform-routing` on that row so it’s clear in the modal why routing is getting pulled in sometimes.

Ethan Park: good call. schema supports `consulted`. I’ll set:
- primary: eng-infra-fleet
- secondary: eng-sre (for comms / coordination)
- consulted: eng-platform-routing

Ravi Menon: secondary should probably stay none unless we want to notify SRE on every provisioning lag incident. consulted routing is enough IMO.

Dylan Brooks: agree. keep secondary empty for now. SRE gets pulled in by severity anyway.

Ethan Park: 👍 will do primary Fleet, consulted Routing.

Devon Price: once v8 is out, I’ll run `/incident admin reload-owner-mapping` in the affected workspaces. if anyone sees it still wrong after that, ping me.

Ethan Park: rollout plan: canary to staging + 5% prod for 10 mins, then 100%. I’ll post when it’s live.

Ethan Park: (also adding a validation test so we don’t regress: `capacity_fleet.*` subtypes cannot have routing as primary; allow routing only as consulted.)

Vanessa Ortiz: :thumbsup:

Allison Grant: thanks for adding a guardrail test, that’s exactly the kind of thing we need.

```
GET /control-plane/owner-mapping/version -> v8
```
