title: #eng-platform: 1763004555
preview:
Benji Okafor: we keep getting on-call asks for “why did this request get 429’d / shed” and we’re stuck piecing it from logs + dashboards. proposing an internal debug endpoint in routing/policy eval path that returns the admission decision + reason + budgets snapshot. thoughts on security/access control before i open PR?

Jordan Lee: +1 on having a deterministic view. but we need to be really careful about what we return. policy eval includes tenant tier, route group, sometimes account allowlists, and capacity signals. easy to leak cross-tenant info if we’re not strict.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: what service are you thinking hosts it? gateway vs routing vs policy-engine?

Benji Okafor: leaning routing service because it already has request context (route group, region, model pinning) and calls policy-engine. endpoint would take request_id OR a synthetic “dry-run” input.

Ravi Menon: dry-run input scares me more than request_id lookup. folks will start pasting in full payloads (prompts, customer data) into a debug tool.

Ethan Park: agree. request_id-only is safer. still need to think through: authn (who can call), authz (who can see which tenants), audit logging, and rate limiting. also ensure it’s not reachable from public internet.

Amara Diallo: if routing hosts it, make sure gateway does NOT route any /admin path from external ingress. we’ve had “oops admin endpoint exposed behind edge” incidents before.

Peter Holtz: for on-call usefulness, response needs: tier, priority_class, route_budget_id, capacity_signal snapshot (queue depth, lag), and which guardrail tripped (quota vs overload vs circuit breaker). but we can’t include anything that reveals other tenants’ load.

Jordan Lee: we can structure it as “decision explanation” without global fleet state. e.g. include the *inputs used* but only those scoped to the tenant/request. capacity signals could be bucketed (GREEN/YELLOW/RED) instead of raw numbers.

Rafael Mendes: +1 bucketed. on gameday we saw folks chasing exact queue depth values anyway; bucket is fine for debugging “why best-effort was blocked but enterprise wasn’t”.

Benji Okafor: ok. proposal v0:
- Endpoint: `GET /internal/admission/debug?request_id=...`
- Only works for requests within last 30 min (in-memory cache of decision logs). no DB lookup.
- Response: reason_code, decision (ALLOW/THROTTLE/SHED), tier, priority_class, route_group, selected_backend (region/pool), overload_state (OK/DEGRADED/CRITICAL), quota_hit (true/false), and a stable policy_eval_id.
- Redactions: no customer prompt, no headers, no IP, no api key, no account name.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: how do you authenticate? if this ends up with a bearer token somewhere, it’ll get copied into random scripts.

Benji Okafor: was thinking mTLS + internal service-to-service auth, and then an operator token minted via our admin SSO proxy.

Ethan Park: we should require both:
1) request must come from corp network / VPN OR from an internal jump host (prefer).
2) user identity via SSO (Okta) -> short-lived JWT with “oncall” group.
3) service auth (mTLS) between proxy and routing.

Jordan Lee: and **authz** should be tenant-scoped. on-call should only be able to debug requests for allowlisted enterprise accounts during incidents?

Rafael Mendes: realistically, during an incident, SRE needs to debug any tenant that is paging us. but we can still gate via group membership + audited access.

Peter Holtz: can we require providing the tenant_id along with request_id and verify it matches? prevents accidental cross-tenant lookups if someone posts the wrong request_id.

Benji Okafor: yes, `request_id` + `tenant_id` required, and routing will check the cached decision record matches. if mismatch -> 404.

Amara Diallo: also rate limit the endpoint hard. like 5 rps per operator, and global limit. last thing we need is a debug endpoint taking down routing.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: logging: audit log needs user, tenant_id, request_id, and reason for access (ticket/inc). but do NOT log response body.

Jordan Lee: + don’t include raw config blobs. if we return “route budget config”, it’ll include internal knobs and sometimes per-tenant overrides.

Benji Okafor: ack. return only identifiers and computed values. no config.

Ethan Park: another point: if the decision log includes “fallback considered: true” we should be careful. some enterprise contracts don’t allow fallback; exposing that might create support issues if seen out of context.

Ravi Menon: maybe include `fallback_policy=DISALLOWED|ALLOWED|USED` and if USED, return only the target model family (no exact model id) to avoid “why did you silently switch me to X” screenshots.

Rafael Mendes: i’d rather it be accurate for internal ops. but ok to keep it minimally descriptive. support will ask anyway.

Jordan Lee: we can include exact model id internally but only for users in “platform-oncall” group, not general “support”.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: role-based fields is fine, but implementation needs to be very explicit and tested (no accidental default). security review will ask for this.

Benji Okafor: got it. i’ll implement a strict schema where sensitive fields require `role=platform-oncall`. everyone else gets the minimal set.

INT-Helpdesk Bot: Created internal ticket INT-3302 "Create internal admin access for admission debug endpoint" and assigned to Kaitlyn Nguyen. (link: https://jira.redwoodinference.example/browse/INT-3302)

Kaitlyn Nguyen: thanks. i’ll add requirements to INT-3302:
- endpoint only reachable from admin proxy
- okta group enforcement
- audit log to security index
- 30-day audit retention
- quarterly access review

Amara Diallo: can you also add a note to ensure gateway blocks `/internal/*` from public ingress + tests? we can add an integration test in gateway config.

Benji Okafor: yep. i’ll coordinate with Amara on gateway deny rule.

Peter Holtz: what about “dry run” mode for policy changes? useful but i agree it’s risky.

Jordan Lee: dry-run should live in a separate tooling path, not on routing. like a CLI that hits policy-engine with synthetic inputs, and only in staging.

Ethan Park: agree. let’s keep production endpoint request_id-only for now. if we need policy simulation, we do it in a controlled admin tool later.

Ravi Menon: +1. also, cache should store only the decision explanation fields, not full request context.

Benji Okafor: implementation detail: we already have deterministic decision logging in policy-engine PR-27418. i’ll add a small ring buffer in routing keyed by request_id storing `decision_explanation_v1` (already redacted). endpoint just returns it.

Rafael Mendes: can you include `shed_reason_code` vs `quota_reason_code`? on-call keeps mixing them. matching the new 429 headers would help.

Benji Okafor: yes. will mirror the 429 reason taxonomy:
- `X-Redwood-429-Reason: QUOTA|RATE_LIMIT|OVERLOAD_SHED|ADMISSION_CONTROL`
- plus `X-Redwood-Tier` and `X-Redwood-Priority-Class`
endpoint returns same fields.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: last security nit: ensure responses include `Cache-Control: no-store` and disable in any CDN/proxy caching.

Amara Diallo: :thumbsup: and make sure it’s not in OpenAPI public docs.

Jordan Lee: action items?

Benji Okafor: action items:
1) Benji: draft PR for routing endpoint + ring buffer + role-based field gating.
2) Amara: gateway deny rule for `/internal/*` from public ingress + test.
3) Kaitlyn: INT-3302 access model + audit log wiring requirements.
4) Jordan: sanity-check schema for “explainability” + make sure no high-cardinality fields creep in.

Ethan Park: sounds good. please tag me + Kaitlyn for review before merge. this is one of those “tiny endpoint” things that becomes a Sev1 if misconfigured.

Benji Okafor: will do. (reactions: :eyes: x2, :thumbsup: x4)

title: #eng-platform: 1762738801
preview:
Benji Okafor: can someone sanity check the canary rollout plan for hot-route protection? esp allowlisted accounts + rollback conditions + config migration risk. thinking us-east only first, 5 enterprise accts, chat+embeddings route groups. link to checklist draft: https://drive.google.com/redwood/launch-checklist-hot-route

Ethan Park: yup. what’s the proposed allowlist? and are we gating at gateway flag or policy-engine flag (or both)?

Benji Okafor: both. gateway enforces tier-aware limiter behind `hot_route_protection_gateway` and routing/policy behind `hot_route_protection_policy`. canary is allowlist at account_id in config service.

Jordan Lee: +1 on dual gating. if policy eval changes are on but gateway off, we still risk “silent” admission behavior changes. i’d prefer: policy + routing in shadow mode for 24h, then flip gateway enforcement for allowlist.

Amara Diallo: on gateway side: if we flip enforcement and config migration isn’t perfect, we could start returning 429s for folks we didn’t intend. what’s the migration story for route groups? existing per-route limits are a little… bespoke.

Cole Summers: that’s my main worry too. quota evaluator reads route_group now. migration PR maps legacy `/v1/chat/completions` -> `chat_completions` group, embeddings -> `embeddings_v1`, rerank -> `rerank_v1`. but we still have a bunch of “custom paths” (internal + older SDK variants) that alias.

Ravi Menon: re: canary scope—please keep it to a single “protected” route group first (chat) + one region. embeddings traffic is spiky and tends to look like a DDoS when a customer backfills, which will make signal hard to interpret.

Mina Haddad: routing side ok with that. we can enable priority admission for `chat_completions` only and leave embeddings on existing behavior for canary.

Benji Okafor: ok, so v1 canary = us-east + route_group=chat_completions only. allowlist still 5 accts.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: can we confirm allowlist is by “billing account” not API key? we’ve had cases where enterprise has multiple projects/keys and support gets confused.

Benji Okafor: allowlist is by tenant_account_id (billing account). keys roll up.

Ethan Park: what are the actual account IDs? please don’t put them in slack, but do we have the doc with the list + owner approval?

Benji Okafor: list is in the launch checklist doc under “canary allowlist”, gated access. approvals from Liam + Rachel for the 5.

Peter Holtz: rollback conditions: need to be explicit and automated where possible. “looks bad” is not enough during a burst.

Benji Okafor: current proposal:
- immediate rollback if enterprise p99 latency on chat route > SLO + 20% for 10m (allowlisted)
- or enterprise 5xx > 0.5% for 5m
- or shed rate for enterprise > 0.1% sustained 10m (should be ~0)
- or policy eval overhead > 2ms p95 at gateway
best-effort shedding is expected; we just watch for pager storms.

Jordan Lee: on the policy eval overhead: we should measure at routing too. we added caching but if cache miss rate spikes due to config migration churn, it can go sideways. do we have a “decision log sampling” rate locked down in canary?

Benji Okafor: yes, decision logging sample fixed at 0.1% and only for allowlisted accounts. no per-request debug logging.

Amara Diallo: 429 semantics question: during canary, customers will see new reason codes/headers? if only allowlisted, ok, but support should be ready.

Benji Okafor: yes for allowlisted. payload includes `error.type=rate_limit_exceeded` + `redwood-429-reason` header (`quota_exceeded` vs `overload_shed` vs `admission_control`). Wei/Dev have a macro draft.

Cole Summers: migration concern: do we have a safe default when route_group lookup fails? i want “fallback to legacy behavior” rather than “deny.”

Ethan Park: +1. config bugs should be fail-open (within reason) for enterprise canary, but still fail-closed for obviously invalid configs?

Benji Okafor: current implementation is:
- if route_group missing: gateway uses legacy per-route limiter path (no tier-awareness) and emits metric `route_group_missing=1`
- if policy config invalid: policy engine returns “allow” + emits `policy_config_invalid=1` (and pages us if sustained)

Ravi Menon: ok but make sure `route_group_missing` is low-cardinality (no path tag). we already had a cardinality incident on “route” tags.

Benji Okafor: yep, tag is boolean + region only. no path.

Mina Haddad: can we talk about the allowlist size? 5 is fine, but pick accounts with good observability + steady traffic. don’t pick the ones that only burst once a week.

Benji Okafor: agreed. the shortlist includes 2 steady chat heavy, 1 medium, 2 bursty (we can swap bursty out).

Peter Holtz: i’d avoid bursty for first flip. bursts make rollback noisy; also they’ll hit the new 429 surface area fastest and complain.

Amara Diallo: :thumbsup: + also if we do get complaints, better from a customer who agreed to canary.

Jordan Lee: config migration sequencing: can we run migration in “read-only shadow” first? i.e., compute route_group from incoming request, but don’t enforce budgets. just emit metrics to validate mapping.

Benji Okafor: yes. plan:
Day -1: enable `hot_route_protection_policy_shadow` (no enforcement) in us-east for allowlist; validate metrics
Day 0: enable routing admission + gateway limiter enforcement (allowlist)
Day 1: expand allowlist to +5 if stable

Ethan Park: what’s the actual rollback mechanism? one button/flag flip or multiple toggles?

Benji Okafor: single kill switch `hot_route_protection_enabled=false` per region. it disables both gateway + routing admission control and forces legacy limiter path.

Amara Diallo: can we confirm kill switch propagation time? last time config service took ~2-3 min to fan out.

Benji Okafor: current SLA is <60s, but we should assume 2 min. for “immediate” rollback we also have local override via admin endpoint (on-call only) to disable enforcement at gateway.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: admin endpoint access controls ok? we don’t want broad internal access.

Benji Okafor: restricted to on-call group + audited. INT helpdesk ticket open for provisioning (INT-3302).

Cole Summers: one more migration thing: quota windows. new tier quotas use 60s/5m windows; legacy had some 10s bursty windows. are we changing behavior in canary?

Benji Okafor: for canary, we keep enterprise windows at 60s and preserve legacy burst allowance via “burst_tokens” field. we’re not changing the default limits yet; just tier-aware admission under pressure.

Ravi Menon: acceptance criteria for canary should include: enterprise p95 stable, best-effort shed increases (expected) but no fleetwide latency cascade. we should explicitly watch scheduler lag and queue depth in us-east.

Mina Haddad: + link dashboards in checklist. also add annotation when flags flip so it’s obvious.

Benji Okafor: added. dashboard links:
- Hot-route overview (us-east): https://grafana.redwood/abc/hot-route-protection-us-east
- Enterprise SLO burn: https://grafana.redwood/xyz/enterprise-chat-slo

Peter Holtz: looks good. last ask: write the rollback conditions in the doc as “if X then do Y within Z minutes” + who is DRI.

Benji Okafor: will do. DRI for canary window = me, backup Ethan, routing Mina. paging SRE Rafael if enterprise SLO burn.

Ethan Park: ok from me once you remove bursty accounts from initial allowlist + confirm shadow mode is on for 24h. :+1:

Amara Diallo: ok as long as missing route_group fails open and we have the local gateway override documented. :thumbsup:

Jordan Lee: ok. pls ping me when shadow metrics are in so we can confirm mapping coverage before flipping enforcement.

Benji Okafor: thanks all. action items:
- update checklist with explicit rollback steps/DRI
- swap allowlist to steady-traffic accts only for wave 1
- enable policy shadow in us-east tomorrow 10am PT
will post a note here after shadow is live.
text:
Benji Okafor: can someone sanity check the canary rollout plan for hot-route protection? esp allowlisted accounts + rollback conditions + config migration risk. thinking us-east only first, 5 enterprise accts, chat+embeddings route groups. link to checklist draft: https://drive.google.com/redwood/launch-checklist-hot-route

Ethan Park: yup. what’s the proposed allowlist? and are we gating at gateway flag or policy-engine flag (or both)?

Benji Okafor: both. gateway enforces tier-aware limiter behind `hot_route_protection_gateway` and routing/policy behind `hot_route_protection_policy`. canary is allowlist at account_id in config service.

Jordan Lee: +1 on dual gating. if policy eval changes are on but gateway off, we still risk “silent” admission behavior changes. i’d prefer: policy + routing in shadow mode for 24h, then flip gateway enforcement for allowlist.

Amara Diallo: on gateway side: if we flip enforcement and config migration isn’t perfect, we could start returning 429s for folks we didn’t intend. what’s the migration story for route groups? existing per-route limits are a little… bespoke.

Cole Summers: that’s my main worry too. quota evaluator reads route_group now. migration PR maps legacy `/v1/chat/completions` -> `chat_completions` group, embeddings -> `embeddings_v1`, rerank -> `rerank_v1`. but we still have a bunch of “custom paths” (internal + older SDK variants) that alias.

Ravi Menon: re: canary scope—please keep it to a single “protected” route group first (chat) + one region. embeddings traffic is spiky and tends to look like a DDoS when a customer backfills, which will make signal hard to interpret.

Mina Haddad: routing side ok with that. we can enable priority admission for `chat_completions` only and leave embeddings on existing behavior for canary.

Benji Okafor: ok, so v1 canary = us-east + route_group=chat_completions only. allowlist still 5 accts.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: can we confirm allowlist is by “billing account” not API key? we’ve had cases where enterprise has multiple projects/keys and support gets confused.

Benji Okafor: allowlist is by tenant_account_id (billing account). keys roll up.

Ethan Park: what are the actual account IDs? please don’t put them in slack, but do we have the doc with the list + owner approval?

Benji Okafor: list is in the launch checklist doc under “canary allowlist”, gated access. approvals from Liam + Rachel for the 5.

Peter Holtz: rollback conditions: need to be explicit and automated where possible. “looks bad” is not enough during a burst.

Benji Okafor: current proposal:
- immediate rollback if enterprise p99 latency on chat route > SLO + 20% for 10m (allowlisted)
- or enterprise 5xx > 0.5% for 5m
- or shed rate for enterprise > 0.1% sustained 10m (should be ~0)
- or policy eval overhead > 2ms p95 at gateway
best-effort shedding is expected; we just watch for pager storms.

Jordan Lee: on the policy eval overhead: we should measure at routing too. we added caching but if cache miss rate spikes due to config migration churn, it can go sideways. do we have a “decision log sampling” rate locked down in canary?

Benji Okafor: yes, decision logging sample fixed at 0.1% and only for allowlisted accounts. no per-request debug logging.

Amara Diallo: 429 semantics question: during canary, customers will see new reason codes/headers? if only allowlisted, ok, but support should be ready.

Benji Okafor: yes for allowlisted. payload includes `error.type=rate_limit_exceeded` + `redwood-429-reason` header (`quota_exceeded` vs `overload_shed` vs `admission_control`). Wei/Dev have a macro draft.

Cole Summers: migration concern: do we have a safe default when route_group lookup fails? i want “fallback to legacy behavior” rather than “deny.”

Ethan Park: +1. config bugs should be fail-open (within reason) for enterprise canary, but still fail-closed for obviously invalid configs?

Benji Okafor: current implementation is:
- if route_group missing: gateway uses legacy per-route limiter path (no tier-awareness) and emits metric `route_group_missing=1`
- if policy config invalid: policy engine returns “allow” + emits `policy_config_invalid=1` (and pages us if sustained)

Ravi Menon: ok but make sure `route_group_missing` is low-cardinality (no path tag). we already had a cardinality incident on “route” tags.

Benji Okafor: yep, tag is boolean + region only. no path.

Mina Haddad: can we talk about the allowlist size? 5 is fine, but pick accounts with good observability + steady traffic. don’t pick the ones that only burst once a week.

Benji Okafor: agreed. the shortlist includes 2 steady chat heavy, 1 medium, 2 bursty (we can swap bursty out).

Peter Holtz: i’d avoid bursty for first flip. bursts make rollback noisy; also they’ll hit the new 429 surface area fastest and complain.

Amara Diallo: :thumbsup: + also if we do get complaints, better from a customer who agreed to canary.

Jordan Lee: config migration sequencing: can we run migration in “read-only shadow” first? i.e., compute route_group from incoming request, but don’t enforce budgets. just emit metrics to validate mapping.

Benji Okafor: yes. plan:
Day -1: enable `hot_route_protection_policy_shadow` (no enforcement) in us-east for allowlist; validate metrics
Day 0: enable routing admission + gateway limiter enforcement (allowlist)
Day 1: expand allowlist to +5 if stable

Ethan Park: what’s the actual rollback mechanism? one button/flag flip or multiple toggles?

Benji Okafor: single kill switch `hot_route_protection_enabled=false` per region. it disables both gateway + routing admission control and forces legacy limiter path.

Amara Diallo: can we confirm kill switch propagation time? last time config service took ~2-3 min to fan out.

Benji Okafor: current SLA is <60s, but we should assume 2 min. for “immediate” rollback we also have local override via admin endpoint (on-call only) to disable enforcement at gateway.

Kaitlyn Nguyen: admin endpoint access controls ok? we don’t want broad internal access.

Benji Okafor: restricted to on-call group + audited. INT helpdesk ticket open for provisioning (INT-3302).

Cole Summers: one more migration thing: quota windows. new tier quotas use 60s/5m windows; legacy had some 10s bursty windows. are we changing behavior in canary?

Benji Okafor: for canary, we keep enterprise windows at 60s and preserve legacy burst allowance via “burst_tokens” field. we’re not changing the default limits yet; just tier-aware admission under pressure.

Ravi Menon: acceptance criteria for canary should include: enterprise p95 stable, best-effort shed increases (expected) but no fleetwide latency cascade. we should explicitly watch scheduler lag and queue depth in us-east.

Mina Haddad: + link dashboards in checklist. also add annotation when flags flip so it’s obvious.

Benji Okafor: added. dashboard links:
- Hot-route overview (us-east): https://grafana.redwood/abc/hot-route-protection-us-east
- Enterprise SLO burn: https://grafana.redwood/xyz/enterprise-chat-slo

Peter Holtz: looks good. last ask: write the rollback conditions in the doc as “if X then do Y within Z minutes” + who is DRI.

Benji Okafor: will do. DRI for canary window = me, backup Ethan, routing Mina. paging SRE Rafael if enterprise SLO burn.

Ethan Park: ok from me once you remove bursty accounts from initial allowlist + confirm shadow mode is on for 24h. :+1:

Amara Diallo: ok as long as missing route_group fails open and we have the local gateway override documented. :thumbsup:

Jordan Lee: ok. pls ping me when shadow metrics are in so we can confirm mapping coverage before flipping enforcement.

Benji Okafor: thanks all. action items:
- update checklist with explicit rollback steps/DRI
- swap allowlist to steady-traffic accts only for wave 1
- enable policy shadow in us-east tomorrow 10am PT
will post a note here after shadow is live.

title: #eng-platform: 1761123456
preview:
Ava Chen: Quick sync — infra needs a concrete list of KV / prefix-cache signals for the Console dashboards and routing policy UX. Can runtime teams confirm what we can realistically emit today?

Ravi Patel: We can emit hit/miss counters, eviction counters, and a reuse counter scoped to (tenant, model, route). Need to agree on label cardinality.

Juno Park: Agree on labels. Proposal: tenant_hash, model, route, seq_len_bucket (0-128,129-512,>512), workload_tag. Keep tenant hashed to avoid PII.

Eli Morales: For prefix cache specifically we also have prefix_reuse_count and prefix_reuse_window_ms — useful to surface reuse vs short-lived prefixes.

Ava Chen: Good. Console product asks for hit_rate and eviction_rate (ratio) and a per-workload "reuse by workload" bar/heatmap. Also a small table of top N tenants by eviction spikes. Privacy ok with hashed tenant?

Fran López: hashed tenant_id is fine. For top-N, we should sample or only show tenants with consent if enterprise opt-in. We can show anonymized IDs like t_1, t_2 for public orgs.

Ravi Patel: Implementation notes: metrics names and types (prometheus-style) below. We should add a "cache_variant" label (kv/prefix) so dashboards can share panels.

Ravi Patel (code):
```
kvcache_hits_total{tenant_hash="...", model="gpt-open-1", route="/v1/complete", cache_variant="kv", seq_len_bucket="0-128", workload_tag="chat"}
kvcache_misses_total{...}
kvcache_evictions_total{...}
prefix_cache_reuse_total{...}
prefix_cache_reuse_window_ms_sum{...}
```

Juno Park: Also emit kvcache_kv_warmth gauge (0..1) computed as recent_hit_count / request_count so we avoid heavy cardinality on histograms.

Eli Morales: For evictions, we need eviction_reason label (capacity, ttl, manual) to help SRE debug. Also include host_pool and region for Dedicated routing analysis.

Ava Chen: UX ask: Console wants 1) overall hit_rate time series, 2) eviction-rate stacked by reason, 3) reuse-by-workload heatmap, 4) top-tenant anomalies alert (threshold + sudden drop in hit_rate). Are these metrics sufficient?

Fran López: Sounds sufficient. Need retention plan: keep 1s or 10s resolution metrics for 7d, rollup to 1m for 90d. Also add sampling guidance for high-cardinality routes.

Ravi Patel: One more: prefix length bucket label (prefix_len_bucket= "1-8","9-32","33+" ) — reuse varies a lot by prefix length.

ConsoleApp: @Ava we can create dashboard skeleton once metrics emitted. Will need queries for: hit_rate = rate(kvcache_hits_total[1m]) / rate(kvcache_hits_total[1m] + kvcache_misses_total[1m]) and eviction_rate = rate(kvcache_evictions_total[5m]) / rate(kvcache_hits_total[5m] + kvcache_misses_total[5m]).

Ava Chen: Nice. Who owns the ingest + schema? I can own Console mapping, but need runtime/infra to define metric names/labels and agree SLA for delays (e.g., <60s ingestion).

Ravi Patel: Runtime will own emitting and docs. Infra will own pipeline ingestion and schema enforcement. We should file a short RFC for metric names + examples.

Eli Morales: I'll draft a tiny RFC with examples and perf cost estimates for 99th-percentile extra labels. Expecting slight CPU/net overhead; will provide numbers.

Fran López: I'll add retention and access rules to the RFC (anonymization, sampling, top-N policy).

InfraBot: New ticket created: INFRA-3742 "kvcache/prefix metrics ingestion pipeline" — https://redwood.atlassian.com/browse/INFRA-3742 :eyes:

Juno Park: For rollout: let's emit metrics behind a flag (kvcache_metrics_v2) for 2 weeks, then enable by tenant opt-in, then default on. That minimizes noise and gives us rollout signal.

Ava Chen: Timeline: RFC this week, instrumentation PR next week, pipeline ingest and dashboard mock in 2 weeks, canary enable + monitor in week 4. Anyone object?

Fran López: No objections. Please include example queries for the canary tenants in the RFC.

Ravi Patel: I'll add example PromQL and a small exporter patch.

Eli Morales: I'll include cost estimate and a small perf test plan.

Ava Chen: Thanks team. Follow-ups: Eli will post RFC link, Ravi will post metric name PR, Fran will post retention/access doc, ConsoleApp will create dashboard skeleton issue. :thumbsup:

InfraBot: INFRA-3742 updated: linked RFC INFRA-3743, added assignees: Eli Morales, Ravi Patel, Fran López.

Ava Chen: Great, locking. We'll reconvene in the eng-platform weekly sync if anything blocks. :calendar:

title: #eng-platform: 1760965400
preview:
Dylan Brooks: We keep getting support asking for “what bundle did you install + did verify pass” and it’s not in the support bundle today. Can we add offline bundle metadata + signature verification output to the support bundle artifact? (for air-gapped / Private)

Ethan Park: +1. Right now we have to ask customers to rerun preflight with `--verbose` and paste logs.

Logan Wright: From telemetry side: we already emit local audit events for verify success/failure, but support bundle is the right place to snapshot it. What exactly do we want included?

Dev Patel: Support POV: minimum is (1) bundle build id/version, (2) manifest hash, (3) signature verify status + failure reason/error code, (4) signing key id / fingerprint that was used, and (5) whether break-glass was used.

Owen Phillips: Also the *path* to the bundle they pointed the installer at. Half of the time it’s the wrong tarball / partially copied.

Ethan Park: Careful on path. It can include hostnames, mount points, etc. Maybe include basename + a redacted path?

Caleb Johnson: If we add it, please make it deterministic and easy to parse. Support bundle ingestion expects flat text and/or JSON.

Dylan Brooks: Proposed: new folder in support bundle:
- `offline_bundle/manifest.json` (copied as-is)
- `offline_bundle/manifest.sig` (copied as-is if present)
- `offline_bundle/verification_report.txt` (human-readable)
- `offline_bundle/verification_report.json` (machine-readable)

Jordan Lee: Copying manifest “as-is” is fine but make sure we also include its SHA-256 of bytes as collected so we can detect line-ending transforms/unzip corruption. I’d put it in the report.

Kevin Osei: +1. Also include `key_id` and `key_fingerprint` fields in the JSON report. That aligns w/ audit event schema we’re drafting.

Logan Wright: Any redaction requirements? Manifest can include image refs + chart versions etc. It shouldn’t include secrets, but it is still customer sensitive.

Dev Patel: For support, that’s okay. Customers already send us versions/images during troubleshooting. But we should NOT include:
- kubeconfig
- registry credentials
- any local env var dumps

Sean Gallagher: SRE here: support bundles sometimes get shipped into internal tooling. Keep size bounded. Full bundle tarball should not be included. Just metadata.

Ethan Park: Agree. Definitely do NOT include the full offline bundle or images.

Dylan Brooks: Implementation question: where do we pull verify result from? We run verification in preflight. If customer skips preflight and goes straight install, it still runs verify (unless break-glass). We should store verify result in a local state file and have `support-bundle` command read it.

Ethan Park: We already write install state for downgrade protection. We can extend that state record to include:
- last_bundle_path (maybe redacted)
- last_manifest_digest
- last_signature_status
- last_error_code
- signing_key_id
- verified_at

Jordan Lee: Please don’t include wall-clock time as authoritative in air-gapped. Time skew is a recurring issue. But having `verified_at` is still helpful. Just label it as “local system time”.

Caleb Johnson: For JSON report schema, keep it stable:
`bundle_version`, `bundle_build_id`, `manifest_sha256`, `signature_present`, `signature_verified`, `verification_error_code`, `verification_error_message`, `signing_key_fingerprint`, `break_glass_used`, `installer_version`.

Kevin Osei: Add `trust_store_version` / `trusted_keys_count` if possible. “unknown signing key after rotation” cases need that.

Logan Wright: That’s a good call. Also add `trust_store_source` (embedded vs external file) if we have it.

Owen Phillips: Error codes should match what we’ve been using in the installer output (e.g., UNKNOWN_KEY, HASH_MISMATCH, SIG_INVALID, NOT_YET_VALID). Support uses those to pick canned steps.

Ethan Park: Yup. We’ll reuse the existing verification error taxonomy. Report can include both a short code + the raw error string.

Dylan Brooks: What about including a “bundle inspect” output? We added `installer offline-bundle inspect ...` recently. Could we just run that and capture output?

Ethan Park: Running it during support-bundle generation might fail if the bundle file isn’t there anymore. Better to store results at verification time + optionally include inspect output if the bundle path still exists.

Sean Gallagher: +1. Support bundle shouldn’t have side effects or require the original media.

Logan Wright: Re: local audit events: we can also include the last N audit events from `bundle_verification` category (success/failure). But that’s potentially noisy.

Dev Patel: Keep it simple for v1. One snapshot report + the manifest/sig bytes. Later we can add recent events.

Caleb Johnson: For deterministic support bundle content: if the manifest isn’t available, write a report with `manifest_collected=false` and reason.

Jordan Lee: One more: include the canonicalized manifest digest used for signature verification (if you canonicalize). Otherwise confusion between “file bytes digest” vs “canonical digest”.

Ethan Park: We do canonicalization internally before verify. So we can include:
- `manifest_file_sha256`
- `manifest_canonical_sha256`

Kevin Osei: Great. That maps cleanly to evidence too.

Dylan Brooks: Cool. Ethan can you take the PR? Targeting it for the next private installer cut.

Ethan Park: I can do it. I’ll wire it into the existing `support-bundle` command output. Naming: `offline_bundle/verification_report.json` + `.txt`. I’ll also ensure we include `bundle_build_id` if present in manifest.

redwood-ci-bot: Reminder: PRs touching support bundle output should update `docs/support-bundle.md` and add/adjust golden tests.

Ethan Park: :thumbsup: will update docs + add a golden test for new files.

Logan Wright: I’ll review for telemetry/audit alignment.

Kevin Osei: I’ll review schema fields + key fingerprint formatting.

Dev Patel: I’ll sanity check the human-readable report so it’s actionable.

Dylan Brooks: Action items:
- Ethan: PR to add offline bundle metadata + verification report to support bundle
- Kevin/Logan/Dev: reviews
- Caleb/Jordan: quick schema eyes

Ethan Park: One last q: path redaction. Proposal: store only basename + a stable hash of full path (sha256) so we can correlate “same path” across runs without leaking. e.g. `bundle_path_basename` + `bundle_path_sha256`.

Sean Gallagher: That’s reasonable.

Dev Patel: Works for Support.

Jordan Lee: +1.

Caleb Johnson: +1.

Dylan Brooks: Done. Let’s land it this week if possible.

- Kevin/Logan/Dev: reviews
- Caleb/Jordan: quick schema eyes

Ethan Park: One last q: path redaction. Proposal: store only basename + a stable hash of full path (sha256) so we can correlate “same path” across runs without leaking. e.g. `bundle_path_basename` + `bundle_path_sha256`.

Sean Gallagher: That’s reasonable.

Dev Patel: Works for Support.

Jordan Lee: +1.

Caleb Johnson: +1.

Dylan Brooks: Done. Let’s land it this week if possible.

title: #eng-platform: 1759910223
preview:
Allison Grant: trying to unblock status page v2 rollout: dashboards + alerts naming is all over the place rn. we need a consistent mapping from internal service names -> public status components so oncall can pick the right component quickly.

Aisha Bello: +1. the SLO burn alerts i’m wiring up are labeled `service=gateway` / `cluster=use1` / etc. but status page wants `component_id` + `region` + maybe `capability` (streaming/embeddings).

Logan Wright: i can take a pass at the mapping table, but we need to agree on the “public label” vocabulary. do we already have canonical ids?

Christine Wong: taxonomy doc has draft ids, but we keep drifting in Grafana. example: some dashboards call it “API Gateway - US East” and others “Hosted API (use1)”.

Mina Haddad: canonical should be: product surface x region x capability. region tokens should match routing taxonomy: `us-east`, `us-west`, `eu-west`. please no `use1` in public-facing identifiers.

Ravi Menon: internal reality: metrics tags are `region=use1` today in a bunch of pipelines. we can map in dashboards/alerts short-term, but long-term we should emit `region=us-east` at source (or add a derived label).

Caleb Johnson: we can do a relabel step in Prometheus for now:
```
- source_labels: [region]
  regex: use1
  target_label: public_region
  replacement: us-east
```
…but this gets messy when teams have `us_east_1` variants.

Allison Grant: propose we standardize on `component_id` label for anything that can page SRE + might be used for status. dashboards should be grouped by `component_id` and `public_region`.

Aisha Bello: yes pls. for burn alerts, i need stable label keys:
- `component_id` (ex: `hosted-api`, `hosted-api-streaming`, `hosted-api-embeddings`)
- `public_region` (ex: `us-east`)
- optional `capability`? (might be redundant if baked into component_id)

Mina Haddad: i’d prefer capability encoded in component_id to keep status component map 1:1. `capability` as separate dim is fine internally, but status page can’t do “multi-select” cleanly.

Logan Wright: can we define an actual mapping list in one place? dashboard creators keep inventing names. maybe we keep a registry JSON in repo + dashboards/alerts reference it.

Christine Wong: +1. right now i’m literally copy/pasting ids into incident-bot fields. would love a single source-of-truth + schema validation.

Ethan Park: for Dedicated control-plane signals: what’s the public component? “Dedicated Control Plane” is vague but probably what we can expose. i can tag control-plane metrics with `component_id=dedicated-control-plane`.

Allison Grant: ok, concrete outputs:
1) naming convention for dashboards
2) alert annotations include status component + runbook link
3) mapping rules for internal services -> component_id

Aisha Bello: dashboard naming proposal:
`Status Component / <component_id> / <public_region>`
and title like `Hosted API - US East` (display name). folder in Grafana: `Status Components`.

Ravi Menon: for mapping rules, here’s a starting point:
- `gateway` + route `POST /v1/chat/completions` => `hosted-api`
- `streaming-gateway` or `stream=true` traffic => `hosted-api-streaming`
- `embeddings-worker` or route `/v1/embeddings` => `hosted-api-embeddings`
- `rerank` => `hosted-api-reranking`
- console backend => `console`
- rollout/control plane => `dedicated-control-plane`

Christine Wong: small nit: console incidents should be split “Console (UI/API)” vs “Console - Auth”? or too granular?

Allison Grant: too granular for v2. keep `console` single component. we can expand later once we have better signals.

Caleb Johnson: re: service names -> component_id, we should do it off request tags when possible. service-name-only breaks when gateway errors are specific to embeddings route. better to compute component_id from `route` + `streaming` flag.

Aisha Bello: yep. otherwise we’ll mark Hosted API degraded when only embeddings is down, which is exactly what customers complained about.

Logan Wright: who owns turning those tags on in metrics? i saw a PR draft about gateway metrics tags.

Christine Wong: Logan can you link it? i want to align with the schema before we merge more dashboard changes.

Logan Wright: yeah, it’s in redwood repo, adds `component_id` tagging at the gateway. still WIP but directionally:
- `component_id` derived from `{route, streaming, model_type}`
- `public_region` derived from routing region

Ethan Park: for control plane, we can’t derive from route the same way. it’s internal gRPC. we’ll tag at source.

Allison Grant: works. action items:
- Aisha: update SLO burn alerts to use `component_id` + `public_region` (and keep legacy labels for a week)
- Caleb: prom relabel rules to populate `public_region` consistently (use mapping table)
- Ravi + Mina: finalize the internal->public region mapping tokens (no `use1` in any customer-facing strings)
- Christine: update dashboards naming + folder structure for the status components set
- Logan: draft component registry doc + proposed JSON schema (ids + display name + owner)

Christine Wong: for dashboard naming, can we lock display names? e.g.
- `Hosted API`
- `Hosted API - Streaming`
- `Hosted API - Embeddings`
- `Hosted API - Reranking`
- `Console`
- `Dedicated Control Plane`

Mina Haddad: + region suffix in the title: `Hosted API - Streaming (US East)` etc. but keep component_id stable and lowercase.

Aisha Bello: also pls keep alert titles predictable:
`[SLO] <component display> <region> burn rate (page)`
so oncall isn’t decoding 3 different formats.

Allison Grant: +1. add to alert annotations:
- `status_component_id`
- `status_component_display`
- `status_region`
- `runbook_url` (status page update runbook)

Devon Price: FYI incident-bot can auto-suggest component if alert payload includes `status_component_id`. if we standardize this, we can shave a few mins off TTFU.

Christine Wong: love that. currently we just paste screenshots :grimacing:

Caleb Johnson: re: relabel mapping table, can we just agree:
`use1 -> us-east`
`usw2 -> us-west`
`euw1 -> eu-west`
? i’ll handle weird ones as we find them.

Mina Haddad: yes, and document it. also note: `eu-central` will come soon; don’t hardcode only those 3.

Ravi Menon: i’ll put the region map in the working notes + link here once it’s cleaned up.

Allison Grant: cool. let’s aim to have:
- dashboards renamed by EOW
- burn alerts updated early next week
- registry + schema draft in review by mid next week

Aisha Bello: sounds good. dropping a quick example query for dashboards so folks can copy:
```
sum by (component_id, public_region) (rate(request_errors_total{component_id!=""}[5m]))
```

Christine Wong: :thumbsup: i’ll start with the US East panels first since that’s where we’ve had the comms issues.

Allison Grant: thanks all. please keep any new dashboards/alerts using `component_id` + `public_region` from here on, even if we have to bridge legacy tags temporarily.

title: #eng-platform: 1759004321
preview:
Alex Chen: Quick sync — planning the /api/generate shim cutover next Thursday (TZ: PST). Owners: Maria (SDK), Samir (runtime), Jordan (docs). Any blockers?
Maria Lopez: I have PR 4523 open for the SDK header compatibility. Link: https://git.company/repo/pull/4523. Tests failing in CI because of an auth header shape change.
Samir Patel: Noted. I'm seeing a perf regression on long-context requests with the new kernel selection, likely unrelated to the header but will triage. ETA tomorrow EOD.
Jordan Blake: Docs draft is here: https://docs.company/springboard/shim-cutover. Need final callouts on fallback behavior and example code.
Alex Chen: Maria — how much work to make SDK accept both header forms for a short backcompat window?
Maria Lopez: Quick patch is ~2 files, ~45 LOC. I can push a short-lived shim that accepts both and logs a deprecation warning. Will add an env flag to flip off.
Priya Rao: FYI security review wanted explicit mention of token rotation impact — Samir/Maria can you confirm no token exchange required?
Samir Patel: Confirming runtime doesn't require token exchange for the shim. Headers are forwarded untouched.
Maria Lopez: SDK also just forwards header; no rotation. I'll add the security note to the PR description.
Alex Chen: Great. Blocker list: 1) CI test flake (Maria), 2) runtime perf triage (Samir), 3) docs + changelog (Jordan), 4) release infra slot (deploy-bot needs to confirm).
deploy-bot: Canary slot requested for 2026-04-08 22:00 UTC. Reply with :thumbsup: to reserve.
Jordan Blake: I'll prepare the changelog entry and consumer migration guide — aiming to have it published the same day as cutover.
Maria Lopez: Pushed a WIP that accepts both headers and emits a deprecation log. Running CI now.
Samir Patel: Repro for perf regression: run with seq=4096, model=xlarge. Root cause looks like kernel fallback for attention. Mitigating patch in progress.
Alex Chen: If Samir needs more time, we can gate long-seq traffic to legacy model variant for 48h. Is that feasible?
Samir Patel: Yes, can route long sequences to legacy variant via routing policy. Will draft the config.
Priya Rao: Also tagging security docs with example mitigation for compromised headers. Will add to Jordan's doc.
Maria Lopez: CI passed on main-compat branch. PR updated: https://git.company/repo/pull/4523. Recommend merging after Samir's patch lands and canary slot confirmed.
deploy-bot: Canary slot confirmed for 2026-04-08 22:00 UTC. Slot ID: cs-7782.
Alex Chen: OK next steps — Samir lands perf patch, Maria merges shim PR, Jordan publishes docs, then we run canary. I'll run a final smoke checklist the day before.
Jordan Blake: I'll put a checklist in the doc with items: smoke tests, rollback steps, support playbook.
Maria Lopez: Ping me on review if you need the env flag name.
Samir Patel: Will post the kernel patch PR shortly.
Alex Chen: Thanks all. If no objections I'll mark owners in the rollout board and start the notification sequence.
Priya Rao: No objections from Sec. :+1: 
Maria Lopez: :thumbsup: 
Samir Patel: :thumbsup:

title: #eng-platform: 1758019999
preview:
Ava Chen: quick late drop-in — routing policy PR merged but staging shows intermittent 401s after jwt rotation. PR: https://github.com/redwood-inference/routing/pull/482 :wave:
Samir Patel: saw the 401 spike around 10:12. stacktrace points to the proxy dropping `kid` when header is forwarded through the edge. adding logs now.
Maya Ortiz: pinging @Theo Li — did we change anything in the jwt middleware for staging yesterday?
Theo Li: I deployed the rotation job but didn't touch middleware. I'll take a look at proxy headers in 20m. Can you paste an example failing header?
Samir Patel: here's a sanitized example: `Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJSUzI1NiIsInR5cCI6IkpXVCIsImtpZCI6ImVjMiJ9...` The `kid` is present in the token header but downstream service sees empty kid.
Ava Chen: repro steps I used: `curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>" https://staging-gw.redwood/api/health` got 401. On the other hand calling the service direct with the same token passes.
Samir Patel: suspecting our edge mutates headers on gzip chunk boundary; pushing a small debug build to staging: `edge-proxy:debugging/kid-echo`
deploy-bot: Canary deploy started for edge-proxy:debugging/kid-echo to gw-staging (canary id c-78). monitoring 3 pods.
Maya Ortiz: I can pair with Samir for 15m after your logs land. also quick ask: can someone review PR 482? it's small but blocks the dedicated policy rollout.
Theo Li: on it — will check proxy logs and confirm whether rotation job changed outbound behavior. ETA 25m.
Samir Patel: logs pushed, link: https://logs.redwood/team/edge/2024/08/1758019999-kid-echo — you'll see header before/after rewrite.
Ava Chen: thanks — if this is a proxy issue we should rollback the canary for rotation until fix. @team-ops FYI.
Maya Ortiz: review ping -> https://github.com/redwood-inference/routing/pull/482 (small change to route-match precedence). If anyone can give quick ✅ it would let us promote.
Theo Li: confirmed header rewrite at the proxy egress: a middleware step strips `kid` when chunked transfer flag is set. I'll open a PR to preserve auth headers.
Samir Patel: if Theo's PR is ready we can canary it against the debug build.
Ava Chen: plan: 1) hold rotation rollout, 2) Theo opens fix PR, 3) Samir validates in staging with kid-echo canary, 4) promote routing PR once stable.
Maya Ortiz: noted blockers for my side: need the routing PR to land to finish the Dedicated rollout checklist. Will pause dependent tasks.
Theo Li: PR opened: https://github.com/redwood-inference/edge-proxy/pull/213 — small header-preserve patch.
Samir Patel: validating now, initial canary shows kid preserved. will update in 10m. :thumbsup:
Ava Chen: perfect — thanks everyone. will follow up in incidents channel if this escalates. For now tagging reviewers for PR482: @Maya Ortiz @Samir Patel @Theo Li
Maya Ortiz: doing review now.
Samir Patel: can confirm staging auth passes with fixed proxy. rolling forward to promote.
Ava Chen: thanks — closing the async drop-in. will post final notes in the release thread later. :white_check_mark:

title: #eng-platform: 1755004327
preview:
Alex Kim: Quick follow-up to the RFC — proposal to move 'circuit-breaker' decision from router to observer service and make router do only routing+fallback execution. Thoughts?

Priya Singh: curious — what's the motivation? operationally it's easier to centralize decisions, but we lose local fast-fail ability.

Alex Kim: Main reasons: 1) observer has aggregated cross-model signals (error-rate spikes, latency p50/p95, token-queue-growth) to make more informed degrade decisions; 2) easier to share safety policies across hosted/dedicated/private. Router still needs a fast local check (cacheable) for immediate O(1) rejects.

Ben Carter: Do we have an API surface sketched? e.g., router calls observer.isHealthy(model, tenant, cohort) sync -> bool? That could add latency.

Jia Li: maybe make it async: router consults cached verdicts (ttl 5s) + probe hook to observer for stale entries. If cache miss, fallback to local heuristics and mark for async reconciliation.

Alex Kim: exactly. Example config snippet:
```
fallback_policy:
  decision_owner: observer
  cache_ttl_ms: 5000
  local_heuristic_thresholds:
    latency_ms: 1200
    error_rate_pct: 5
```

Priya Singh: love the cache TTL idea. we should add a 'grace_count' for in-flight requests so we don't cut mid-flight.

Ben Carter: how do we signal rollbacks for canary cohorts? want automatic fallback when cohort error delta > X and sustained > Y secs.

Jia Li: propose signals: delta_error_pct, delta_latency_ms, unusual_token_stop_rate. algorithm: streaming EWMA + burst detector. trigger if EWMA(delta_error) > 3% && sustained 60s.

deploy-bot: deploy started: canary-control-plane v2 -> staging (sha: abc123) :rocket:

Alex Kim: ops note: router should allow a 'soft-fallback' (route to old variant but continue collecting shadow telemetry) vs 'hard-fallback' (stop routing to variant). that way we can revert without losing signal.

Priya Singh: also need a cooldown/backoff: if observer flips fallback state repeatedly, escalate to manual and open incident.

Ben Carter: can we keep a per-tenant risk-budget? small tenants shouldn't trigger system-wide fallbacks.

Jia Li: yes, decision_owner should take tenant quota into account. Include weight-attenuation. config example continues: 
```
cohort_rules:
  - name: small-tenant
    weight_threshold: 0.01
    escalate_to_manual: true
```

Alex Kim: next steps: I'll draft an RFC appendix with API schemas, cache semantics, and a proposed EWMA formula. Please comment on the heuristics above by EOD.

Priya Singh: ack

Ben Carter: ack

Jia Li: will add sample probe graphs

Alex Kim: thanks all :thumbsup:
text:
Alex Kim: Quick follow-up to the RFC — proposal to move 'circuit-breaker' decision from router to observer service and make router do only routing+fallback execution. Thoughts?

Priya Singh: curious — what's the motivation? operationally it's easier to centralize decisions, but we lose local fast-fail ability.

Alex Kim: Main reasons: 1) observer has aggregated cross-model signals (error-rate spikes, latency p50/p95, token-queue-growth) to make more informed degrade decisions; 2) easier to share safety policies across hosted/dedicated/private. Router still needs a fast local check (cacheable) for immediate O(1) rejects.

Ben Carter: Do we have an API surface sketched? e.g., router calls observer.isHealthy(model, tenant, cohort) sync -> bool? That could add latency.

Jia Li: maybe make it async: router consults cached verdicts (ttl 5s) + probe hook to observer for stale entries. If cache miss, fallback to local heuristics and mark for async reconciliation.

Alex Kim: exactly. Example config snippet:
```
fallback_policy:
  decision_owner: observer
  cache_ttl_ms: 5000
  local_heuristic_thresholds:
    latency_ms: 1200
    error_rate_pct: 5
```

Priya Singh: love the cache TTL idea. we should add a 'grace_count' for in-flight requests so we don't cut mid-flight.

Ben Carter: how do we signal rollbacks for canary cohorts? want automatic fallback when cohort error delta > X and sustained > Y secs.

Jia Li: propose signals: delta_error_pct, delta_latency_ms, unusual_token_stop_rate. algorithm: streaming EWMA + burst detector. trigger if EWMA(delta_error) > 3% && sustained 60s.

deploy-bot: deploy started: canary-control-plane v2 -> staging (sha: abc123) :rocket:

Alex Kim: ops note: router should allow a 'soft-fallback' (route to old variant but continue collecting shadow telemetry) vs 'hard-fallback' (stop routing to variant). that way we can revert without losing signal.

Priya Singh: also need a cooldown/backoff: if observer flips fallback state repeatedly, escalate to manual and open incident.

Ben Carter: can we keep a per-tenant risk-budget? small tenants shouldn't trigger system-wide fallbacks.

Jia Li: yes, decision_owner should take tenant quota into account. Include weight-attenuation. config example continues: 
```
cohort_rules:
  - name: small-tenant
    weight_threshold: 0.01
    escalate_to_manual: true
```

Alex Kim: next steps: I'll draft an RFC appendix with API schemas, cache semantics, and a proposed EWMA formula. Please comment on the heuristics above by EOD.

Priya Singh: ack

Ben Carter: ack

Jia Li: will add sample probe graphs

Alex Kim: thanks all :thumbsup:

title: #eng-platform: 1749991111
preview:
Priya Desai: FYI customer hit a billing surprise when a streaming generation was truncated by server-side max_tokens enforcement mid-toolcall and the client retried. Result: provisional reserve from original stream + confirmed smaller final = weird net. anyone seen truncation + retry double-charge?

Ethan Park: can you sketch the flow quickly? truncation during toolcall sounds nasty.

Priya Desai: high level: client starts stream -> server marks provisional 120 tokens when toolcall begins -> server enforces max_tokens and truncates output at 80 tokens, responds with truncated toolcall_confirm(80) -> client perceives truncated response and retries the toolcall -> replay causes another provisional 80 then confirm 80. end state: provisional 120 + confirmed 160 in ledger -> customer sees overage.

Riley Chen: ack. that's different from a reconnect; truncation caused provisional and confirm mismatch. do we have ledger snippet?

Priya Desai: snippet and ids:
```
2026-04-02T09:15:10Z TRACE sess=0xA9 tc=start toolcall_id=tc-774 provisional_tokens=120
2026-04-02T09:15:12Z WARN sess=0xA9 toolcall_truncate toolcall_id=tc-774 truncated_tokens=80 reason=max_tokens
2026-04-02T09:15:12Z TRACE sess=0xA9 tc=confirm toolcall_id=tc-774 confirmed_tokens=80
2026-04-02T09:15:20Z TRACE sess=0xA9 tc=start toolcall_id=tc-775 provisional_tokens=80 (retry)
2026-04-02T09:15:22Z TRACE sess=0xA9 tc=confirm toolcall_id=tc-775 confirmed_tokens=80
# end -> provisional 120, confirmed 160
```

ops-bot: Alert BILL-PR-334: possible truncation-retry double provisional detected for customer acct=ac-09123.

Ethan Park: root cause: provisional stake is taken at toolcall begin without a later correction path when truncation reduces the final confirmation. Confirm path increases confirmed tokens but we never reduce the provisional claim to match actual confirm for partial truncation.

Riley Chen: two candidate fixes: (A) always reserve provisional == max_possible but mark provisional as adjustable: on confirm if confirmed < provisional then decrement provisional by (provisional - confirmed). requires atomic ledger ops and be careful with races. (B) change protocol: don't provisional-reserve full predicted tokens on start; instead do small incremental provisional reservations as tokens stream out. lowers headroom but avoids big provisional mismatch.

Priya Desai: both have tradeoffs. A is easiest for short-term (we can patch ledger aggregator to auto-shrink provisional on confirm if reason=truncate). B is more robust for long-term but touches streaming runtime and QoS.

Ethan Park: console/ops angle: ops couldn't surface truncation reasons per-session, so they assumed customer retried intentionally. we should add a "truncation_reason" tag in trace + show provisional vs confirmed waterfall in the request timeline view so SRE can see shrink events.

Riley Chen: telemetry bits I'll add: billing.provisional_adjustments_total (counter), billing.provisional_shrink_amount (counter), stream.truncate_events{reason}. Also add request.timeline.{provisional,confirm,truncate} spans to traces.

Priya Desai: runtime patch plan: short-term patch that on detect of toolcall_truncate we emit an adjustment event that decrements provisional by (provisional - confirmed) and mark adjustment as tentative until confirmed by reconciler. Deploy behind flag billing.adjust_on_truncate=true.

Ethan Park: please add unit tests for races: confirm and retry arriving in quick succession and ensure we don't undercharge. Also check interactions with rate/quotas: if provisional was decremented then retry reserves more, quotas might allow extra tokens. Need to coordinate with quota service.

Riley Chen: good point — adding quota hook: when provisional is shrunk, adjust quota back. That will require a small cross-service event with idempotency token.

ops-bot: Noted. Scheduling: canary patch billing.adjust_on_truncate=true for 2% traffic -> 2026-04-03T01:00Z.

billing-bot: Flagging affected invoices for manual review and creating refund queue entry if net charge delta > $5 for last 24h. Ticket BILL-PR-334 created.

Priya Desai: I'll open GH issue with repro harness (client that forces truncate then retries) and attach ledger + timeline screenshots. Will PR the conservative adjustment patch first.

Ethan Park: UX follow-up: add a one-paragraph note to rollout docs explaining truncation-adjust behavior and how ops should interpret spikes in provisional when truncation is enabled during model / batch config changes.

Riley Chen: will update dashboards: per-route "provisional vs confirmed waterfall" and a filter for truncate/retry events.

Priya Desai: thanks team. assigning ENG-6022. will post PR & canary metrics here. closing thread for now.

title: #eng-platform: 1749472201
preview:
Nina Petrova: looping in eng-platform folks — we need to nail down the integration points between the new VPC terraform modules and platform/installer config. Today we’re doing a lot of “paste subnet ids here” in pilots and it’s brittle.

Victor Alvarez: +1. main question from my side: what outputs do we want to be part of the “contract” for alpha? if we change names/shape every PR it’ll be chaos.

Irene Choi: yep. for alpha I’d like a minimal stable set that redwood-private-installer can consume (either flags or a config file). optional extras can be “best effort” outputs.

Bruno Silva: current modules output the basics (vpc_id, private_subnet_ids, public_subnet_ids, nat_gw_ids) but we haven’t standardized endpoint outputs. also log sinks.

Sean Gallagher: from SRE standpoint: the only things we *must* know to validate networking before we start are: subnets for nodes, cluster SGs, and whether required VPC endpoints exist + private DNS enabled where expected. (we’re adding preflight checks in installer; it needs something deterministic).

Allison Grant: observability angle: we need to know where to send/expect logs. at minimum: cloudwatch log group names/arns for flow logs (if enabled) and any S3 bucket used as sink (flow logs / access logs). plus tags so we can discover.

Rafael Mendes: +1 on deterministic. installer preflight should not scrape AWS too much without inputs; but we can also do discovery if we have VPC ID and region and explicit “I expect endpoints ON”.

Nina Petrova: what’s the preferred format? terraform output -json can be consumed by installer? or we want the terraform to render a yaml file?

Irene Choi: preference: don’t generate files in terraform for alpha. keep it as outputs, then docs say: `terraform output -json > networking.json` and pass to installer.

Victor Alvarez: okay so define a single composite output? e.g. `redwood_private_networking` containing everything.

Bruno Silva: we can, but then we have to keep object shape stable. also some people want individual outputs for reuse.

Sean Gallagher: composite is fine as long as it’s additive-only in alpha. but note: in terraform output -json you’ll get nested structures. installer can parse that.

Allison Grant: I’m fine w nested, but we should keep the “leaf values” as IDs/ARNs/strings. no giant lists of objects unless needed.

Simran Kaur: from RA review: docs should clearly map outputs -> installer flags/values. a table would help. also call out which are required vs optional.

Victor Alvarez: concrete proposal for v0.1 contract:
- required:
  - aws_region
  - vpc_id
  - private_subnet_ids (list)
  - cluster_node_security_group_id (or sg_ids list if pattern module returns multiple)
  - vpc_endpoints_required (map service->endpoint_id?)
- optional:
  - public_subnet_ids
  - nat_gateway_ids
  - flow_logs: enabled + destination (cw log group arn OR s3 bucket arn)
  - interface_endpoint_security_group_id
  - resolver / private hosted zone info (if we ever create)

Bruno Silva: we don’t currently create cluster SG in the VPC module; that’s in SG patterns module. but we can wire outputs through example.

Nina Petrova: for EKS private control plane constraints: installer/cluster will need the endpoint access pattern too. do we need to output whether EKS endpoint private/public is allowed? (or is that out-of-scope since modules aren’t creating EKS)

Irene Choi: out of scope for modules, but we should include “assumptions” in outputs/docs. i.e. “private subnets have route to NAT or endpoints for ECR/S3/Logs”.

Rafael Mendes: installer needs to know ecr endpoints specifically. we already have PR to detect + warn when missing.

Sean Gallagher: yeah PR-19110 is exactly this. it currently checks:
- `com.amazonaws.<region>.ecr.api`
- `com.amazonaws.<region>.ecr.dkr`
- `com.amazonaws.<region>.s3` (gateway)
plus `logs` and `sts` depending on mode.
We should align the terraform endpoints module defaults to match what the installer expects, then output “expected endpoints list” so preflight error messages can be explicit.

Victor Alvarez: re: “expected endpoints list” — do we output actual endpoint IDs or just booleans?

Bruno Silva: actual IDs are useful for debug. but installer can also query by service name + VPC ID.

Irene Choi: I’d like both: output the service names we *intend* to have and whether private DNS is enabled for each interface endpoint. then installer can cross-check reality.

Allison Grant: +1. private DNS misconfig is a common pilot issue (ECR). if we output the intended `private_dns_enabled=true` for ecr endpoints, that maps nicely to a preflight message.

Nina Petrova: can we keep it simple like:

```
output "redwood_network" {
  value = {
    region = var.region
    vpc_id = aws_vpc.this.id
    private_subnet_ids = module.subnets.private_ids
    public_subnet_ids  = module.subnets.public_ids
    endpoints = {
      ecr_api = { service = "ecr.api", endpoint_id = module.endpoints.ecr_api_id, private_dns = true }
      ecr_dkr = { service = "ecr.dkr", endpoint_id = module.endpoints.ecr_dkr_id, private_dns = true }
      logs    = { service = "logs",    endpoint_id = module.endpoints.logs_id,    private_dns = true }
      sts     = { service = "sts",     endpoint_id = module.endpoints.sts_id,     private_dns = true }
      s3      = { service = "s3",      endpoint_id = module.endpoints.s3_gateway_id, private_dns = "n/a" }
    }
    flow_logs = {
      enabled = var.flow_logs_enabled
      destination = var.flow_logs_destination
      cw_log_group_arn = try(aws_cloudwatch_log_group.flow.arn, null)
      s3_bucket_arn    = try(aws_s3_bucket.flow.arn, null)
    }
  }
}
```

Victor Alvarez: yes-ish. minor: service names should be full AWS service strings or standardized keys? i’d prefer full: `com.amazonaws.${region}.ecr.api` to avoid ambiguity.

Bruno Silva: agreed. and keep `endpoint_type` too (Interface/Gateway) so installer messaging can be better.

Rafael Mendes: re: `private_dns = "n/a"` — better to omit or set null. :)

Allison Grant: for log sink naming: can we output a “recommended log group name” even when not created? (customers sometimes bring their own). maybe:
- `flow_logs.destination_type` = `cloudwatch|s3|none`
- `flow_logs.log_group_name` OR `flow_logs.s3_bucket_name`

Irene Choi: careful: we shouldn’t output customer-provided values in a way that looks like Redwood-managed. but if it’s an input and we just echo it, that’s fine.

Sean Gallagher: also add `tags` output? not required but helps SRE inventory.

Victor Alvarez: tags in terraform output can be noisy. i’d skip for alpha contract.

Simran Kaur: +1 skip tags, but doc should say we tag everything with `redwood:component` etc.

Nina Petrova: question: should installer accept `--networking-json` path? or will it be `--terraform-outputs-json`? naming matters.

Rafael Mendes: i vote `--networking-outputs` or `--network-config`. `terraform-outputs-json` sounds too terraform-y.

Sean Gallagher: installer side can accept a JSON file with just the subset we need. but docs can recommend generating it with terraform output.

Bruno Silva: we can add an example script in the repo:

```
terraform output -json redwood_network > redwood-network.json
redwood-private-installer install --network-config ./redwood-network.json
```

Irene Choi: this is good. action items:
1) Victor/Bruno: standardize a `redwood_network` (or `redwood_private_network`) output in the examples (not necessarily in every module) with: region, vpc_id, private_subnet_ids, endpoints (service_name + endpoint_id + private_dns_enabled + endpoint_type), flow_logs destination info.
2) Sean/Rafael: confirm which fields installer will read in alpha and what’s optional; we can update preflight messaging to reference service names.
3) Allison: provide the minimal log sink fields SRE wants surfaced (cw log group arn/name and/or s3 bucket).
4) Simran: update RA/deployment guide mapping table outputs -> installer inputs.

Victor Alvarez: works. i’ll post a proposed schema in the PR description when we wire it.

Sean Gallagher: please also make sure endpoint keys match installer checks. we’ve been bitten by “logs” vs “cloudwatch-logs” naming.

Bruno Silva: noted. i’ll align to full service names:
- `com.amazonaws.${region}.ecr.api`
- `com.amazonaws.${region}.ecr.dkr`
- `com.amazonaws.${region}.logs`
- `com.amazonaws.${region}.sts`
- `com.amazonaws.${region}.s3` (gateway)

Allison Grant: for flow logs, if destination is CloudWatch we should output retention days too (defaults matter for security/compliance). if it’s customer-owned log group, we can output “unknown”.

Irene Choi: ok but keep it optional. don’t block alpha on it.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: once we agree schema, we should freeze names for v0.x and only add fields. breaking changes require bump and release note callout.

Nina Petrova: +1. also if we can, add a “schema_version”: "0.1" in the output so installer can warn if incompatible.

Victor Alvarez: good call. we can set `schema_version = "0.1"` at top-level.

Sean Gallagher: :+1: that will save us in pilots.

Irene Choi: cool. let’s do it. please link the PR here when ready.

Sean Gallagher: also add `tags` output? not required but helps SRE inventory.

Victor Alvarez: tags in terraform output can be noisy. i’d skip for alpha contract.

Simran Kaur: +1 skip tags, but doc should say we tag everything with `redwood:component` etc.

Nina Petrova: question: should installer accept `--networking-json` path? or will it be `--terraform-outputs-json`? naming matters.

Rafael Mendes: i vote `--networking-outputs` or `--network-config`. `terraform-outputs-json` sounds too terraform-y.

Sean Gallagher: installer side can accept a JSON file with just the subset we need. but docs can recommend generating it with terraform output.

Bruno Silva: we can add an example script in the repo:

```
terraform output -json redwood_network > redwood-network.json
redwood-private-installer install --network-config ./redwood-network.json
```

Irene Choi: this is good. action items:
1) Victor/Bruno: standardize a `redwood_network` (or `redwood_private_network`) output in the examples (not necessarily in every module) with: region, vpc_id, private_subnet_ids, endpoints (service_name + endpoint_id + private_dns_enabled + endpoint_type), flow_logs destination info.
2) Sean/Rafael: confirm which fields installer will read in alpha and what’s optional; we can update preflight messaging to reference service names.
3) Allison: provide the minimal log sink fields SRE wants surfaced (cw log group arn/name and/or s3 bucket).
4) Simran: update RA/deployment guide mapping table outputs -> installer inputs.

Victor Alvarez: works. i’ll post a proposed schema in the PR description when we wire it.

Sean Gallagher: please also make sure endpoint keys match installer checks. we’ve been bitten by “logs” vs “cloudwatch-logs” naming.

Bruno Silva: noted. i’ll align to full service names:
- `com.amazonaws.${region}.ecr.api`
- `com.amazonaws.${region}.ecr.dkr`
- `com.amazonaws.${region}.logs`
- `com.amazonaws.${region}.sts`
- `com.amazonaws.${region}.s3` (gateway)

Allison Grant: for flow logs, if destination is CloudWatch we should output retention days too (defaults matter for security/compliance). if it’s customer-owned log group, we can output “unknown”.

Irene Choi: ok but keep it optional. don’t block alpha on it.

Rafael Mendes: :thumbsup: once we agree schema, we should freeze names for v0.x and only add fields. breaking changes require bump and release note callout.

Nina Petrova: +1. also if we can, add a “schema_version”: "0.1" in the output so installer can warn if incompatible.

Victor Alvarez: good call. we can set `schema_version = "0.1"` at top-level.

Sean Gallagher: :+1: that will save us in pilots.

Irene Choi: cool. let’s do it. please link the PR here when ready.

title: #eng-platform: 1749256629
preview:
Logan Wright: Heads up: marketing site needs to pull benchmark results JSON for the interactive table. We have the internal results endpoint in `redwood` behind auth, but we need to settle: caching strategy + auth model + rate limiting so we don’t get scraped to death on launch week.

Felix Schneider: +1. Current plan in DES/PRD assumes a static-ish JSON artifact per report version (ex: `v1.0.0`) + optional “latest” pointer. If we call the internal endpoint from the client, we’ll leak too much + hammer origin.

Henry Cho: product POV: we should treat results as public once published, but we can’t expose internal IDs / infra details (region shard names, fleet tags, etc). Also want “last known good” behavior if we republish.

Mei Lin: data pipeline exports a versioned `results.json` already (from benchmark-lab). It has schema version + report_version + per-model/region stats. We can publish that as the source of truth and stop hitting the compute path.

Wei Chen: docs-site PR is set up to host a landing page under `redwood.com/benchmarks/latency-per-dollar`. Ideally the page loads fast even on cold cache. If we can put JSON behind CDN with strong cache headers, that’s easiest.

Naomi Feldman: security note: we should NOT directly expose the internal results service; it has extra fields (even if “harmless”) and it lives on infra that’s not meant for public traffic. Also any auth token embedded client-side is a no-go.

Sean Gallagher: from SRE side: assume we get hit by bots + social spike. If it’s a public URL, we need:
- CDN caching (edge)
- origin shield
- rate limiting at edge (per-IP)
- timeouts + circuit breaker to fall back to stale cache
Also: export endpoints (CSV/JSON download) can amplify traffic.

Felix Schneider: Proposal:
1) Publish benchmark artifacts to a public bucket (or docs build output) at a stable path:
   - `/benchmarks/artifacts/{report_version}/results.min.json`
   - `/benchmarks/artifacts/{report_version}/results.csv`
   - `/benchmarks/artifacts/latest.json` (tiny file pointing to latest version)
2) Marketing site fetches `latest.json` server-side (Next.js) and then fetches versioned results.
3) Set `Cache-Control: public, max-age=300, s-maxage=3600, stale-while-revalidate=86400` on artifacts.
4) Add `ETag` so revalidations are cheap.

Logan Wright: +1. Question: do we still need the internal results service at all for this? Or can we fully decouple and serve from static artifacts?

Mei Lin: We can decouple. Internal results service can exist for internal previews / staging, but public should read from exported artifacts only. That also helps legal substantiation: artifact hash = what we claimed.

Alicia Perez: UX: interactive table does filter/sort client-side. JSON is ~a few MB depending on how many metrics. If it’s too big, we need a “summary” JSON for initial render + lazy load full dataset.

Felix Schneider: Yep. We can split:
- `summary.json` (top-level metrics for default view)
- `full.json` (includes breakdowns like p50/p95/throughput)
Marketing page can render with summary first.

Omar El-Sayed: instrumentation note: if we’re worried about scraping, we might still want the “download full JSON” as an explicit click with separate URL and stricter limits.

Sean Gallagher: rate limiting: suggest at CDN:
- `/benchmarks/artifacts/*` allow higher (it’s cacheable) but still cap per-IP, e.g. 120 req/min.
- `/benchmarks/artifacts/*/full.json` lower, e.g. 30 req/min.
- download endpoints for CSV maybe 10 req/min.
Also block obvious bad user agents if we see abuse.

Naomi Feldman: auth model: if it’s truly public artifacts, no auth required. But we should ensure:
- artifacts contain only approved fields (redaction pass)
- no internal region codes; use public region names
- no request IDs, customer IDs, fleet hostnames
Can we add an automated “public artifact linter” to CI before publish?

Mei Lin: yes. We can add a validation step in the export pipeline: schema allowlist + regex scan for forbidden tokens (hostnames, UUID patterns) + fail build.

Wei Chen: For caching headers, docs site is behind CDN already. Need to confirm we can set `stale-while-revalidate` and `s-maxage` with our current config.

Felix Schneider: I’ll sync with web infra, but we can also just drop artifacts into the same static build output so it inherits CDN behavior.

Logan Wright: One more concern: “latest.json” is a single hot key. If everyone requests it on page load, it might be the thundering herd.

Sean Gallagher: we can cache `latest.json` aggressively too. It changes rarely. Set `s-maxage=3600` and allow stale for a day. Worst case, people see previous version for an hour.

Henry Cho: That’s acceptable. If we need an urgent pull/update, we can purge CDN.

Alicia Perez: Can we also include a `generated_at` and `report_version` in the payload so we can display “Data as of …” and reduce confusion.

Mei Lin: already in export. Will make sure it’s in summary.json as well.

Naomi Feldman: + add a disclaimer string in the payload that the UI can display if needed (but keep it generic). Anything legal-y should still be in page copy.

Felix Schneider: Implementation detail: if Next.js server fetches artifacts, do we need an internal token? If it’s public, no. But server fetch avoids CORS issues + lets us do fallback logic.

Logan Wright: let’s do server fetch + cache in the app layer too (in-memory / edge) to minimize origin calls.

Sean Gallagher: + set a hard timeout for origin fetch (e.g. 1s) and if it fails, serve stale cached copy. Launch week we’d rather be slightly stale than down.

DeployBot: DeployBot APP: Reminder: any change to CDN cache/rate-limit rules requires a config PR and 1 reviewer from SRE.

Felix Schneider: Action items:
- [Felix] draft artifact paths + split summary/full; open PR in docs-site to include artifacts in static output.
- [Mei] add public artifact validation/lint + field allowlist.
- [Logan/Sean] propose CDN cache headers + rate limit rules for the artifact paths.
- [Naomi] review allowlist + forbidden patterns.

Logan Wright: Works. I’ll create a short decision note: “Public artifacts via CDN; internal results service only for staging; no client auth tokens; rate limiting at edge; stale fallback.”
